Topic 10: HTML, CSS, & Web Scraping#

  • 03/11/21

  • onl01-dtsc-ft-022221

Learning Objectives / Outline#

  • Part 1: HTML & CSS: Beyond Web Scraping

    • Brief Overview of HTML & CSS

    • Learn when you will use HTML & CSS in your data science journey

    • Demonstrate the power of CSS with a Plotly/Dash dashboard.

    • Demonstrate the value of learning HTML/CSS with VS Code.

  • Part 2: Walk through the basics of web scraping:

    • Learn to use Chrome’s Inspect tool to hunt down target website data

    • Learn how to use Beautiful Soup to scrape the contents of a web page.

Questions#

📓 Part 1: HTML & CSS#

  • HMTL is responsible for the content of a website.

  • CSS is responsible for the appearance / layout of a website.

HTML Overview & Tags#

  • All HTML pages have the following components

    1. document declaration followed by html tag

    <!DOCTYPE html>
    <html> 2. Head html tag
    <head> <title></title></head> 3. Body
    <body> … content… </body>
    </html>

  • Html content is divdied into tags that specify the type of content.

    • Basic Tags Reference Table

    • Full Alphabetical Tag Reference Table

    • tags have attributes

      • Tag Attributes

      • Attributes are always defined in the start/opening tag.

    • tags may have several content-creator-defined attributes such as class or id

  • We will use the tag and its identifying attributes to isolate content we want on a web page with BeautifulSoup.

CSS Overview#

List the Components of CSS#

Excerpt From Section 13: Intro to CSS

For each presentation rule, there are 3 things to keep in mind:

  1. What is the specific HTML we want to style?

  2. What are the qualities we want to modify (e.g. the properties of text in a paragraph)?

  3. How do we want to modify the qualities of the element (e.g. font family, font color, font size, line height, letter spacing, etc.)?

CSS selectors are a way of declaring which HTML elements you wish to style. Selectors can appear a few different ways:

  • The type of HTML element(h1, p, div, etc.)

  • The value of an element’s id or class (<p id='idvalue'></p>, <p class='classname'></p>)

  • The value of an element’s attributes (value="hello")

  • The element’s relationship with surrounding elements (a p within an element with class of .infobox)

Type selectors documentation

The element type class is a commonly used selector. Class selectors are used to select all elements that share a given class name. The class selector syntax is: .classname. Prefix the class name with a ‘.’(period).

/*
select all elements that have the 'important-topic' classname (e.g. <h1 class='important-topic'>
and <h1 class='important-topic'>)
*/
.important-topic

You can also use the id selector to style elements. However, there should be only one element with a given id in an HTML document. This can make styling with the ID selector ideal for one-off styles. The id selector syntax is: #idvalue. Prefix the id attribute of an element with a # (which is called “octothorpe,” “pound sign”, or “hashtag”).

/*
selects the HTML element with the id 'main-header' (e.g. <h1 id='main-header'>)
*/
#main-header

id selectors documentation

When will/can I use HTML & CSS?#

1. Web scraping.#

  • See Part 2 of notebook.

def highlight_max(s):
    '''
    highlight the maximum in a Series yellow.
    '''
    is_max = s == s.max()
    css = 'background-color: yellow;font-weight:bold;color:green;font-size:1.3em;'
    return [css if v else '' for v in is_max]


df.style.apply(highlight_max,axis=0)

HTML/CSS DEMOS#

Demo 1: Loading CSS styles via Python#

  • We can load external CSS files by using IPython.display.HTML and using the code

from IPython.display import HTML
HTML("<style>{}</style>".format(css_info))

from IPython.display import HTML
css_stylesheet = "../../assets/webscrape_example.css"

with open(css_stylesheet,'r')  as f_css:
    style = f_css.read()

## Run Me First
HTML(f"<style>{style}</style>")

## UNCOMMENT TO RESET STYLE
HTML("")

📓 Part 2: Web Scraping 101#

Scraping Task#

  • Our task is to get the tables from the Wikipedia page: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films

Using python’s requests module:#

  • Use requests library to initiate connections to a website.

  • Check the status code returned to determine if connection was successful (status code=200) ~~url = ‘https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stock_market~~~

import requests
url = 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films'

# Connect to the url using requests.get
response = requests.get(url)
response.status_code

Status Code

Code Meaning

1xx

Informational

2xx

Success

3xx

Redirection

4xx

Client Error

5xx

Server Error

  • Adding a sleep time is helpful for avoiding and getting blocked from a server `time.sleep(

  • Note: You can add a timeout to requests.get() to avoid indefinite waiting

    • Best in multiples of 3 (timeout=3 or 6 , 9 ,etc.)

# Add a timeout to prevent hanging
response = requests.get(url, timeout=3)
response.status_code


import requests
from time import sleep

url = 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films'
response = requests.get(url=url, timeout=3)
print(f'Status code: {response.status_code}')

Status code: 200

print(response.text[:1000])

Using BeautifulSoup#

Cook a soup#

  • Connect to a website usingresponse = requests.get(url)

  • Feed response.content into BeautifulSoup

  • Must specify the parser that will analyze the contents

    • default available is 'html.parser'

    • recommended is to install and use lxml [lxml documentation]

  • use soup.prettify() to get a user-friendly version of the content to print

# Define Url and establish connection
url = 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stock_market'
response = requests.get(url, timeout=3)

# Feed the response's .content into BeauitfulSoup
page_content = response.content
soup = BeautifulSoup(page_content,'lxml') #'html.parser')

# Preview soup contents using .prettify()
print(soup.prettify()[:2000])


import bs4
## Make a BeautifulSoup
url = 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films'
response = requests.get(url)
soup = bs4.BeautifulSoup(response.content) 

## Print the prettified preview
print(soup.prettify())

       Wikimedia list article

      <div class="thumb tright">
       <div class="thumbinner" style="width:222px;">
        <a class="image" href="/wiki/File:Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg">
         <img alt="A screencap of the title card from the trailer of Gone with the Wind." class="thumbimage" data-file-height="2000" data-file-width="1299" decoding="async" height="339" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg/220px-Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg/330px-Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg/440px-Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg 2x" width="220"/>
        </a>
        <div class="thumbcaption">
         <div class="magnify">
          <a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg" title="Enlarge">
          </a>
         </div>
         <i>
          Gone with the Wind
         </i>
         held the record of highest-grossing film for twenty-five years and, adjusted for inflation, has earned more than any other film.
        </div>
       </div>
      </div>
      <p>
       <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Films" title="Films">
        Films
       </a>
       generate income from several revenue streams, including
       <a href="/wiki/Movie_theater" title="Movie theater">
        theatrical exhibition
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Home_video" title="Home video">
        home video
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Broadcasting_rights" title="Broadcasting rights">
        television broadcast rights
       </a>
       , and
       <a href="/wiki/Merchandising" title="Merchandising">
        merchandising
       </a>
       . However, theatrical
       <a href="/wiki/Box_office" title="Box office">
        box office
       </a>
       earnings are the primary metric for trade publications in assessing the success of a film, mostly because of the availability of the data compared to sales figures for home video and broadcast rights, but also because of historical practice. Included on the list are charts of the top box-office earners (ranked by both the
       <a href="/wiki/Real_versus_nominal_value_(economics)" title="Real versus nominal value (economics)">
        nominal and real value
       </a>
       of their revenue), a chart of high-grossing films by calendar year, a timeline showing the transition of the highest-grossing film record, and a chart of the highest-grossing film franchises and series. All charts are ranked by international theatrical box-office performance where possible, excluding income derived from home video, broadcasting rights, and merchandise.
      </p>
      <p>
       Traditionally,
       <a href="/wiki/War_film" title="War film">
        war films
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Musical_film" title="Musical film">
        musicals
       </a>
       , and
       <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/List_of_historical_drama_films" title="List of historical drama films">
        historical dramas
       </a>
       have been the most popular genres, but
       <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Media_franchise" title="Media franchise">
        franchise films
       </a>
       have been among the best performers of the 21st century. There is strong interest in the
       <a href="/wiki/Superhero_film" title="Superhero film">
        superhero genre
       </a>
       , with nine films in the
       <a href="/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe" title="Marvel Cinematic Universe">
        Marvel Cinematic Universe
       </a>
       featuring among the nominal top-earners. The most successful superhero film,
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Avengers:_Endgame" title="Avengers: Endgame">
         Avengers: Endgame
        </a>
       </i>
       , is also the highest-grossing film overall on the nominal earnings chart, and there are four films in total based on the
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Avengers_(comics)" title="Avengers (comics)">
         Avengers
        </a>
       </i>
       comic books charting in the top twenty. Other
       <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/List_of_films_based_on_Marvel_Comics" title="List of films based on Marvel Comics">
        Marvel Comics adaptations
       </a>
       have also had success with the
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Spider-Man_in_film" title="Spider-Man in film">
         Spider-Man
        </a>
       </i>
       and
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/X-Men_(film_series)" title="X-Men (film series)">
         X-Men
        </a>
       </i>
       properties, while films based on
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Batman_in_film" title="Batman in film">
         Batman
        </a>
       </i>
       and
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Superman_in_film" title="Superman in film">
         Superman
        </a>
       </i>
       from
       <a href="/wiki/DC_Comics" title="DC Comics">
        DC Comics
       </a>
       have generally performed well.
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Star_Wars" title="Star Wars">
         Star Wars
        </a>
       </i>
       is also represented in the nominal earnings chart with five films, while the
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Harry_Potter_(film_series)" title="Harry Potter (film series)">
         Harry Potter
        </a>
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Jurassic_Park" title="Jurassic Park">
         Jurassic Park
        </a>
       </i>
       and
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean_(film_series)" title="Pirates of the Caribbean (film series)">
         Pirates of the Caribbean
        </a>
       </i>
       franchises feature prominently.
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film)" title="Avatar (2009 film)">
         Avatar
        </a>
       </i>
       , in second place on the nominal chart, is the highest-grossing film that is not a sequel or an adaptation of a pre-existing property. Animated family films have performed consistently well, with
       <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/List_of_Disney_theatrical_animated_features" title="List of Disney theatrical animated features">
        Disney films
       </a>
       enjoying lucrative re-releases prior to the home-video era. Disney also enjoyed later success with films such as
       <i>
        <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Frozen_I" title="Frozen I">
         Frozen I
        </a>
       </i>
       and
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Frozen_II" title="Frozen II">
         II
        </a>
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Zootopia" title="Zootopia">
         Zootopia
        </a>
       </i>
       , and
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/The_Lion_King" title="The Lion King">
         The Lion King
        </a>
       </i>
       (with
       <a href="/wiki/The_Lion_King_(2019_film)" title="The Lion King (2019 film)">
        its computer-animated remake
       </a>
       as the
       <a href="/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_animated_films" title="List of highest-grossing animated films">
        highest-grossing animated film
       </a>
       ), as well as its
       <a href="/wiki/List_of_Pixar_films" title="List of Pixar films">
        Pixar
       </a>
       brand, of which
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Incredibles_2" title="Incredibles 2">
         Incredibles 2
        </a>
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Toy_Story_3" title="Toy Story 3">
         Toy Story 3
        </a>
       </i>
       and
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Toy_Story_4" title="Toy Story 4">
         4
        </a>
       </i>
       , and
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Finding_Dory" title="Finding Dory">
         Finding Dory
        </a>
       </i>
       have been the best performers. Beyond Disney and Pixar animation, the
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Despicable_Me" title="Despicable Me">
         Despicable Me
        </a>
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Shrek_(franchise)" title="Shrek (franchise)">
         Shrek
        </a>
       </i>
       , and
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Ice_Age_(franchise)" title="Ice Age (franchise)">
         Ice Age
        </a>
       </i>
       series have met with the most success.
      </p>
      <p>
       While
       <a href="/wiki/Inflation" title="Inflation">
        inflation
       </a>
       has eroded away the achievements of most films from the 1960s and 1970s, there are franchises originating from that period that are still active. Besides the
       <i>
        Star Wars
       </i>
       and
       <i>
        Superman
       </i>
       franchises,
       <i>
        <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/James_Bond_in_film" title="James Bond in film">
         James Bond
        </a>
       </i>
       and
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/List_of_Star_Trek_films_and_television_series" title="List of Star Trek films and television series">
         Star Trek
        </a>
       </i>
       films are still being released periodically; all four are among the highest-grossing franchises. Some of the older films that held the record of highest-grossing film still have respectable grosses by today's standards, but no longer compete numerically against today's top-earners in an era of much higher individual ticket prices. When those prices are adjusted for inflation, however, then
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Gone_with_the_Wind_(film)" title="Gone with the Wind (film)">
         Gone with the Wind
        </a>
       </i>
       —which was the highest-grossing film outright for twenty-five years—is still the highest-grossing film of all time. All grosses on the list are expressed in U.S. dollars at their nominal value, except where stated otherwise.
      </p>
      <style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r886046785">
       .mw-parser-output .toclimit-2 .toclevel-1 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-3 .toclevel-2 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-4 .toclevel-3 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-5 .toclevel-4 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-6 .toclevel-5 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-7 .toclevel-6 ul{display:none}
      </style>
      <div class="toclimit-2">
       <div aria-labelledby="mw-toc-heading" class="toc" id="toc" role="navigation">
        <input class="toctogglecheckbox" id="toctogglecheckbox" role="button" style="display:none" type="checkbox"/>
        <div class="toctitle" dir="ltr" lang="en">
         <h2 id="mw-toc-heading">
          Contents
         </h2>
         <span class="toctogglespan">
          <label class="toctogglelabel" for="toctogglecheckbox">
          </label>
         </span>
        </div>
        <ul>
         <li class="toclevel-1 tocsection-1">
          <a href="#Highest-grossing_films">
           <span class="tocnumber">
            1
           </span>
           <span class="toctext">
            Highest-grossing films
           </span>
          </a>
         </li>
         <li class="toclevel-1 tocsection-2">
          <a href="#Highest-grossing_films_adjusted_for_inflation">
           <span class="tocnumber">
            2
           </span>
           <span class="toctext">
            Highest-grossing films adjusted for inflation
           </span>
          </a>
         </li>
         <li class="toclevel-1 tocsection-3">
          <a href="#High-grossing_films_by_year">
           <span class="tocnumber">
            3
           </span>

            High-grossing films by year
           </span>
          </a>
         </li>
         <li class="toclevel-1 tocsection-4">
          <a href="#Timeline_of_highest-grossing_films">
           <span class="tocnumber">
            4
           </span>
           <span class="toctext">
            Timeline of highest-grossing films
           </span>
          </a>
         </li>
         <li class="toclevel-1 tocsection-5">
          <a href="#Highest-grossing_franchises_and_film_series">
           <span class="tocnumber">
            5
           </span>

            Highest-grossing franchises and film series
           </span>
          </a>
         </li>

          <a href="#See_also">
           <span class="tocnumber">
            6
           </span>

           </span>
          </a>
         </li>

           <span class="tocnumber">
            7
           </span>

           </span>
          </a>
          <ul>
           <li class="toclevel-2 tocsection-8">
            <a href="#Box_office_sources">
             <span class="tocnumber">
              7.1
             </span>
             <span class="toctext">
              Box office sources
             </span>
            </a>
           </li>
           <li class="toclevel-2 tocsection-9">
            <a href="#§_Franchise_and_series_sources">
             <span class="tocnumber">

             </span>

               <b>
                <sup>
                 §
                </sup>

         </li>
        </ul>
       </div>
      </div>
      <h2>
       <span class="mw-headline" id="Highest-grossing_films">
        Highest-grossing films
       </span>
      </h2>
      <div class="thumb tright">
       <div class="thumbinner" style="width:172px;">
        <a class="image" href="/wiki/File:Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_(cropped).jpg">
         <img alt="A portrait of a middle aged man." class="thumbimage" data-file-height="2193" data-file-width="1530" decoding="async" height="244" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_%28cropped%29.jpg/170px-Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_%28cropped%29.jpg" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_%28cropped%29.jpg/255px-Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_%28cropped%29.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_%28cropped%29.jpg/340px-Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_%28cropped%29.jpg 2x" width="170"/>
        </a>
        <div class="thumbcaption">
         <div class="magnify">
          <a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_(cropped).jpg" title="Enlarge">
          </a>
         </div>
         Eight of the twenty-five highest-grossing films, including
         <i>
          Avengers: Endgame
         </i>
         at number one, were produced by
         <a href="/wiki/Marvel_Studios" title="Marvel Studios">
          Marvel Studios
         </a>
         president
         <a href="/wiki/Kevin_Feige" title="Kevin Feige">
          Kevin Feige
         </a>
         .
        </div>
       </div>
      </div>
      <p>
       With a worldwide box-office gross of over $2.797 billion,
       <i>
        Avengers: Endgame
       </i>
       is proclaimed to be the "highest-grossing" film, but such claims usually refer to
       <a href="/wiki/Movie_theater" title="Movie theater">
        theatrical
       </a>
       revenues only and do not take into account
       <a href="/wiki/Home_video" title="Home video">
        home video
       </a>
       and
       <a href="/wiki/Television" title="Television">
        television
       </a>
       income, which can form a significant portion of a film's earnings. Once revenue from home entertainment is factored in it is not immediately clear which film is the most successful.
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Titanic_(1997_film)" title="Titanic (1997 film)">
         Titanic
        </a>
       </i>
       earned $1.2 billion from
       <a href="/wiki/Video" title="Video">
        video
       </a>
       and
       <a href="/wiki/DVD" title="DVD">
        DVD
       </a>
       sales and rentals,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-titanic-sales_1-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-titanic-sales-1">
         [1]
        </a>
       </sup>
       in addition to the $2.2 billion it grossed in theaters. While complete sales data are not available for
       <i>
        Avatar
       </i>
       , it earned $345 million from the sale of sixteen million DVD and
       <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Blu-ray_Disc" title="Blu-ray Disc">
        Blu-ray
       </a>
       units in North America,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-2">
        <a href="#cite_note-2">
         [2]
        </a>
       </sup>
       and ultimately sold a total of thirty million DVD and Blu-ray units worldwide.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-3">
        <a href="#cite_note-3">
         [3]
        </a>
       </sup>
       After home video income is accounted for, both films have earned over $3 billion each. Television
       <a href="/wiki/Broadcasting" title="Broadcasting">
        broadcast
       </a>
       rights will also substantially add to a film's earnings, with a film often earning as much as 20–25% of its theatrical box office for a couple of television runs on top of
       <a href="/wiki/Pay-per-view" title="Pay-per-view">
        pay-per-view
       </a>
       revenues;
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-4">
        <a href="#cite_note-4">
         [4]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <i>
        Titanic
       </i>
       earned a further $55 million from the
       <a href="/wiki/NBC" title="NBC">
        NBC
       </a>
       and
       <a href="/wiki/HBO" title="HBO">
        HBO
       </a>
       broadcast rights,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-titanic-sales_1-1">
        <a href="#cite_note-titanic-sales-1">
         [1]
        </a>
       </sup>
       equating to about 9% of its North American gross.
      </p>
      <p>
       When a film is highly exploitable as a commercial property, its ancillary revenues can dwarf its income from direct film sales.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-5">
        <a href="#cite_note-5">
         [5]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <i>
        The Lion King
       </i>
       (1994) earned over $2 billion in box-office and home video sales,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Pincus-Roth_(2006)_6-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Pincus-Roth_(2006)-6">
         [6]
        </a>
       </sup>
       but this pales in comparison to the $8 billion earned at box offices around the world by
       <a href="/wiki/The_Lion_King_(musical)" title="The Lion King (musical)">
        the stage adaptation
       </a>
       .
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-7">
        <a href="#cite_note-7">
         [7]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <a href="/wiki/Merchandising" title="Merchandising">
        Merchandising
       </a>
       can be extremely lucrative too:
       <i>
        The Lion King
       </i>
       also sold $3 billion of merchandise,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-8">
        <a href="#cite_note-8">
         [8]
        </a>
       </sup>
       while Pixar's
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Cars_(film)" title="Cars (film)">
         Cars
        </a>
       </i>
       —which earned $462 million in theatrical revenues and was only a modest hit by comparison to other
       <a href="/wiki/List_of_Pixar_films" title="List of Pixar films">
        Pixar films
       </a>
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-9">
        <a href="#cite_note-9">
         [9]
        </a>
       </sup>
       —generated global merchandise sales of over $8 billion in the five years after its 2006 release.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-10">
        <a href="#cite_note-10">
         [10]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-11">
        <a href="#cite_note-11">
         [11]
        </a>
       </sup>
       Pixar had another huge hit with
       <i>
        Toy Story 3
       </i>
       , which generated almost $10 billion in merchandise retail sales in addition to the $1 billion it earned at the box office.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-12">
        <a href="#cite_note-12">
         [12]
        </a>
       </sup>
      </p>
      <p>
       On this chart, films are ranked by the revenues from theatrical exhibition at their nominal value, along with the highest positions they attained. Five films in total have grossed in excess of $2 billion worldwide, with
       <i>
        Avengers: Endgame
       </i>
       ranked in the top position. All of the films have had a theatrical run (including re-releases) in the 21st century, and films that have not played during this period do not appear on the chart because of ticket-price inflation, population size and ticket purchasing trends not being considered.
      </p>
      <dl>
       <dd>
       </dd>
       <dd>
        <style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r981673959">
         .mw-parser-output .legend{page-break-inside:avoid;break-inside:avoid-column}.mw-parser-output .legend-color{display:inline-block;min-width:1.25em;height:1.25em;line-height:1.25;margin:1px 0;text-align:center;border:1px solid black;background-color:transparent;color:black}.mw-parser-output .legend-text{}
        </style>
        <div class="legend">
         <span class="legend-color" style="background-color:#b6fcb6; color:black;font-size:50%;">
         </span>
         <img alt="film currently playing" data-file-height="14" data-file-width="9" decoding="async" height="14" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/37/Dagger-14-plain.png" width="9"/>
         <i>
          Background shading indicates films playing in the week commencing 5 March 2021 in theaters around the world
         </i>
         .
        </div>
       </dd>
      </dl>
      <table class="wikitable sortable plainrowheaders" style="margin:auto; margin:auto;">
       <caption>
        Highest-grossing films
        <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-13">
         <a href="#cite_note-13">
          [13]
         </a>
        </sup>
       </caption>
       <tbody>

         </th>

         </th>
         <th class="unsortable" scope="col">

         </th>
        </tr>
        <tr>

         </td>

         </td>

           <a href="/wiki/Avengers:_Endgame" title="Avengers: Endgame">

           </a>
          </i>
         </th>
         <td align="right">
          $2,797,800,564
         </td>
         <td data-sort-value="2019-04" style="text-align:center;">

         </td>
         <td style="text-align:center;">
          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-endgame_14-0">

         <th scope="row">

           <a href="/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film)" title="Avatar (2009 film)">
            Avatar
           </a>
          </i>
         </th>
         <td align="right">
          $2,790,439,000
         </td>
         <td style="text-align:center;">
          2009
         </td>
         <td style="text-align:center;">
          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-avatar_16-0">
           <a href="#cite_note-avatar-16">
            [# 3]
           </a>
          </sup>
          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-avatar_peak_17-0">
           <a href="#cite_note-avatar_peak-17">
            [# 4]
           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
         <td>
          3
         </td>
         <td>
          1
         </td>
         <th scope="row">
          <i>
           <a href="/wiki/Titanic_(1997_film)" title="Titanic (1997 film)">
            Titanic
           </a>
          </i>
         </th>
         <td align="right">
          $2,194,439,542
         </td>
         <td style="text-align:center;">
          1997
         </td>
         <td style="text-align:center;">
          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-titanic_18-0">
           <a href="#cite_note-titanic-18">
            [# 5]
           </a>
          </sup>
          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-titanic_peak_19-0">
           <a href="#cite_note-titanic_peak-19">
            [# 6]
           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
         <td>
          4
         </td>
         <td>
          3
         </td>
         <th scope="row">
          <i>
           <a href="/wiki/Star_Wars:_The_Force_Awakens" title="Star Wars: The Force Awakens">
            Star Wars: The Force Awakens
           </a>
          </i>
         </th>
         <td align="right">
          $2,068,223,624
         </td>

      <style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1011217839">
       .mw-parser-output .refbegin{font-size:90%;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .refbegin-hanging-indents>ul{margin-left:0}.mw-parser-output .refbegin-hanging-indents>ul>li{margin-left:0;padding-left:3.2em;text-indent:-3.2em}.mw-parser-output .refbegin-hanging-indents ul,.mw-parser-output .refbegin-hanging-indents ul li{list-style:none}@media(max-width:720px){.mw-parser-output .refbegin-hanging-indents>ul>li{padding-left:1.6em;text-indent:-1.6em}}.mw-parser-output .refbegin-100{font-size:100%}.mw-parser-output .refbegin-columns{margin-top:0.3em}.mw-parser-output .refbegin-columns ul{margin-top:0}.mw-parser-output .refbegin-columns li{page-break-inside:avoid;break-inside:avoid-column}
      </style>
      <div class="refbegin" style="">
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Frozen">
         <sup>
          F
         </sup>
         <a href="/wiki/Box_Office_Mojo" title="Box Office Mojo">
          Box Office Mojo
         </a>
         stopped updating its main total for
         <i>
          Frozen
         </i>
         in August 2014, while it was still in release. The total listed here incorporates subsequent earnings in Japan, Nigeria, Spain, the United Kingdom and Germany up to the end of 2015 but omits earnings in Turkey, Iceland, Brazil, and Australia (2016), which amount to a few hundred thousand dollars. The total is rounded to $1 million to compensate for the numerical inaccuracy. It was re-released in the United Kingdom in December 2017 with
         <i>
          <a href="/wiki/Olaf%27s_Frozen_Adventure" title="Olaf's Frozen Adventure">
           Olaf's Frozen Adventure
          </a>
         </i>
         earning an additional $2.3 million.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Fast_8">
         <sup>
          F8
         </sup>
         In the case of
         <i>
          The Fate of the Furious
         </i>
         the gross is from an archived version of Box Office Mojo, after irregularities were discovered in the current figure. Ongoing weekly drops in the totals for several countries—Argentina being the worst affected—led to a drop in the overall worldwide total.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-94">
          <a href="#cite_note-94">
           [15]
          </a>
         </sup>
         In view of what appears to be an aberration in the source, a previous figure is provided.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_LOTR3">
         <sup>
          RK
         </sup>
         <i>
          Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
         </i>
         saw its original gross corrected in 2019. The result of this correction is that
         <i>
          Spider-Man: Far From Home
         </i>
         ,
         <i>
          Captain Marvel
         </i>
         and
         <i>
          Transformers: Dark of the Moon
         </i>
         all peaked one place lower than shown in the accompanying source.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_TS3">
         <sup>
          TS3
         </sup>
         Box Office Mojo revised the grosses for Pixar films in August 2016, resulting in the gross for
         <i>
          Toy Story 3
         </i>
         being corrected from $1.063 billion to $1.067 billion.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-95">
          <a href="#cite_note-95">
           [16]
          </a>
         </sup>
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-96">
          <a href="#cite_note-96">
           [17]
          </a>
         </sup>
         This means that it peaked at number 4 at the end of its run, ahead of
         <i>
          <a href="/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean:_Dead_Man%27s_Chest" title="Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest">
           Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest
          </a>
         </i>
         , rather than at number 5 as indicated by the source.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_DM2">
         <sup>
          DM2
         </sup>
         Disney issued an erratum to the gross for
         <i>
          <a href="/wiki/The_Lion_King" title="The Lion King">
           The Lion King
          </a>
         </i>
         in May 2016, correcting its gross from $987.5 million to $968.5 million.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-97">
          <a href="#cite_note-97">
           [18]
          </a>
         </sup>
         This means that
         <i>
          Despicable Me 2
         </i>
         peaked at number 19 at the end of its run, ahead of
         <i>
          The Lion King
         </i>
         , rather than at number 20 as indicated by the source.
        </span>
       </p>
      </div>
      <h2>
       <span class="mw-headline" id="Highest-grossing_films_adjusted_for_inflation">
        Highest-grossing films adjusted for inflation
       </span>
      </h2>
      <div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">
       See also:
       <a href="/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films_in_the_United_States_and_Canada#Adjusted_for_ticket-price_inflation" title="List of highest-grossing films in the United States and Canada">
        List of highest-grossing films in the United States and Canada § Adjusted for ticket-price inflation
       </a>
      </div>
      <div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">
       See also:
       <a href="/wiki/List_of_films_by_box_office_admissions" title="List of films by box office admissions">
        List of films by box office admissions
       </a>
      </div>
      <div class="thumb tright">
       <div class="thumbinner" style="width:222px;">
        <a class="image" href="/wiki/File:World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg">
         <img alt="A map of the world with different regions colored in correlating to inflation rates" class="thumbimage" data-file-height="1314" data-file-width="2560" decoding="async" height="113" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg/220px-World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg.png" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg/330px-World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg.png 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg/440px-World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg.png 2x" width="220"/>
        </a>
        <div class="thumbcaption">
         <div class="magnify">
          <a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg" title="Enlarge">
          </a>
         </div>
         Inflation rates around the world vary, complicating inflation adjustment.
        </div>
       </div>
      </div>
      <p>
       Because of the long-term effects of
       <a href="/wiki/Inflation" title="Inflation">
        inflation
       </a>
       , notably the significant increase of movie theater ticket prices, the list unadjusted for inflation gives far more weight to later films.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Bialik_(2010-1-29)_98-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Bialik_(2010-1-29)-98">
         [19]
        </a>
       </sup>
       The unadjusted list, while commonly found in the press, is therefore largely meaningless for comparing films widely separated in time, as many films from earlier eras will never appear on a modern unadjusted list, despite achieving higher commercial success when adjusted for price increases.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Pincus-Roth_(2009)_99-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Pincus-Roth_(2009)-99">
         [20]
        </a>
       </sup>
       To compensate for the devaluation of the currency, some charts make adjustments for inflation, but not even this practice fully addresses the issue, since ticket prices and inflation do not necessarily parallel one another. For example, in 1970, tickets cost $1.55 or about $6.68 in inflation-adjusted 2004 dollars; by 1980, prices had risen to about $2.69, a drop to $5.50 in inflation-adjusted 2004 dollars.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-LLU_(2004)_100-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-LLU_(2004)-100">
         [21]
        </a>
       </sup>
       Ticket prices have also risen at different rates of inflation around the world, further complicating the process of adjusting worldwide grosses.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Bialik_(2010-1-29)_98-1">
        <a href="#cite_note-Bialik_(2010-1-29)-98">
         [19]
        </a>
       </sup>
      </p>
      <p>
       Another complication is release in multiple formats for which different ticket prices are charged. One notable example of this phenomenon is
       <i>
        Avatar
       </i>
       , which was also released in 3D and IMAX: almost two-thirds of tickets for that film were for 3D showings with an average price of $10, and about one-sixth were for IMAX showings with an average price over $14.50, compared to a 2010 average price of $7.61 for 2D films.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Gray_(2010-02-03)_101-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Gray_(2010-02-03)-101">
         [22]
        </a>
       </sup>
       Social and economic factors such as population change
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Bialik_(2010-1-30)_102-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Bialik_(2010-1-30)-102">
         [23]
        </a>
       </sup>
       and the growth of international markets
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Kolesnikov-Jessop_103-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Kolesnikov-Jessop-103">
         [24]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Hoad_104-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Hoad-104">
         [25]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Frankel_105-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Frankel-105">
         [26]
        </a>
       </sup>
       also impact on the number of people purchasing theater tickets, along with audience demographics where some films sell a much higher proportion of discounted children's tickets, or perform better in big cities where tickets cost more.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Pincus-Roth_(2009)_99-1">
        <a href="#cite_note-Pincus-Roth_(2009)-99">
         [20]
        </a>
       </sup>
      </p>
      <p>
       The measuring system for gauging a film's success is based on unadjusted grosses, mainly because historically this is the way it has always been done because of the practices of the film industry: the box-office receipts are compiled by theaters and relayed to the distributor, which in turn releases them to the media.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Bialik_(2007)_106-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Bialik_(2007)-106">
         [27]
        </a>
       </sup>
       Converting to a more representative system that counts ticket sales rather than gross is also fraught with problems because the only data available for older films are the sale totals.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Bialik_(2010-1-30)_102-1">
        <a href="#cite_note-Bialik_(2010-1-30)-102">
         [23]
        </a>
       </sup>
       As the motion picture industry is highly oriented towards marketing currently released films, unadjusted figures are always used in marketing campaigns so that new blockbuster films can much more easily achieve a high sales ranking, and thus be promoted as a "top film of all time",
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-LLU_(2004)_100-1">
        <a href="#cite_note-LLU_(2004)-100">
         [21]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Leonhardt_107-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Leonhardt-107">
         [28]
        </a>
       </sup>
       so there is little incentive to switch to a more robust analysis from a marketing or even newsworthy point of view.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Bialik_(2007)_106-1">
        <a href="#cite_note-Bialik_(2007)-106">
         [27]
        </a>
       </sup>
      </p>
      <p>
       Despite the inherent difficulties in accounting for inflation, several attempts have been made. Estimates depend on the
       <a href="/wiki/Price_index" title="Price index">
        price index
       </a>
       used to adjust the grosses,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Leonhardt_107-1">
        <a href="#cite_note-Leonhardt-107">
         [28]
        </a>
       </sup>
       and the
       <a href="/wiki/Exchange_rate" title="Exchange rate">
        exchange rates
       </a>
       used to convert between currencies can also impact upon the calculations, both of which can have an effect on the ultimate rankings of an inflation adjusted list.
       <i>
        Gone with the Wind
       </i>
       —first released in 1939—is generally considered to be the most successful film, with
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Guinness_World_Records" title="Guinness World Records">
         Guinness World Records
        </a>
       </i>
       in 2014 estimating its adjusted global gross at $3.4 billion. Estimates for
       <i>
        Gone with the Wind
       </i>
       <span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">
        '
       </span>
       s adjusted gross have varied substantially: its owner,
       <a href="/wiki/Turner_Entertainment" title="Turner Entertainment">
        Turner Entertainment
       </a>
       , estimated its adjusted earnings at $3.3 billion in 2007, a few years earlier than the
       <i>
        Guinness
       </i>
       estimate;
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-gwtw_tcm_108-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-gwtw_tcm-108">
         [29]
        </a>
       </sup>
       other estimates fall either side of this amount, with one putting its gross just under $3 billion in 2010,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Shone_109-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Shone-109">
         [30]
        </a>
       </sup>
       while another provided an alternative figure of $3.8 billion in 2006.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-110">
        <a href="#cite_note-110">
         [31]
        </a>
       </sup>
       Which film is
       <i>
        Gone with the Wind
       </i>
       <span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">
        '
       </span>
       s nearest rival depends on the set of figures used:
       <i>
        Guinness
       </i>
       had
       <i>
        Avatar
       </i>
       in second place with $3 billion, while other estimates saw
       <i>
        Titanic
       </i>
       in the runner-up spot with first-run worldwide earnings of almost $2.9 billion at 2010 prices.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Shone_109-1">
        <a href="#cite_note-Shone-109">
         [30]
        </a>
       </sup>
      </p>
      <table class="wikitable sortable plainrowheaders" style="margin:auto; margin:auto;">
       <caption>
        <span class="nowrap">
         Highest-grossing films as of 2019
         <sup class="plainlinks noexcerpt noprint asof-tag update" style="display:none;">
          <a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=List_of_highest-grossing_films&amp;action=edit">
           [update]
          </a>
         </sup>
         adjusted for inflation
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Guinness_2015_111-0">
          <a href="#cite_note-Guinness_2015-111">
           [32]
          </a>
         </sup>
        </span>
        <sup class="reference" id="ref_Inflation">
         <a href="#endnote_Inflation">
          [Inf]
         </a>
        </sup>
       </caption>
       <tbody>

          Rank

            (2019 $)
           </span>
          </div>

         </th>
        </tr>

         <td>

         </th>
         <td style="text-align:right;">
          $3,706,000,000
         </td>

         <td>
          2
         </td>
         <th scope="row">

           </a>
          </i>
         </th>
         <td style="text-align:right;">

           <a href="/wiki/Titanic_(1997_film)" title="Titanic (1997 film)">

          </i>
         </th>
         <td style="text-align:right;">
          <span style="display:none">
           $2,516,000,000
          </span>
          <sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Titanic">
           <a href="#endnote_Titanic">
            T
           </a>
          </sup>
          $3,081,000,000
         </td>
         <td style="text-align:center;">
          1997
         </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
         <td>
          4
         </td>
         <th scope="row">
          <i>
           <a href="/wiki/Star_Wars_(film)" title="Star Wars (film)">
            Star Wars
           </a>

         </th>

        <tr>

       </tbody>
      </table>
      <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1011217839" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
      <div class="refbegin" style="">
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Inflation">
         <sup>
          Inf
         </sup>
         Inflation adjustment is carried out using the
         <a href="/wiki/Consumer_price_index" title="Consumer price index">
          Consumer price index
         </a>
         for advanced economies published by the
         <a href="/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund" title="International Monetary Fund">
          International Monetary Fund
         </a>
         .
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-112">
          <a href="#cite_note-112">
           [33]
          </a>
         </sup>
         The index is uniformly applied to the grosses in the chart published by
         <i>
          Guinness World Records
         </i>
         in 2014, beginning with the 2014 index. The figures in the above chart take into account inflation that occurred in 2014, and in every available year since then, through 2019.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Titanic">
         <sup>
          T
         </sup>
         <i>
          Guinness
         </i>
         <span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">
          '
         </span>
         adjusted total for
         <i>
          Titanic
         </i>
         only increased by $102,000,000 between the 2012 (published in 2011) and 2015 editions, a rise of 4.2% shared by the other adjusted totals in the chart, and omitted the gross from a 3D re-release in 2012.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Guinness_2015_111-1">
          <a href="#cite_note-Guinness_2015-111">
           [32]
          </a>
         </sup>
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-113">
          <a href="#cite_note-113">
           [34]
          </a>
         </sup>
         This chart incorporates the gross of $343,550,770 from the reissue and adjusts it from the 2014 index.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-114">
          <a href="#cite_note-114">
           [35]
          </a>
         </sup>
         <i>
          Titanic
         </i>
         grossed a further $691,642 during a limited re-release in 2017 for its 20th anniversary, but this figure is not represented in the adjusted total.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-115">
          <a href="#cite_note-115">
           [36]
          </a>
         </sup>
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Avengers_Endgame">
         <sup>
          AE
         </sup>
         Since
         <i>
          Avengers: Endgame
         </i>
         was released in 2019, no inflation adjustment has been applied to its gross.
        </span>
       </p>
      </div>
      <h2>
       <span class="mw-headline" id="High-grossing_films_by_year">
        High-grossing films by year
       </span>
      </h2>
      <div class="quotebox pullquote floatright" style="width:20em;; ; background-color: #F0EAD6;;">
       <style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r887775652">
        .mw-parser-output .quotebox{background-color:#F9F9F9;border:1px solid #aaa;box-sizing:border-box;padding:10px;font-size:88%;max-width:100%}.mw-parser-output .quotebox.floatleft{margin:0.5em 1.4em 0.8em 0}.mw-parser-output .quotebox.floatright{margin:0.5em 0 0.8em 1.4em}.mw-parser-output .quotebox.centered{margin:0.5em auto 0.8em auto}.mw-parser-output .quotebox.floatleft p,.mw-parser-output .quotebox.floatright p{font-style:inherit}.mw-parser-output .quotebox-title{background-color:#F9F9F9;text-align:center;font-size:larger;font-weight:bold}.mw-parser-output .quotebox-quote.quoted:before{font-family:"Times New Roman",serif;font-weight:bold;font-size:large;color:gray;content:" “ ";vertical-align:-45%;line-height:0}.mw-parser-output .quotebox-quote.quoted:after{font-family:"Times New Roman",serif;font-weight:bold;font-size:large;color:gray;content:" ” ";line-height:0}.mw-parser-output .quotebox .left-aligned{text-align:left}.mw-parser-output .quotebox .right-aligned{text-align:right}.mw-parser-output .quotebox .center-aligned{text-align:center}.mw-parser-output .quotebox cite{display:block;font-style:normal}@media screen and (max-width:360px){.mw-parser-output .quotebox{min-width:100%;margin:0 0 0.8em!important;float:none!important}}
       </style>
       <div class="quotebox-title" style="background-color: #F0EAD6;;">
        <span class="anchor" id="distributor_rental">
        </span>
        Glossary:
        <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Theatrical_rental" title="Theatrical rental">
         Distributor rentals
        </a>
       </div>
       <div class="quotebox-quote left-aligned" style="">
        Box-office figures are reported in the form of gross or
        <i>
         distributor rentals
        </i>
        , the latter being especially true of older films. Commonly mistaken for home video revenue, the rentals are the distributor's share of the film's theatrical revenue i.e. the box office gross less the exhibitor's cut.
        <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Cones_116-0">
         <a href="#cite_note-Cones-116">
          [37]
         </a>
        </sup>
        <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Marich_117-0">
         <a href="#cite_note-Marich-117">
          [38]
         </a>
        </sup>
        Historically, the rental price averaged at 30–40% when the distributors owned the theater chains, equating to just over a third of the gross being paid to the distributor of the film.
        <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Balio_118-0">
         <a href="#cite_note-Balio-118">
          [39]
         </a>
        </sup>
        In the modern marketplace, rental fees can vary greatly—depending on a number of factors—although the films from the major studios average out at 43%.
        <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Cones_116-1">
         <a href="#cite_note-Cones-116">
          [37]
         </a>
        </sup>
       </div>
      </div>
      <p>
       Audience tastes were fairly eclectic during the 20th century, but several trends did emerge. During the
       <a href="/wiki/Silent_film" title="Silent film">
        silent era
       </a>
       , films with
       <a href="/wiki/War_film" title="War film">
        war themes
       </a>
       were popular with audiences, with
       <i>
        The Birth of a Nation
       </i>
       (
       <a href="/wiki/American_Civil_War" title="American Civil War">
        American Civil War
       </a>
       ),
       <i>
        The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        The Big Parade
       </i>
       and
       <i>
        Wings
       </i>
       (all
       <a href="/wiki/World_War_I" title="World War I">
        World War I
       </a>
       ) becoming the most successful films in their respective years of release, with the trend coming to an end with
       <i>
        All Quiet on the Western Front
       </i>
       in 1930. With the advent of
       <a href="/wiki/Sound_film" title="Sound film">
        sound
       </a>
       in 1927, the
       <a href="/wiki/Musical_film" title="Musical film">
        musical
       </a>
       —the genre best placed to showcase the new technology—took over as the most popular type of film with audiences, with 1928 and 1929 both being topped by musical films. The genre continued to perform strongly in the 1930s, but the outbreak of
       <a href="/wiki/World_War_II" title="World War II">
        World War II
       </a>
       saw war-themed films dominate again during this period, starting with
       <i>
        Gone with the Wind
       </i>
       (American Civil War) in 1939, and finishing with
       <i>
        The Best Years of Our Lives
       </i>
       (World War II) in 1946.
       <i>
        Samson and Delilah
       </i>
       (1949) saw the beginning of a trend of increasingly expensive
       <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/List_of_historical_drama_films" title="List of historical drama films">
        historical dramas
       </a>
       set during
       <a href="/wiki/Ancient_Rome" title="Ancient Rome">
        Ancient Rome
       </a>
       /
       <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Biblical_times" title="Biblical times">
        biblical times
       </a>
       throughout the 1950s as cinema competed with television for audiences,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-119">
        <a href="#cite_note-119">
         [40]
        </a>
       </sup>
       with
       <i>
        Quo Vadis
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        The Robe
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        The Ten Commandments
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        Ben-Hur
       </i>
       and
       <i>
        Spartacus
       </i>
       all becoming the highest-grossing film of the year during initial release, before the genre started to wane after several high-profile failures.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HallNeale179_120-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-HallNeale179-120">
         [41]
        </a>
       </sup>
       The success of
       <i>
        White Christmas
       </i>
       and
       <i>
        South Pacific
       </i>
       in the 1950s foreshadowed the comeback of the musical in the 1960s with
       <i>
        West Side Story
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        Mary Poppins
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        My Fair Lady
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        The Sound of Music
       </i>
       and
       <i>
        Funny Girl
       </i>
       all among the top films of the decade. The 1970s saw a shift in audience tastes to
       <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/High_concept" title="High concept">
        high concept
       </a>
       films, with six such films made by either
       <a href="/wiki/George_Lucas" title="George Lucas">
        George Lucas
       </a>
       or
       <a href="/wiki/Steven_Spielberg" title="Steven Spielberg">
        Steven Spielberg
       </a>
       topping the chart during the 1980s. The 21st century has seen an increasing dependence on franchises and
       <a href="/wiki/Film_adaptation" title="Film adaptation">
        adaptations
       </a>
       , with the box-office dominance of films based on pre-existing intellectual property at record levels.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-121">
        <a href="#cite_note-121">
         [42]
        </a>
       </sup>
      </p>
      <div class="thumb tleft">
       <div class="thumbinner" style="width:172px;">
        <a class="image" href="/wiki/File:Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg">
         <img alt="A portrait of a bespectacled middle aged man." class="thumbimage" data-file-height="3248" data-file-width="2416" decoding="async" height="229" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/170px-Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/255px-Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/340px-Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg 2x" width="170"/>
        </a>
        <div class="thumbcaption">
         <div class="magnify">
          <a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg" title="Enlarge">
          </a>
         </div>
         Films directed by Steven Spielberg have been the highest-grossing film of the year on six occasions, and on three occasions have been the highest-grossing film of all time.
        </div>
       </div>
      </div>
      <p>
       Steven Spielberg is the most represented director on the chart, with six films to his credit, occupying the top spot in 1975, 1981, 1982, 1984, 1989 and 1993.
       <a href="/wiki/Cecil_B._DeMille" title="Cecil B. DeMille">
        Cecil B. DeMille
       </a>
       (1932, 1947, 1949, 1952 and 1956) and
       <a href="/wiki/William_Wyler" title="William Wyler">
        William Wyler
       </a>
       (1942, 1946, 1959 and 1968) are in second and third place with five and four films respectively, while
       <a href="/wiki/D._W._Griffith" title="D. W. Griffith">
        D. W. Griffith
       </a>
       (1915, 1916 and 1920),
       <a href="/wiki/George_Roy_Hill" title="George Roy Hill">
        George Roy Hill
       </a>
       (1966, 1969 and 1973),
       <a href="/wiki/James_Cameron" title="James Cameron">
        James Cameron
       </a>
       (1991, 1997 and 2009) and the
       <a href="/wiki/Russo_brothers" title="Russo brothers">
        Russo brothers
       </a>
       (2016, 2018 and 2019) all feature heavily with three films apiece. George Lucas directed two chart-toppers in 1977 and 1999, but also served in a strong creative capacity as a producer and writer in 1980, 1981, 1983, 1984 and 1989 as well. The following directors have also all directed two films on the chart:
       <a href="/wiki/Frank_Lloyd" title="Frank Lloyd">
        Frank Lloyd
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/King_Vidor" title="King Vidor">
        King Vidor
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Frank_Capra" title="Frank Capra">
        Frank Capra
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Michael_Curtiz" title="Michael Curtiz">
        Michael Curtiz
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Leo_McCarey" title="Leo McCarey">
        Leo McCarey
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock" title="Alfred Hitchcock">
        Alfred Hitchcock
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/David_Lean" title="David Lean">
        David Lean
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick" title="Stanley Kubrick">
        Stanley Kubrick
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Guy_Hamilton" title="Guy Hamilton">
        Guy Hamilton
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Mike_Nichols" title="Mike Nichols">
        Mike Nichols
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/William_Friedkin" title="William Friedkin">
        William Friedkin
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Peter_Jackson" title="Peter Jackson">
        Peter Jackson
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Gore_Verbinski" title="Gore Verbinski">
        Gore Verbinski
       </a>
       , and
       <a href="/wiki/Michael_Bay" title="Michael Bay">
        Michael Bay
       </a>
       ;
       <a href="/wiki/Mervyn_LeRoy" title="Mervyn LeRoy">
        Mervyn LeRoy
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Ken_Annakin" title="Ken Annakin">
        Ken Annakin
       </a>
       and
       <a href="/wiki/Robert_Wise" title="Robert Wise">
        Robert Wise
       </a>
       are each represented by one solo credit and one shared credit, and
       <a href="/wiki/John_Ford" title="John Ford">
        John Ford
       </a>
       co-directed two films. Disney films are usually co-directed and some directors have served on several winning teams:
       <a href="/wiki/Wilfred_Jackson" title="Wilfred Jackson">
        Wilfred Jackson
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Hamilton_Luske" title="Hamilton Luske">
        Hamilton Luske
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Clyde_Geronimi" title="Clyde Geronimi">
        Clyde Geronimi
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/David_Hand_(animator)" title="David Hand (animator)">
        David Hand
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Ben_Sharpsteen" title="Ben Sharpsteen">
        Ben Sharpsteen
       </a>
       ,
       <a href="/wiki/Wolfgang_Reitherman" title="Wolfgang Reitherman">
        Wolfgang Reitherman
       </a>
       and Bill Roberts have all co-directed at least two films on the list. Only seven directors have topped the chart in consecutive years: McCarey (1944 and 1945), Nichols (1966 and 1967), Spielberg (1981 and 1982), Jackson (2002 and 2003), Verbinski (2006 and 2007) and the Russo brothers (2018 and 2019).
      </p>
      <p>
       Because of release schedules—especially in the case of films released towards the end of the year—and different release patterns across the world, many films can do business in two or more calendar years; therefore the grosses documented here are not confined to just the year of release. Grosses are not limited to original theatrical runs either, with many older films often being re-released periodically so the figures represent all the business a film has done since its original release; a film's first-run gross is included in brackets after the total if known. Because of incomplete data it cannot be known for sure how much money some films have made and when they made it, but generally the chart chronicles the films from each year that went on to earn the most. In the cases where estimates conflict both films are recorded, and in cases where a film has moved into first place because of being re-released the previous record-holder is also retained.
      </p>
      <dl>
       <dd>
       </dd>
       <dd>
        <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r981673959" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
        <div class="legend">
         <span class="legend-color" style="background-color:#b6fcb6; color:black;font-size:50%;">
         </span>
         <img alt="film currently playing" data-file-height="14" data-file-width="9" decoding="async" height="14" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/37/Dagger-14-plain.png" width="9"/>
         <i>
          Background shading indicates films playing in the week commencing 5 March 2021 in theaters around the world
         </i>
         .
        </div>
       </dd>
      </dl>
      <div style="clear:both;">
      </div>

        <tr>
         <td>
          <i>
           <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Ben-Hur_(1925_film)" title="Ben-Hur (1925 film)">
            Ben-Hur
           </a>
          </i>
         </td>
         <td align="right">
          <span data-sort-value="7007107380000000000♠">
           $10,738,000
          </span>
          <sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Rentals">
           <a href="#endnote_Rentals">
            R
           </a>
          </sup>
          (
          <span data-sort-value="7006938600000000000♠">
           $9,386,000
          </span>
