            [# 300]
           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
        </tr>
       </tbody>
      </table>
      <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1011217839" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
      <div class="refbegin" style="">
       <p>
        (...) Since grosses are not limited to original theatrical runs, a film's first-run gross is included in brackets after the total if known.
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_USgross">
         <sup>
          *
         </sup>
         Canada and U.S. gross only.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Rentals">
         <sup>
          R
         </sup>
         Distributor rental.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote">
         <sup>
          TBA
         </sup>
         To be ascertained.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Intolerance">
         <sup>
          IN
         </sup>
         No contemporary sources provide figures for
         <i>
          20,000 Leagues Under the Sea
         </i>
         , although
         <a href="/wiki/The_Numbers_(website)" title="The Numbers (website)">
          The Numbers
         </a>
         provides a figure of $8,000,000 for the North American box-office gross.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-346">
          <a href="#cite_note-346">
           [46]
          </a>
         </sup>
         However, it is possible this figure has been mistaken for the gross of the 1954 remake which also earned $8,000,000 in North American rentals.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Finler358_347-0">
          <a href="#cite_note-Finler358-347">
           [47]
          </a>
         </sup>
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_For_Heaven's_Sake">
         <sup>
          FH
         </sup>
         Some sources such as The Numbers state that
         <i>
          Aloma of the South Seas
         </i>
         is the highest grossing film of the year, earning $3 million.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-348">
          <a href="#cite_note-348">
           [48]
          </a>
         </sup>
         However, no contemporary sources provide figures for
         <i>
          Aloma of the South Seas
         </i>
         , so it is unclear what the $3 million figure relates to. If it were the rental gross then that would have made it not only the highest-grossing film of the year, but one of the highest-grossing films of the silent era, and if that is the case it would be unusual for both
         <i>
          International Motion Picture Almanac
         </i>
         and
         <i>
          Variety
         </i>
         to omit it from their lists.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Sunny_Side_Up">
         <sup>
          SS
         </sup>
         It is not clear if the figure for
         <i>
          Sunny Side Up
         </i>
         is for North America or worldwide. Other sources put its earnings at $2 million,
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-349">
          <a href="#cite_note-349">
           [49]
          </a>
         </sup>
         which may suggest the higher figure is the worldwide rental, given the confusion over international figures during this period.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HallNeale6_350-0">
          <a href="#cite_note-HallNeale6-350">
           [50]
          </a>
         </sup>
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_One_Night">
         <sup>
          ON
         </sup>
         The figure for
         <i>
          It Happened One Night
         </i>
         is not truly representative of its success: it was distributed as a package deal along with more than two dozen other Columbia films, and the total earnings were averaged out; the true gross would have been much higher.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Snow_White">
         <sup>
          S7
         </sup>
         <i>
          Snow White
         </i>
         <span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">
          '
         </span>
         s $418 million global
         <a href="/wiki/Cume" title="Cume">
          cume
         </a>
         omits earnings outside of North America from 1987 onwards.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_GWTW">
         <sup>
          GW
         </sup>
         It is not absolutely clear how much
         <i>
          Gone with the Wind
         </i>
         earned from its initial release. Contemporary accounts often list it as earning $32 million in North American rentals and retrospective charts have often duplicated this claim; however, it is likely this was the worldwide rental figure. Trade journals would collate the data by either obtaining it from the distributors themselves, who were keen to promote a successful film, or by surveying theaters and constructing an estimate. Distributors would often report the worldwide rental since the higher figure made the film appear more successful, while estimates were limited to performance in North America; therefore it was not unusual for worldwide and North American rentals to be mixed up. Following the outbreak of World War II, many of the foreign markets were unavailable to Hollywood so it became standard practice to just report on North American box-office performance.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HallNeale6_350-1">
          <a href="#cite_note-HallNeale6-350">
           [50]
          </a>
         </sup>
         In keeping with this new approach, the North American rental for
         <i>
          Gone with the Wind
         </i>
         was revised to $21 million in 1947 ($11 million lower than the previous figure),
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-351">
          <a href="#cite_note-351">
           [51]
          </a>
         </sup>
         and as of 1953—following the 1947 re-release—
         <i>
          Variety
         </i>
         was reporting earnings of $26 million.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-352">
          <a href="#cite_note-352">
           [52]
          </a>
         </sup>
         Through 1956, MGM reported cumulative North American earnings of $30,015,000 and foreign earnings of $18,964,000, from three releases.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-BlockWilson129_353-0">
          <a href="#cite_note-BlockWilson129-353">
           [53]
          </a>
         </sup>
         Worldwide rentals of $32 million from the initial release is consistent with the revised figures and later reported worldwide figures: they indicate that the film earned $21 million in North America and $11 million overseas from the initial release, and added a further $9 million in North America and $8 million overseas from subsequent re-releases up to 1956.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Mom_and_Dad">
         <sup>
          MD
         </sup>
         <i>
          Mom and Dad
         </i>
         does not generally feature in 'high-gross' lists such as those published by
         <i>
          Variety
         </i>
         due to its independent distribution. Essentially belonging to the
         <a href="/wiki/Exploitation_film" title="Exploitation film">
          exploitation genre
         </a>
         , it was marketed as an educational
         <a href="/wiki/Social_guidance_film" title="Social guidance film">
          sex hygiene
         </a>
         film in an effort to circumvent
         <a href="/wiki/Film_censorship" title="Film censorship">
          censorship laws
         </a>
         . Falling foul of the
         <a href="/wiki/Motion_Picture_Production_Code" title="Motion Picture Production Code">
          Motion Picture Production Code
         </a>
         ,
         <i>
          Mom and Dad
         </i>
         was prevented from obtaining mainstream distribution and restricted to independent and
         <a href="/wiki/Drive-in_theater" title="Drive-in theater">
          drive-in theaters
         </a>
         . It was the biggest hit of its kind, and remained in continual distribution until the 1970s when
         <a href="/wiki/Hardcore_pornography" title="Hardcore pornography">
          hardcore pornography
         </a>
         eventually took over. At the end of 1947 it had earned $2 million, and by 1949, $8 million; by 1956 it had earned $22 million in rentals, representing a gross of $80 million, and would have easily placed in the top ten films in the late 1940s and early 1950s. Estimates of its total earnings are as high as $100 million.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Unconquered">
         <sup>
          UN
         </sup>
         Chopra-Gant stipulates that the figure given for
         <i>
          Unconquered
         </i>
         is for North American box-office, but as was common at the time, the chart confuses worldwide and North American grosses. Other sources state that the takings for
         <i>
          Forever Amber
         </i>
         ($8 million) and
         <i>
          Life with Father
         </i>
         ($6.5 million)
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-354">
          <a href="#cite_note-354">
           [54]
          </a>
         </sup>
         were in fact worldwide rental grosses, so it is possible this is also true of
         <i>
          Unconquered
         </i>
         .
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Cinerama">
         <sup>
          CI
         </sup>
         The
         <a href="/wiki/Cinerama" title="Cinerama">
          Cinerama
         </a>
         figures represent gross amounts. Since the Cinerama corporation owned the theaters there were no rental fees for the films, meaning the studio received 100% of the box-office gross, unlike the case with most other films where the distributor typically receives less than half the gross. Since
         <i>
          Variety
         </i>
         at the time ranked films by their U.S. rental, they constructed a hypothetical rental figure for the Cinerama films to provide a basis for comparison to other films in their chart: in the case of
         <i>
          This Is Cinerama
         </i>
         , the $50 million worldwide gross was reconfigured as a $12.5 million U.S. rental gross; this is exactly 25% of the amount reported by Cinerama, so
         <i>
          Variety'
         </i>
         s formula seemingly halved the gross to obtain an estimate for the U.S. share, and halved it again to simulate a rental fee.
         <i>
          Variety
         </i>
         <span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">
          '
         </span>
         s 'rental' amounts are often repeated, but have no basis in the reality of what the films actually earned—they are hypothetical figures conceived for comparative analysis.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-355">
          <a href="#cite_note-355">
           [55]
          </a>
         </sup>
         All five Cinerama features collectively generated $120 million in worldwide box office receipts.
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-356">
          <a href="#cite_note-356">
           [56]
          </a>
         </sup>
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Greatest_Show">
         <sup>
          GS
         </sup>
         <i>
          Variety
         </i>
         put the worldwide rental for
         <i>
          The Greatest Show on Earth
         </i>
         at around $18.35 million (with $12.8 million coming from the United States
         <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Finler358_347-1">
          <a href="#cite_note-Finler358-347">
           [47]
          </a>
         </sup>
         ) a year after its release; however, Birchard puts its earnings at just over $15 million up to 1962. It is likely that Birchard's figure is just the North American gross rental, and includes revenue from the 1954 and 1960 reissues.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Star_Wars">
         <sup>
          SW
         </sup>
         The "first run"
         <i>
          Star Wars
         </i>
         grosses do not include revenue from the 1997 special-edition releases; however, the figure does include revenue from the re-releases prior to the special editions.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_HP_budget">
         <sup>
          HP
         </sup>
         Production costs were shared with
         <i>
          Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows – Part 1
         </i>
         .
        </span>
       </p>
      </div>
      <h2>
       <span class="mw-headline" id="Timeline_of_highest-grossing_films">
        Timeline of highest-grossing films
       </span>
      </h2>
      <div class="thumb tright">
       <div class="thumbinner" style="width:172px;">
        <a class="image" href="/wiki/File:Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg">
         <img alt="The theatrical poster for The Birth of a Nation depicting a hooded man carrying a burning cross on horse back." class="thumbimage" data-file-height="1482" data-file-width="961" decoding="async" height="262" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg/170px-Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg/255px-Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg/340px-Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg 2x" width="170"/>
        </a>
        <div class="thumbcaption">
         <div class="magnify">
          <a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg" title="Enlarge">
          </a>
         </div>
         <i>
          The Birth of a Nation
         </i>
         pioneered many of the techniques used in filmmaking today, becoming the most successful film ever made at the time of its release.
        </div>
       </div>
      </div>
      <p>
       At least eleven films have held the record of 'highest-grossing film' since
       <i>
        The Birth of a Nation
       </i>
       assumed the top spot in 1915. Both
       <i>
        The Birth of a Nation
       </i>
       and
       <i>
        Gone with the Wind
       </i>
       spent twenty-five consecutive years apiece as the highest-grosser, with films directed by Steven Spielberg holding the record on three occasions and James Cameron twice. Spielberg became the first director to break his own record when
       <i>
        Jurassic Park
       </i>
       overtook
       <i>
        E.T.
       </i>
       , and Cameron emulated the feat when
       <i>
        Avatar
       </i>
       broke the record set by
       <i>
        Titanic
       </i>
       . When it took over the top spot in 2019,
       <i>
        Avengers: Endgame
       </i>
       became the first sequel to hold the record of highest-grossing film, and in doing so ended thirty-six years of Spielberg/Cameron dominance.
      </p>
      <p>
       Some sources claim that
       <i>
        The Big Parade
       </i>
       superseded
       <i>
        The Birth of a Nation
       </i>
       as highest-grossing film, eventually being replaced by
       <i>
        Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs
       </i>
       , which in turn was quickly usurped by
       <i>
        Gone with the Wind
       </i>
       .
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Dirks_(2010)_357-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Dirks_(2010)-357">
         [57]
        </a>
       </sup>
       Exact figures are not known for
       <i>
        The Birth of a Nation
       </i>
       , but contemporary records put its worldwide earnings at $5.2 million as of 1919.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Wasko_358-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Wasko-358">
         [58]
        </a>
       </sup>
       Its international release was delayed by World War I, and it was not released in many foreign territories until the 1920s; coupled with further re-releases in the United States, its $10 million earnings as reported by
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Variety_(magazine)" title="Variety (magazine)">
         Variety
        </a>
       </i>
       in 1932 are consistent with the earlier figure.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Variety_(1932)_359-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Variety_(1932)-359">
         [59]
        </a>
       </sup>
       At this time,
       <i>
        Variety
       </i>
       still had
       <i>
        The Birth of a Nation
       </i>
       ahead of
       <i>
        The Big Parade
       </i>
       ($6,400,000) on distributor rentals and—if its estimate is correct—
       <i>
        Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs
       </i>
       ($8,500,000)
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-360">
        <a href="#cite_note-360">
         [60]
        </a>
       </sup>
       would not have earned enough on its first theatrical run to take the record;
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-361">
        <a href="#cite_note-361">
         [61]
        </a>
       </sup>
       although it would have been the highest-grossing 'talkie',
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-362">
        <a href="#cite_note-362">
         [62]
        </a>
       </sup>
       displacing
       <i>
        The Singing Fool
       </i>
       ($5,900,000).
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-363">
        <a href="#cite_note-363">
         [63]
        </a>
       </sup>
       Although received wisdom holds that it is unlikely
       <i>
        The Birth of a Nation
       </i>
       was ever overtaken by a silent-era film,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-364">
        <a href="#cite_note-364">
         [64]
        </a>
       </sup>
       the record would fall to 1925's
       <i>
        Ben-Hur
       </i>
       ($9,386,000) if
       <i>
        The Birth of a Nation
       </i>
       earned significantly less than its estimated gross.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HallNeale163_365-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-HallNeale163-365">
         [65]
        </a>
       </sup>
       In addition to its gross rental earnings through public exhibition,
       <i>
        The Birth of a Nation
       </i>
       played at a large number of private, club and organizational engagements which figures are unavailable for.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-du_Brow_366-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-du_Brow-366">
         [66]
        </a>
       </sup>
       It was hugely popular with the
       <a href="/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan" title="Ku Klux Klan">
        Ku Klux Klan
       </a>
       who used it to drive recruitment,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-367">
        <a href="#cite_note-367">
         [67]
        </a>
       </sup>
       and at one point
       <i>
        Variety
       </i>
       estimated its total earnings to stand at around $50 million.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-368">
        <a href="#cite_note-368">
         [68]
        </a>
       </sup>
       Despite later retracting the claim, the sum has been widely reported even though it has never been substantiated.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Wasko_358-1">
        <a href="#cite_note-Wasko-358">
         [58]
        </a>
       </sup>
       While it is generally accepted that
       <i>
        Gone with the Wind
       </i>
       took over the record of highest-grossing film on its initial release—which is true in terms of public exhibition—it is likely it did not overtake
       <i>
        The Birth of a Nation
       </i>
       in total revenue until a much later date, with it still being reported as the highest earner up until the 1960s.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-du_Brow_366-1">
        <a href="#cite_note-du_Brow-366">
         [66]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <i>
        Gone with the Wind
       </i>
       itself may have been briefly overtaken by
       <i>
        The Ten Commandments
       </i>
       (1956), which closed at the end of 1960 with worldwide rentals of $58–60 million
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-369">
        <a href="#cite_note-369">
         [69]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HallNeale160_370-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-HallNeale160-370">
         [70]
        </a>
       </sup>
       compared to
       <i>
        Gone with the Wind
       </i>
       <span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">
        '
       </span>
       s $59 million;
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Oviatt_(1961)_371-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Oviatt_(1961)-371">
         [71]
        </a>
       </sup>
       if it did claim the top spot its tenure there was short-lived, since
       <i>
        Gone with the Wind
       </i>
       was re-released the following year and increased its earnings to $67 million. Depending on how accurate the estimates are, the 1959 remake of
       <i>
        Ben-Hur
       </i>
       may also have captured the record from
       <i>
        Gone with the Wind
       </i>
       : as of the end of 1961 it had earned $47 million worldwide,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-372">
        <a href="#cite_note-372">
         [72]
        </a>
       </sup>
       and by 1963 it was trailing
       <i>
        Gone with the Wind
       </i>
       by just $2 million with international takings of $65 million,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Thomas_(1963)_373-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Thomas_(1963)-373">
         [73]
        </a>
       </sup>
       ultimately earning $66 million from its initial release.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-374">
        <a href="#cite_note-374">
         [74]
        </a>
       </sup>
      </p>
      <div class="thumb tleft">
       <div class="thumbinner" style="width:172px;">
        <a class="image" href="/wiki/File:Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg">
         <img alt="" class="thumbimage" data-file-height="2388" data-file-width="1824" decoding="async" height="223" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg/170px-Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg/255px-Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg/340px-Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg 2x" width="170"/>
        </a>
        <div class="thumbcaption">
         <div class="magnify">
          <a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg" title="Enlarge">
          </a>
         </div>
         The 1972 pornographic film
         <i>
          Deep Throat
         </i>
         reportedly earned as much as $600 million, a figure that may have been inflated by gangsters in money laundering schemes.
        </div>
       </div>
      </div>
      <p>
       Another film purported to have been the highest-grosser is the 1972
       <a href="/wiki/Pornographic_film" title="Pornographic film">
        pornographic film
       </a>
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Deep_Throat_(film)" title="Deep Throat (film)">
         Deep Throat
        </a>
       </i>
       . In 1984,
       <a href="/wiki/Linda_Lovelace" title="Linda Lovelace">
        Linda Lovelace
       </a>
       testified to a United States Senate Judiciary Subcommittee on juvenile justice that the film had earned $600 million;
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-375">
        <a href="#cite_note-375">
         [75]
        </a>
       </sup>
       this figure has been the subject of much speculation, since if it is accurate then the film would have made more money than
       <i>
        Star Wars
       </i>
       , and finished the 1970s as the highest-grossing film. The main argument against this figure is that it simply did not have a wide enough release to sustain the sort of sums that would be required for it to ultimately gross this amount.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-376">
        <a href="#cite_note-376">
         [76]
        </a>
       </sup>
       Exact figures are not known, but testimony in a federal trial in 1976—about four years into the film's release—showed the film had grossed over $25 million.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-377">
        <a href="#cite_note-377">
         [77]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <a href="/wiki/Roger_Ebert" title="Roger Ebert">
        Roger Ebert
       </a>
       has reasoned it possibly did earn as much as $600 million on paper, since mobsters owned most of the
       <a href="/wiki/Adult_movie_theater" title="Adult movie theater">
        adult movie theaters
       </a>
       during this period and would
       <a href="/wiki/Money_laundering" title="Money laundering">
        launder
       </a>
       income from drugs and prostitution through them, so probably inflated the box office receipts for the film.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-378">
        <a href="#cite_note-378">
         [78]
        </a>
       </sup>
      </p>
      <p>
       <i>
        The Birth of a Nation
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        Gone with the Wind
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        The Godfather
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        Jaws
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        Star Wars
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        E.T.
       </i>
       and
       <i>
        Avatar
       </i>
       all increased their record grosses with re-releases. The grosses from their original theatrical runs are included here along with totals from re-releases up to the point that they lost the record; therefore the total for
       <i>
        The Birth of a Nation
       </i>
       includes income from its reissues up to 1940; the total for
       <i>
        Star Wars
       </i>
       includes revenue from the late 1970s and early 1980s reissues but not from the 1997 Special Edition; the total for
       <i>
        E.T.
       </i>
       incorporates its gross from the 1985 reissue but not from 2002; the total for
       <i>
        Avatar
       </i>
       includes revenue from the 2010 Special Edition, which represents all of its earnings up to the point it relinquished the record.
       <i>
        Gone with the Wind
       </i>
       is represented twice on the chart: the 1940 entry includes earnings from its staggered 1939–1942 release (
       <a href="/wiki/Roadshow_theatrical_release" title="Roadshow theatrical release">
        roadshow
       </a>
       /
       <a href="/wiki/Wide_release" title="Wide release">
        general release
       </a>
       /
       <a href="/wiki/Discount_theater" title="Discount theater">
        second-run
       </a>
       )
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Bartel_379-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Bartel-379">
         [79]
        </a>
       </sup>
       along with all of its revenue up to the 1961 reissue prior to losing the record to
       <i>
        The Sound of Music
       </i>
       in 1966; its 1971 entry—after it took back the record—includes income from the 1967 and 1971 reissues but omitting later releases.
       <i>
        The Godfather
       </i>
       was re-released in 1973 after its success at the
       <a href="/wiki/45th_Academy_Awards" title="45th Academy Awards">
        45th Academy Awards
       </a>
       , and
       <i>
        Jaws
       </i>
       was released again in 1976, and their grosses here most likely include earnings from those releases.
       <i>
        The Sound of Music
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        The Godfather
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        Jaws
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        Jurassic Park
       </i>
       and
       <i>
        Titanic
       </i>
       increased their earnings with further releases in 1973, 1997, 1979, 2013 and 2012 respectively, but they are not included in the totals here because they had already conceded the record prior to being re-released.
      </p>
      <table class="wikitable plainrowheaders" style="margin:auto; margin:auto;">
       <caption>
        Timeline of the highest-grossing film record
       </caption>
       <tbody>
        <tr>
         <th scope="col">

         </th>
         <th scope="col">

         </th>
         <th scope="col">

         </th>

         </th>
        </tr>

          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Dirks_(2010)_357-1">

          </sup>
         </th>
         <td rowspan="2">

          <sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Rentals2">

           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
         <td style="text-align:center;">
          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Wasko_126-1">

           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>

          <sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Rentals2">
           <a href="#endnote_Rentals2">
            R
           </a>
          </sup>
          <sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Reissue">
           <a href="#endnote_Reissue">
            ‡
           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
         <td style="text-align:center;">
          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Time_(1940)_380-0">
           <a href="#cite_note-Time_(1940)-380">
            [# 301]
           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
         <th scope="row">

          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-gwtw_tcm_108-1">
           <a href="#cite_note-gwtw_tcm-108">
            [29]
           </a>
          </sup>
         </th>
         <td rowspan="2">
          <i>
           <a href="/wiki/Gone_with_the_Wind_(film)" title="Gone with the Wind (film)">
            Gone with the Wind
           </a>
          </i>
         </td>
         <td style="text-align:right;">
          $32,000,000
          <sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Rentals2">
           <a href="#endnote_Rentals2">
            R
           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
         <td style="text-align:center;">
          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-gwtw_tcm_176-1">
           <a href="#cite_note-gwtw_tcm-176">
            [# 131]
           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
         <th scope="row">
          <i>
           1963
          </i>
         </th>
         <td style="text-align:right;">
          $67,000,000
          <sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Rentals2">
           <a href="#endnote_Rentals2">
            R
           </a>
          </sup>
          <sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Reissue">
           <a href="#endnote_Reissue">
            ‡
           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
         <td style="text-align:center;">
          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Thomas_(1963)_381-0">
           <a href="#cite_note-Thomas_(1963)-381">
            [# 302]
           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
         <th scope="row">
          1966
          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Dirks_(2010)_357-2">
           <a href="#cite_note-Dirks_(2010)-357">
            [57]
           </a>
          </sup>
         </th>
         <td>
          <i>
           <a href="/wiki/The_Sound_of_Music_(film)" title="The Sound of Music (film)">
            The Sound of Music
           </a>
          </i>
         </td>
         <td style="text-align:right;">
          $114,600,000
          <sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Rentals2">
           <a href="#endnote_Rentals2">
            R
           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
         <td style="text-align:center;">
          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-BlockWilson434_251-6">

           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
         <th scope="row">
          1971
          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Dirks_(2010)_357-3">

          <sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Rentals2">

          <sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Reissue">

         <th scope="row">

          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-titanic_18-2">
           <a href="#cite_note-titanic-18">
            [# 5]
           </a>

           <a href="#cite_note-Cieply_(2010)-392">
            [86]

          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Segers_(2010)_393-0">

        <tr>
         <td style="text-align:right;">
          $2,789,679,794
          <sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Reissue">

            ‡
           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
         <th scope="row">
          2019
          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-394">
           <a href="#cite_note-394">
            [88]
           </a>

          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-395">
           <a href="#cite_note-395">
            [89]
           </a>
          </sup>
         </th>
         <td>
          <i>
           <a href="/wiki/Avengers:_Endgame" title="Avengers: Endgame">
            Avengers: Endgame
           </a>

         </td>
         <td style="text-align:center;">
          <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-endgame_14-2">

            [# 1]
           </a>
          </sup>
         </td>
        </tr>
       </tbody>
      </table>
      <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1011217839" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
      <div class="refbegin" style="">
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Rentals2">
         <sup>
          R
         </sup>
         Distributor rental.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Reissue">
         <sup>
          <img alt="Includes re-releases" data-file-height="14" data-file-width="9" decoding="async" height="14" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/f9/Double-dagger-14-plain.png" width="9"/>
         </sup>
         Includes revenue from re-releases. If a film increased its gross through re-releases while holding the record, the year in which it recorded its highest gross is also noted in italics.
        </span>
       </p>
      </div>
      <h2>
       <span class="mw-headline" id="Highest-grossing_franchises_and_film_series">
        Highest-grossing franchises and film series
       </span>
      </h2>
      <div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">
       See also:
       <a href="/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_media_franchises" title="List of highest-grossing media franchises">
        List of highest-grossing media franchises
       </a>
      </div>
      <p>
       Prior to 2000, only seven
       <a href="/wiki/Film_series" title="Film series">
        film series
       </a>
       had grossed over $1 billion at the box office:
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/List_of_James_Bond_films" title="List of James Bond films">
         James Bond
        </a>
       </i>
       ,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-396">
        <a href="#cite_note-396">
         [90]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Star_Wars" title="Star Wars">
         Star Wars
        </a>
       </i>
       ,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-397">
        <a href="#cite_note-397">
         [91]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <i>
        <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Indiana_Jones_(franchise)" title="Indiana Jones (franchise)">
         Indiana Jones
        </a>
       </i>
       ,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-398">
        <a href="#cite_note-398">
         [92]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <i>
        <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Rocky_(film_series)" title="Rocky (film series)">
         Rocky
        </a>
       </i>
       ,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-399">
        <a href="#cite_note-399">
         [93]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-400">
        <a href="#cite_note-400">
         [94]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-401">
        <a href="#cite_note-401">
         [95]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Batman_in_film" title="Batman in film">
         Batman
        </a>
       </i>
       ,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-402">
        <a href="#cite_note-402">
         [96]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Jurassic_Park" title="Jurassic Park">
         Jurassic Park
        </a>
       </i>
       ,
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-403">
        <a href="#cite_note-403">
         [97]
        </a>
       </sup>
       and
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/List_of_Star_Trek_films_and_television_series" title="List of Star Trek films and television series">
         Star Trek
        </a>
       </i>
       .
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-404">
        <a href="#cite_note-404">
         [98]
        </a>
       </sup>
       Since the turn of the century that number has increased to over fifty (not including one-off hits such as
       <i>
        Avatar
       </i>
       ,
       <i>
        Titanic
       </i>
       , and
       <i>
        Zootopia
       </i>
       ).
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Numbers_franchises_405-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Numbers_franchises-405">
         [99]
        </a>
       </sup>
       This is partly due to inflation and market growth, but also to Hollywood's adoption of the
       <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Media_franchise" title="Media franchise">
        franchise
       </a>
       model: films that have built-in brand recognition, such as being based on a well-known literary source or an established character. The methodology is based on the concept that films associated with things audiences are already familiar with can be more effectively marketed to them, and as such are known as "pre-sold" films within the industry.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-economist_406-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-economist-406">
         [100]
        </a>
       </sup>
      </p>
      <p>
       A franchise is typically defined to be at least two works derived from a common
       <a href="/wiki/Intellectual_property" title="Intellectual property">
        intellectual property
       </a>
       . Traditionally, the work has a tautological relationship with the property, but this is not a prerequisite. An enduring staple of the franchise model is the concept of the
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Crossover_(fiction)" title="Crossover (fiction)">
         crossover
        </a>
       </i>
       , which can be defined as "a story in which characters or concepts from two or more discrete texts or series of texts meet".
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-407">
        <a href="#cite_note-407">
         [101]
        </a>
       </sup>
       A consequence of a crossover is that an intellectual property may be utilized by more than one franchise. For example,
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Batman_v_Superman:_Dawn_of_Justice" title="Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice">
         Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice
        </a>
       </i>
       belongs to not only the
       <a href="/wiki/Batman" title="Batman">
        Batman
       </a>
       and
       <a href="/wiki/Superman" title="Superman">
        Superman
       </a>
       franchises, but also to the
       <a href="/wiki/DC_Extended_Universe" title="DC Extended Universe">
        DC Extended Universe
       </a>
       , which is a
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Shared_universe" title="Shared universe">
         shared universe
        </a>
       </i>
       . A shared universe is a particular type of crossover where a number of characters from a wide range of fictional works wind up sharing a fictional world.
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-408">
        <a href="#cite_note-408">
         [102]
        </a>
       </sup>
       The most successful shared universe in the medium of film is the
       <a href="/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe" title="Marvel Cinematic Universe">
        Marvel Cinematic Universe
       </a>
       , a crossover between multiple superhero properties owned by
       <a href="/wiki/Marvel_Comics" title="Marvel Comics">
        Marvel Comics
       </a>
       . The Marvel Cinematic Universe is also the highest-grossing franchise, amassing over $22 billion at the box office.
      </p>
      <p>
       The
       <i>
        Star Wars
       </i>
       films are the highest-grossing series based on a single property, earning over $10 billion at the box office (although the
       <a href="/wiki/Eon_Productions" title="Eon Productions">
        Eon
       </a>
       <i>
        James Bond
       </i>
       films have earned over $18 billion in total when adjusted to current prices).
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-410">
        <a href="#cite_note-410">
         [104]
        </a>
       </sup>
       If ancillary income from merchandise is included, then
       <i>
        Star Wars
       </i>
       is the most lucrative property;
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Harrod_411-0">
        <a href="#cite_note-Harrod-411">
         [105]
        </a>
       </sup>
       it holds the
       <i>
        Guinness
       </i>
       world record for the "most successful film merchandising franchise" and was valued at £19.51 billion in 2012 (approximately $30 billion).
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-412">
        <a href="#cite_note-412">
         [106]
        </a>
       </sup>
       <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-413">
        <a href="#cite_note-413">
         [107]
        </a>
       </sup>
       The Marvel Cinematic Universe has had the most films gross over $1 billion with nine. The four
       <i>
        <a href="/wiki/Avengers_(comics)_in_other_media#Live_action" title="Avengers (comics) in other media">
         Avengers
        </a>
       </i>
       films and the two
       <a href="/wiki/Frozen_(franchise)" title="Frozen (franchise)">
        <i>
         Frozen
        </i>
       </a>
       films are the only franchises where each installment has grossed over $1 billion. Along with
       <a href="/wiki/The_Lion_King_(franchise)" title="The Lion King (franchise)">
        <i>
         The Lion King
        </i>
       </a>
       , these are also the only franchises to have a series average of over $1 billion per film.
      </p>
      <dl>
       <dd>
        <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r981673959" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
        <div class="legend">
         <span class="legend-color" style="background-color:#b6fcb6; color:black;">
          <span class="legend-text">
           <img alt="film currently playing" data-file-height="14" data-file-width="9" decoding="async" height="14" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/37/Dagger-14-plain.png" width="9"/>
          </span>
         </span>
         <i>
          Background shading indicates that at least one film in the series is playing in the week commencing 5 March 2021 in theaters around the world
         </i>
         .
        </div>
       </dd>
      </dl>

        </sup>

       </caption>

      <div class="refbegin" style="">
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Shared">
         <sup>
          S
         </sup>
         Shared universes for which some properties also have their own entries.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_USgross3">
         <sup>
          *
         </sup>
         Canada and U.S. gross only.
        </span>
       </p>
       <p>
        <span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Rentals3">
         <sup>
          R
         </sup>
         Distributor rental.
        </span>
       </p>

           <cite class="citation journal cs1" id="CITEREFPincus-Roth2006">

           </span>
          </span>
         </li>
         <li id="cite_note-3">
          <span class="mw-cite-backlink">
           <b>
            <a href="#cite_ref-3">
             ^
            </a>
           </b>
          </span>
          <span class="reference-text">
           <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r999302996" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
           <cite class="citation news cs1">
            <a class="external text" href="http://www.economist.com/node/18386456" rel="nofollow">
             "Unkind unwind"
            </a>
            .
            <i>
             <a href="/wiki/The_Economist" title="The Economist">
              The Economist
             </a>
            </i>
            . March 17, 2011
            <span class="reference-accessdate">
             . Retrieved
             <span class="nowrap">
              April 12,
             </span>
             2012
            </span>
            .
           </cite>
           <span class="Z3988" title="ctx_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=article&amp;rft.jtitle=The+Economist&amp;rft.atitle=Unkind+unwind&amp;rft.date=2011-03-17&amp;rft_id=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.economist.com%2Fnode%2F18386456&amp;rfr_id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AList+of+highest-grossing+films">
           </span>
          </span>
         </li>
         <li id="cite_note-4">
          <span class="mw-cite-backlink">
           <b>
            <a href="#cite_ref-4">
             ^
            </a>
           </b>
          </span>
          <span class="reference-text">
           <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r999302996" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
           <cite class="citation book cs1" id="CITEREFVogel2010">
            Vogel, Harold L. (2010).
            <i>
             Entertainment Industry Economics: A Guide for Financial Analysis
            </i>
            .
            <a href="/wiki/Cambridge_University_Press" title="Cambridge University Press">
             Cambridge University Press
            </a>
            . p.
            <a class="external text" href="https://books.google.co.uk/books?id=BfyFFCyRvX0C&amp;pg=PA224" rel="nofollow">
             224
            </a>
            .
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" title="ISBN (identifier)">
             ISBN
            </a>
            <a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-107-00309-5" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-107-00309-5">
             <bdi>
              978-1-107-00309-5
             </bdi>
            </a>
            .
            <q>
             Most pictures would likely receive 20% to 25% of theatrical box office gross for two prime-time network runs.
            </q>
           </cite>
           <span class="Z3988" title="ctx_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=Entertainment+Industry+Economics%3A+A+Guide+for+Financial+Analysis&amp;rft.pages=224&amp;rft.pub=Cambridge+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2010&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-107-00309-5&amp;rft.aulast=Vogel&amp;rft.aufirst=Harold+L.&amp;rfr_id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AList+of+highest-grossing+films">
           </span>
          </span>
         </li>
         <li id="cite_note-5">
          <span class="mw-cite-backlink">
           <b>
            <a href="#cite_ref-5">
             ^
            </a>
           </b>
          </span>
          <span class="reference-text">
           <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r999302996" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
           <cite class="citation news cs1" id="CITEREFClark2001">
            Clark, Emma (November 12, 2001).

          <span class="reference-text">
           <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r999302996" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>

           <span class="Z3988" title="ctx_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=%D0%93%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%BD%D0%B5%D1%81%D1%81.+%D0%9C%D0%B8%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%8B%D0%B5+%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B4%D1%8B&amp;rft.place=Moscow&amp;rft.pages=211&amp;rft.edition=2012&amp;rft.pub=Astrel&amp;rft.date=2011&amp;rft.isbn=978-5-271-36423-5&amp;rfr_id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AList+of+highest-grossing+films">

         <li id="cite_note-114">

           <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r999302996" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
           <cite class="citation web cs1">
            <a class="external text" href="https://www.boxofficemojo.com/movies/?page=intl&amp;id=titanic3d.htm" rel="nofollow">

            .

              Box Office Mojo

            </span>
            .
            <q>
             North America: $57,884,114; Overseas: $285,666,656
            </q>
           </cite>
           <span class="Z3988" title="ctx_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=unknown&amp;rft.jtitle=Box+Office+Mojo&amp;rft.atitle=Titanic+3D+%282012%29+%E2%80%93+International+Box+Office+results&amp;rft_id=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.boxofficemojo.com%2Fmovies%2F%3Fpage%3Dintl%26id%3Dtitanic3d.htm&amp;rfr_id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AList+of+highest-grossing+films">
           </span>
          </span>
         </li>
         <li id="cite_note-115">
          <span class="mw-cite-backlink">
           <b>
            <a href="#cite_ref-115">
             ^
            </a>
           </b>
          </span>
          <span class="reference-text">
           <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r999302996" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
           <cite class="citation web cs1">
            <a class="external text" href="https://www.boxofficemojo.com/movies/?id=titanic20thanniversary.htm" rel="nofollow">
             "Titanic (20th Anniversary)"
            </a>
            .
            <i>
             <a href="/wiki/Box_Office_Mojo" title="Box Office Mojo">
              Box Office Mojo
             </a>
            </i>
            <span class="reference-accessdate">
             . Retrieved
             <span class="nowrap">
              July 1,
             </span>
             2019
            </span>
            .
            <q>
             Domestic Total Gross: $691,642
            </q>
           </cite>
           <span class="Z3988" title="ctx_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=unknown&amp;rft.jtitle=Box+Office+Mojo&amp;rft.atitle=Titanic+%2820th+Anniversary%29&amp;rft_id=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.boxofficemojo.com%2Fmovies%2F%3Fid%3Dtitanic20thanniversary.htm&amp;rfr_id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AList+of+highest-grossing+films">
           </span>
          </span>
         </li>
         <li id="cite_note-Cones-116">
          <span class="mw-cite-backlink">
           ^
           <a href="#cite_ref-Cones_116-0">
            <sup>
             <i>
              <b>
               a
              </b>
             </i>
            </sup>
           </a>
           <a href="#cite_ref-Cones_116-1">
            <sup>
             <i>
              <b>
               b
              </b>
             </i>
            </sup>
           </a>
          </span>
          <span class="reference-text">
           <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r999302996" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
           <cite class="citation book cs1" id="CITEREFCones1997">
            Cones, John W. (1997).
            <i>
             The feature film distribution deal: a critical analysis of the single most important film industry agreement
            </i>
            .
            <a href="/wiki/Southern_Illinois_University_Press" title="Southern Illinois University Press">
             Southern Illinois University Press
            </a>
            . p.
            <a class="external text" href="https://books.google.co.uk/books?id=EoeJ7VmwnDIC&amp;pg=PA41" rel="nofollow">
             41
            </a>
            .
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" title="ISBN (identifier)">
             ISBN
            </a>
            <a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-8093-2082-0" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-8093-2082-0">
             <bdi>
              978-0-8093-2082-0
             </bdi>
            </a>
            .
            <q>
             Distributor rentals: It is also important to know and recognize the difference between the distributor's gross receipts and the gross rentals. The term "rentals" refers to the aggregate amount of the film distributor's share of monies paid at theatre box offices computed on the basis of negotiated agreements between the distributor and the exhibitor. Note that gross receipts refers to amounts actually received and from all markets and media, whereas gross rentals refers to amounts earned from theatrical exhibition only, regardless of whether received by the distributor. Thus, gross receipts is the much broader term and includes distributor rentals. The issue of film rentals (i.e., what percentage of a film's box office gross comes back to the distributor) is of key importance...More current numbers suggest that distributor rentals for the major studio/distributor released films average in the neighborhood of 43% of box office gross. Again, however, such an average is based on widely divergent distributor rental ratios on individual films.
            </q>
           </cite>
           <span class="Z3988" title="ctx_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=The+feature+film+distribution+deal%3A+a+critical+analysis+of+the+single+most+important+film+industry+agreement&amp;rft.pages=41&amp;rft.pub=Southern+Illinois+University+Press&amp;rft.date=1997&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-8093-2082-0&amp;rft.aulast=Cones&amp;rft.aufirst=John+W.&amp;rfr_id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AList+of+highest-grossing+films">
           </span>
          </span>
         </li>
         <li id="cite_note-Marich-117">
          <span class="mw-cite-backlink">
           <b>
            <a href="#cite_ref-Marich_117-0">
             ^
            </a>
           </b>
          </span>
          <span class="reference-text">
           <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r999302996" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
           <cite class="citation book cs1" id="CITEREFMarich2009">
            Marich, Robert (2009) [1st. pub.
            <a href="/wiki/Focal_Press" title="Focal Press">
             Focal Press
            </a>
            :2005].
            <i>
             Marketing to moviegoers: a handbook of strategies used by major studios and independents
            </i>
            (2 ed.).
            <a href="/wiki/Southern_Illinois_University_Press" title="Southern Illinois University Press">
             Southern Illinois University Press
            </a>
            . p.
            <a class="external text" href="https://books.google.co.uk/books?id=_6USFaYxyeUC&amp;pg=PA252" rel="nofollow">
             252
            </a>
            .
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" title="ISBN (identifier)">
             ISBN
            </a>
            <a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-8093-2884-0" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-8093-2884-0">
             <bdi>
              978-0-8093-2884-0
             </bdi>
            </a>
            .
            <q>
             Rentals are the distributors' share of the box office gross and typically set by a complex, two-part contract.
            </q>
           </cite>
           <span class="Z3988" title="ctx_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=Marketing+to+moviegoers%3A+a+handbook+of+strategies+used+by+major+studios+and+independents&amp;rft.pages=252&amp;rft.edition=2&amp;rft.pub=Southern+Illinois+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2009&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-8093-2884-0&amp;rft.aulast=Marich&amp;rft.aufirst=Robert&amp;rfr_id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AList+of+highest-grossing+films">
           </span>
          </span>
         </li>
         <li id="cite_note-Balio-118">
          <span class="mw-cite-backlink">
           <b>
            <a href="#cite_ref-Balio_118-0">
             ^
            </a>
           </b>
          </span>
          <span class="reference-text">
           <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r999302996" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
           <cite class="citation book cs1" id="CITEREFBalio2005">
            Balio, Tino (2005).
            <i>
             The American film industry
            </i>
            .
            <a href="/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin_Press" title="University of Wisconsin Press">
             University of Wisconsin Press
            </a>
            . p.
            <a class="external text" href="https://books.google.co.uk/books?id=OBpDf_uUp30C&amp;pg=pa296" rel="nofollow">
             296
            </a>
            .
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" title="ISBN (identifier)">
             ISBN
            </a>
            <a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-299-09874-2" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-299-09874-2">
             <bdi>
              978-0-299-09874-2
             </bdi>
            </a>
            .
            <q>
             Film Rentals as Percent of Volume of Business (1939): 36.4
            </q>
           </cite>
           <span class="Z3988" title="ctx_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=The+American+film+industry&amp;rft.pages=296&amp;rft.pub=University+of+Wisconsin+Press&amp;rft.date=2005&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-299-09874-2&amp;rft.aulast=Balio&amp;rft.aufirst=Tino&amp;rfr_id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AList+of+highest-grossing+films">
           </span>
          </span>
         </li>
         <li id="cite_note-119">
          <span class="mw-cite-backlink">
           <b>
            <a href="#cite_ref-119">
             ^
            </a>
           </b>
          </span>
          <span class="reference-text">
           <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r999302996" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
           <cite class="citation book cs1" id="CITEREFBalio1987">
            Balio, Tino (1987).
            <a class="external text" href="https://archive.org/details/unitedartistscom00bali/page/124" rel="nofollow">
             <i>
              United Artists: the Company that Changed the Film Industry
             </i>
            </a>
            .
            <a href="/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin_Press" title="University of Wisconsin Press">
             University of Wisconsin Press
            </a>
            . p.
            <a class="external text" href="https://books.google.co.uk/books?id=9EeK5s3aw44C&amp;pg=PA124#v=twopage" rel="nofollow">
             124–125
            </a>
            .
            <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" title="ISBN (identifier)">
             ISBN
            </a>
            <a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-299-11440-4" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-299-11440-4">
             <bdi>
              978-0-299-11440-4
             </bdi>
            </a>
            .
            <q>
             To rekindle interest in the movies, Hollywood not only had to compete with television but also with other leisure-time activities...Movies made a comeback by 1955, but audiences had changed. Moviegoing became a special event for most people, creating the phenomenon of the big picture.
            </q>
           </cite>
           <span class="Z3988" title="ctx_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=United+Artists%3A+the+Company+that+Changed+the+Film+Industry&amp;rft.pages=124-125&amp;rft.pub=University+of+Wisconsin+Press&amp;rft.date=1987&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-299-11440-4&amp;rft.aulast=Balio&amp;rft.aufirst=Tino&amp;rft_id=https%3A%2F%2Farchive.org%2Fdetails%2Funitedartistscom00bali%2Fpage%2F124&amp;rfr_id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AList+of+highest-grossing+films">
           </span>
          </span>
         </li>
         <li id="cite_note-HallNeale179-120">
          <span class="mw-cite-backlink">
           <b>
            <a href="#cite_ref-HallNeale179_120-0">
             ^
            </a>
           </b>
          </span>
          <span class="reference-text">
           <a href="#CITEREFHallNeale2010">
            Hall &amp; Neale 2010
           </a>
           , p.
           <a class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=jhvrSwOOsRgC&amp;pg=PA179" rel="nofollow">
            179
           </a>
           . "Later epics proved far more disastrous for the backers. Samuel Bronston's
           <i>
            The Fall of the Roman Empire
           </i>
           , filmed in Spain, cost $17,816,876 and grossed only $1.9 million in America. George Stevens's long-gestating life of Christ,
           <i>
            The Greatest Story Ever Told
           </i>
           (1965), which had been in planning since 1954 and in production since 1962, earned domestic rentals of $6,962,715 on a $21,481,745 negative cost, the largest amount yet spent on a production made entirely within the United States.
           <i>
            The Bible—in the Beginning
           </i>
           ... (1966) was financed by the Italian producer Dino De Laurentiis from private investors and Swiss banks. He then sold distribution rights outside Italy jointly to Fox and Seven Arts for $15 million (70 percent of which came from Fox), thereby recouping the bulk of his $18 million investment. Although
           <i>
            The Bible
           </i>
           returned a respectable world rental of $25.3 million, Fox was still left with a net loss of just over $1.5 million. It was the last biblical epic to be released by any major Hollywood studio for nearly twenty years."
          </span>
         </li>
         <li id="cite_note-121">
          <span class="mw-cite-backlink">
           <b>
            <a href="#cite_ref-121">
             ^
            </a>
           </b>
          </span>
          <span class="reference-text">
           <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r999302996" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
           <cite class="citation web cs1" id="CITEREFWilliams2015">
            Williams, Trey (September 25, 2015).
            <a class="external text" href="http://www.marketwatch.com/story/ridley-scotts-latest-alien-announcement-drives-hollywoods-sequel-problem-2015-09-25" rel="nofollow">
             "Ridley Scott's latest 'Alien' announcement drives Hollywood's sequel problem"
            </a>
            .
            <i>
             <a href="/wiki/MarketWatch" title="MarketWatch">
              MarketWatch
             </a>
            </i>
            <span class="reference-accessdate">
             . Retrieved
             <span class="nowrap">
              May 12,
             </span>
             2016
            </span>
            .
           </cite>
           <span class="Z3988" title="ctx_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=unknown&amp;rft.jtitle=MarketWatch&amp;rft.atitle=Ridley+Scott%27s+latest+%27Alien%27+announcement+drives+Hollywood%27s+sequel+problem&amp;rft.date=2015-09-25&amp;rft.aulast=Williams&amp;rft.aufirst=Trey&amp;rft_id=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.marketwatch.com%2Fstory%2Fridley-scotts-latest-alien-announcement-drives-hollywoods-sequel-problem-2015-09-25&amp;rfr_id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AList+of+highest-grossing+films">
           </span>
          </span>
         </li>
         <li id="cite_note-122">
          <span class="mw-cite-backlink">
           <b>
            <a href="#cite_ref-122">
             ^
            </a>
           </b>
          </span>
          <span class="reference-text">
           <link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r999302996" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/>
           <cite class="citation web cs1">
            <a class="external text" href="https://www.boxofficemojo.com/yearly/?view2=worldwide&amp;view=releasedate&amp;p=.htm" rel="nofollow">
             "Yearly Box Office"
            </a>
            .
            <i>
             <a href="/wiki/Box_Office_Mojo" title="Box Office Mojo">
              Box Office Mojo
             </a>
            </i>
            <span class="reference-accessdate">
             . Retrieved
             <span class="nowrap">
              January 6,
             </span>
             2012
            </span>
            .
           </cite>
           <span class="Z3988" title="ctx_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=unknown&amp;rft.jtitle=Box+Office+Mojo&amp;rft.atitle=Yearly+Box+Office&amp;rft_id=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.boxofficemojo.com%2Fyearly%2F%3Fview2%3Dworldwide%26view%3Dreleasedate%26p%3D.htm&amp;rfr_id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AList+of+highest-grossing+films">
