# soup.body

Navigating the HTML Family Tree: Children, siblings, and parents#

  • Each tag is located within a tree-hierarchy of parents, siblings, and children

    • The family-relation is based on the identation level of the tags.

  • Methods/attributes for the location/related tags of a tag

    • .parent, .parents

    • .child, .children

    • .descendents

    • .next_sibling, .previous_sibling

  • Note: a newline character \n is also considered a tag/sibling/child

Accessing Child Tags#

  • To get to later occurances of a tag type (i.e. the 2nd <p> tag in a tree), we need to navigate through the parent tag’s children

    • To access an iterable list of a tag’s children use .children

      • But, this only returns its direct children (one indentation level down)

    # print direct children of the body tag
body = soup.body
for child in body.children:
    # print child if its not empty
    print(child if child is not None else ' ', '\n\n')  # '\n\n' for visual separation

  • To access all children use .descendents

    • Returns all chidren and children of children

    for child in body.descendents:
    # print all children/grandchildren, etc
    print(child if child is not None else ' ','\n\n')  

## What is the .children for the soup.body?
soup.body.children

<list_iterator at 0x7fed4c591040>

## Make the children viewable and check how many there are
body_tags = list(soup.body.children)
len(body_tags)

18

Accessing Parent tags#

  • To access the parent of a tag use .parent

title = soup.head.title
print(title.parent.name)

  • To get a list of all parents use .parents

title = soup.head.title
for parent in title.parents:
    print(parent.name)

Accessing Sibling tags#

  • siblings are tags in the same tree indentation level

  • .next_sibling, .previous_sibling

## Check the parents of the first p
p_parents = list(soup.body.p.parents)
p_parents[0]

<div class="mw-parser-output"><div class="shortdescription nomobile noexcerpt noprint searchaux" style="display:none">Wikimedia list article</div>
<p class="mw-empty-elt">
</p>
<div class="thumb tright"><div class="thumbinner" style="width:222px;"><a class="image" href="/wiki/File:Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg"><img alt="A screencap of the title card from the trailer of Gone with the Wind." class="thumbimage" data-file-height="2000" data-file-width="1299" decoding="async" height="339" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg/220px-Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg/330px-Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg/440px-Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg 2x" width="220"/></a> <div class="thumbcaption"><div class="magnify"><a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:Poster_-_Gone_With_the_Wind_01.jpg" title="Enlarge"></a></div><i>Gone with the Wind</i> held the record of highest-grossing film for twenty-five years and, adjusted for inflation, has earned more than any other film.</div></div></div>
<p><a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Films" title="Films">Films</a> generate income from several revenue streams, including <a href="/wiki/Movie_theater" title="Movie theater">theatrical exhibition</a>, <a href="/wiki/Home_video" title="Home video">home video</a>, <a href="/wiki/Broadcasting_rights" title="Broadcasting rights">television broadcast rights</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Merchandising" title="Merchandising">merchandising</a>. However, theatrical <a href="/wiki/Box_office" title="Box office">box office</a> earnings are the primary metric for trade publications in assessing the success of a film, mostly because of the availability of the data compared to sales figures for home video and broadcast rights, but also because of historical practice. Included on the list are charts of the top box-office earners (ranked by both the <a href="/wiki/Real_versus_nominal_value_(economics)" title="Real versus nominal value (economics)">nominal and real value</a> of their revenue), a chart of high-grossing films by calendar year, a timeline showing the transition of the highest-grossing film record, and a chart of the highest-grossing film franchises and series. All charts are ranked by international theatrical box-office performance where possible, excluding income derived from home video, broadcasting rights, and merchandise.
</p><p>Traditionally, <a href="/wiki/War_film" title="War film">war films</a>, <a href="/wiki/Musical_film" title="Musical film">musicals</a>, and <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/List_of_historical_drama_films" title="List of historical drama films">historical dramas</a> have been the most popular genres, but <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Media_franchise" title="Media franchise">franchise films</a> have been among the best performers of the 21st century. There is strong interest in the <a href="/wiki/Superhero_film" title="Superhero film">superhero genre</a>, with nine films in the <a href="/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe" title="Marvel Cinematic Universe">Marvel Cinematic Universe</a> featuring among the nominal top-earners. The most successful superhero film, <i><a href="/wiki/Avengers:_Endgame" title="Avengers: Endgame">Avengers: Endgame</a></i>, is also the highest-grossing film overall on the nominal earnings chart, and there are four films in total based on the <i><a href="/wiki/Avengers_(comics)" title="Avengers (comics)">Avengers</a></i> comic books charting in the top twenty. Other <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/List_of_films_based_on_Marvel_Comics" title="List of films based on Marvel Comics">Marvel Comics adaptations</a> have also had success with the <i><a href="/wiki/Spider-Man_in_film" title="Spider-Man in film">Spider-Man</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/X-Men_(film_series)" title="X-Men (film series)">X-Men</a></i> properties, while films based on <i><a href="/wiki/Batman_in_film" title="Batman in film">Batman</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/Superman_in_film" title="Superman in film">Superman</a></i> from <a href="/wiki/DC_Comics" title="DC Comics">DC Comics</a> have generally performed well. <i><a href="/wiki/Star_Wars" title="Star Wars">Star Wars</a></i> is also represented in the nominal earnings chart with five films, while the <i><a href="/wiki/Harry_Potter_(film_series)" title="Harry Potter (film series)">Harry Potter</a></i>, <i><a href="/wiki/Jurassic_Park" title="Jurassic Park">Jurassic Park</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean_(film_series)" title="Pirates of the Caribbean (film series)">Pirates of the Caribbean</a></i> franchises feature prominently. <i><a href="/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film)" title="Avatar (2009 film)">Avatar</a></i>, in second place on the nominal chart, is the highest-grossing film that is not a sequel or an adaptation of a pre-existing property. Animated family films have performed consistently well, with <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/List_of_Disney_theatrical_animated_features" title="List of Disney theatrical animated features">Disney films</a> enjoying lucrative re-releases prior to the home-video era. Disney also enjoyed later success with films such as <i><a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Frozen_I" title="Frozen I">Frozen I</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/Frozen_II" title="Frozen II">II</a></i>, <i><a href="/wiki/Zootopia" title="Zootopia">Zootopia</a></i>, and <i><a href="/wiki/The_Lion_King" title="The Lion King">The Lion King</a></i> (with <a href="/wiki/The_Lion_King_(2019_film)" title="The Lion King (2019 film)">its computer-animated remake</a> as the <a href="/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_animated_films" title="List of highest-grossing animated films">highest-grossing animated film</a>), as well as its <a href="/wiki/List_of_Pixar_films" title="List of Pixar films">Pixar</a> brand, of which <i><a href="/wiki/Incredibles_2" title="Incredibles 2">Incredibles 2</a></i>, <i><a href="/wiki/Toy_Story_3" title="Toy Story 3">Toy Story 3</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/Toy_Story_4" title="Toy Story 4">4</a></i>, and <i><a href="/wiki/Finding_Dory" title="Finding Dory">Finding Dory</a></i> have been the best performers. Beyond Disney and Pixar animation, the <i><a href="/wiki/Despicable_Me" title="Despicable Me">Despicable Me</a></i>, <i><a href="/wiki/Shrek_(franchise)" title="Shrek (franchise)">Shrek</a></i>, and <i><a href="/wiki/Ice_Age_(franchise)" title="Ice Age (franchise)">Ice Age</a></i> series have met with the most success.
</p><p>While <a href="/wiki/Inflation" title="Inflation">inflation</a> has eroded away the achievements of most films from the 1960s and 1970s, there are franchises originating from that period that are still active. Besides the <i>Star Wars</i> and <i>Superman</i> franchises, <i><a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/James_Bond_in_film" title="James Bond in film">James Bond</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/List_of_Star_Trek_films_and_television_series" title="List of Star Trek films and television series">Star Trek</a></i> films are still being released periodically; all four are among the highest-grossing franchises. Some of the older films that held the record of highest-grossing film still have respectable grosses by today's standards, but no longer compete numerically against today's top-earners in an era of much higher individual ticket prices. When those prices are adjusted for inflation, however, then <i><a href="/wiki/Gone_with_the_Wind_(film)" title="Gone with the Wind (film)">Gone with the Wind</a></i>—which was the highest-grossing film outright for twenty-five years—is still the highest-grossing film of all time. All grosses on the list are expressed in U.S. dollars at their nominal value, except where stated otherwise.
</p>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r886046785">.mw-parser-output .toclimit-2 .toclevel-1 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-3 .toclevel-2 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-4 .toclevel-3 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-5 .toclevel-4 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-6 .toclevel-5 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-7 .toclevel-6 ul{display:none}</style><div class="toclimit-2"><div aria-labelledby="mw-toc-heading" class="toc" id="toc" role="navigation"><input class="toctogglecheckbox" id="toctogglecheckbox" role="button" style="display:none" type="checkbox"/><div class="toctitle" dir="ltr" lang="en"><h2 id="mw-toc-heading">Contents</h2><span class="toctogglespan"><label class="toctogglelabel" for="toctogglecheckbox"></label></span></div>
<ul>
<li class="toclevel-1 tocsection-1"><a href="#Highest-grossing_films"><span class="tocnumber">1</span> <span class="toctext">Highest-grossing films</span></a></li>
<li class="toclevel-1 tocsection-2"><a href="#Highest-grossing_films_adjusted_for_inflation"><span class="tocnumber">2</span> <span class="toctext">Highest-grossing films adjusted for inflation</span></a></li>
<li class="toclevel-1 tocsection-3"><a href="#High-grossing_films_by_year"><span class="tocnumber">3</span> <span class="toctext">High-grossing films by year</span></a></li>
<li class="toclevel-1 tocsection-4"><a href="#Timeline_of_highest-grossing_films"><span class="tocnumber">4</span> <span class="toctext">Timeline of highest-grossing films</span></a></li>
<li class="toclevel-1 tocsection-5"><a href="#Highest-grossing_franchises_and_film_series"><span class="tocnumber">5</span> <span class="toctext">Highest-grossing franchises and film series</span></a></li>
<li class="toclevel-1 tocsection-6"><a href="#See_also"><span class="tocnumber">6</span> <span class="toctext">See also</span></a></li>
<li class="toclevel-1 tocsection-7"><a href="#References"><span class="tocnumber">7</span> <span class="toctext">References</span></a>
<ul>
<li class="toclevel-2 tocsection-8"><a href="#Box_office_sources"><span class="tocnumber">7.1</span> <span class="toctext">Box office sources</span></a></li>
<li class="toclevel-2 tocsection-9"><a href="#§_Franchise_and_series_sources"><span class="tocnumber">7.2</span> <span class="toctext"><span><b><sup>§</sup></b></span> Franchise and series sources</span></a></li>
<li class="toclevel-2 tocsection-10"><a href="#Bibliography"><span class="tocnumber">7.3</span> <span class="toctext">Bibliography</span></a></li>
</ul>
</li>
<li class="toclevel-1 tocsection-11"><a href="#External_links"><span class="tocnumber">8</span> <span class="toctext">External links</span></a></li>
</ul>
</div>
</div>
<h2><span class="mw-headline" id="Highest-grossing_films">Highest-grossing films</span></h2>
<div class="thumb tright"><div class="thumbinner" style="width:172px;"><a class="image" href="/wiki/File:Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_(cropped).jpg"><img alt="A portrait of a middle aged man." class="thumbimage" data-file-height="2193" data-file-width="1530" decoding="async" height="244" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_%28cropped%29.jpg/170px-Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_%28cropped%29.jpg" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_%28cropped%29.jpg/255px-Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_%28cropped%29.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_%28cropped%29.jpg/340px-Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_%28cropped%29.jpg 2x" width="170"/></a> <div class="thumbcaption"><div class="magnify"><a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:Kevin_Feige_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_premiere_-_July_2014_(cropped).jpg" title="Enlarge"></a></div>Eight of the twenty-five highest-grossing films, including <i>Avengers: Endgame</i> at number one, were produced by <a href="/wiki/Marvel_Studios" title="Marvel Studios">Marvel Studios</a> president <a href="/wiki/Kevin_Feige" title="Kevin Feige">Kevin Feige</a>.</div></div></div>
<p>With a worldwide box-office gross of over $2.797 billion, <i>Avengers: Endgame</i> is proclaimed to be the "highest-grossing" film, but such claims usually refer to <a href="/wiki/Movie_theater" title="Movie theater">theatrical</a> revenues only and do not take into account <a href="/wiki/Home_video" title="Home video">home video</a> and <a href="/wiki/Television" title="Television">television</a> income, which can form a significant portion of a film's earnings. Once revenue from home entertainment is factored in it is not immediately clear which film is the most successful. <i><a href="/wiki/Titanic_(1997_film)" title="Titanic (1997 film)">Titanic</a></i> earned $1.2 billion from <a href="/wiki/Video" title="Video">video</a> and <a href="/wiki/DVD" title="DVD">DVD</a> sales and rentals,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-titanic-sales_1-0"><a href="#cite_note-titanic-sales-1">[1]</a></sup> in addition to the $2.2 billion it grossed in theaters. While complete sales data are not available for <i>Avatar</i>, it earned $345 million from the sale of sixteen million DVD and <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Blu-ray_Disc" title="Blu-ray Disc">Blu-ray</a> units in North America,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-2"><a href="#cite_note-2">[2]</a></sup> and ultimately sold a total of thirty million DVD and Blu-ray units worldwide.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-3"><a href="#cite_note-3">[3]</a></sup> After home video income is accounted for, both films have earned over $3 billion each. Television <a href="/wiki/Broadcasting" title="Broadcasting">broadcast</a> rights will also substantially add to a film's earnings, with a film often earning as much as 20–25% of its theatrical box office for a couple of television runs on top of <a href="/wiki/Pay-per-view" title="Pay-per-view">pay-per-view</a> revenues;<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-4"><a href="#cite_note-4">[4]</a></sup> <i>Titanic</i> earned a further $55 million from the <a href="/wiki/NBC" title="NBC">NBC</a> and <a href="/wiki/HBO" title="HBO">HBO</a> broadcast rights,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-titanic-sales_1-1"><a href="#cite_note-titanic-sales-1">[1]</a></sup> equating to about 9% of its North American gross.
</p><p>When a film is highly exploitable as a commercial property, its ancillary revenues can dwarf its income from direct film sales.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-5"><a href="#cite_note-5">[5]</a></sup> <i>The Lion King</i> (1994) earned over $2 billion in box-office and home video sales,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Pincus-Roth_(2006)_6-0"><a href="#cite_note-Pincus-Roth_(2006)-6">[6]</a></sup> but this pales in comparison to the $8 billion earned at box offices around the world by <a href="/wiki/The_Lion_King_(musical)" title="The Lion King (musical)">the stage adaptation</a>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-7"><a href="#cite_note-7">[7]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Merchandising" title="Merchandising">Merchandising</a> can be extremely lucrative too: <i>The Lion King</i> also sold $3 billion of merchandise,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-8"><a href="#cite_note-8">[8]</a></sup> while Pixar's <i><a href="/wiki/Cars_(film)" title="Cars (film)">Cars</a></i>—which earned $462 million in theatrical revenues and was only a modest hit by comparison to other <a href="/wiki/List_of_Pixar_films" title="List of Pixar films">Pixar films</a><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-9"><a href="#cite_note-9">[9]</a></sup>—generated global merchandise sales of over $8 billion in the five years after its 2006 release.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-10"><a href="#cite_note-10">[10]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-11"><a href="#cite_note-11">[11]</a></sup> Pixar had another huge hit with <i>Toy Story 3</i>, which generated almost $10 billion in merchandise retail sales in addition to the $1 billion it earned at the box office.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-12"><a href="#cite_note-12">[12]</a></sup>
</p><p>On this chart, films are ranked by the revenues from theatrical exhibition at their nominal value, along with the highest positions they attained. Five films in total have grossed in excess of $2 billion worldwide, with <i>Avengers: Endgame</i> ranked in the top position. All of the films have had a theatrical run (including re-releases) in the 21st century, and films that have not played during this period do not appear on the chart because of ticket-price inflation, population size and ticket purchasing trends not being considered.
</p>
<dl><dd></dd>
<dd><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r981673959">.mw-parser-output .legend{page-break-inside:avoid;break-inside:avoid-column}.mw-parser-output .legend-color{display:inline-block;min-width:1.25em;height:1.25em;line-height:1.25;margin:1px 0;text-align:center;border:1px solid black;background-color:transparent;color:black}.mw-parser-output .legend-text{}</style><div class="legend"><span class="legend-color" style="background-color:#b6fcb6; color:black;font-size:50%;"> </span> <img alt="film currently playing" data-file-height="14" data-file-width="9" decoding="async" height="14" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/37/Dagger-14-plain.png" width="9"/> <i>Background shading indicates films playing in the week commencing 5 March 2021 in theaters around the world</i>.</div></dd></dl>

</td>

</td>
<td style="text-align:center;"><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-lion_king_93-0"><a href="#cite_note-lion_king-93">[# 79]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Jurassic_Park_peak_78-1"><a href="#cite_note-Jurassic_Park_peak-78">[# 64]</a></sup>
</td></tr></tbody></table>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1011217839">.mw-parser-output .refbegin{font-size:90%;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .refbegin-hanging-indents>ul{margin-left:0}.mw-parser-output .refbegin-hanging-indents>ul>li{margin-left:0;padding-left:3.2em;text-indent:-3.2em}.mw-parser-output .refbegin-hanging-indents ul,.mw-parser-output .refbegin-hanging-indents ul li{list-style:none}@media(max-width:720px){.mw-parser-output .refbegin-hanging-indents>ul>li{padding-left:1.6em;text-indent:-1.6em}}.mw-parser-output .refbegin-100{font-size:100%}.mw-parser-output .refbegin-columns{margin-top:0.3em}.mw-parser-output .refbegin-columns ul{margin-top:0}.mw-parser-output .refbegin-columns li{page-break-inside:avoid;break-inside:avoid-column}</style><div class="refbegin" style="">
<p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Frozen"><sup>F</sup><a href="/wiki/Box_Office_Mojo" title="Box Office Mojo">Box Office Mojo</a> stopped updating its main total for <i>Frozen</i> in August 2014, while it was still in release. The total listed here incorporates subsequent earnings in Japan, Nigeria, Spain, the United Kingdom and Germany up to the end of 2015 but omits earnings in Turkey, Iceland, Brazil, and Australia (2016), which amount to a few hundred thousand dollars. The total is rounded to $1 million to compensate for the numerical inaccuracy. It was re-released in the United Kingdom in December 2017 with <i><a href="/wiki/Olaf%27s_Frozen_Adventure" title="Olaf's Frozen Adventure">Olaf's Frozen Adventure</a></i> earning an additional $2.3 million.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Fast_8"><sup>F8</sup>In the case of <i>The Fate of the Furious</i> the gross is from an archived version of Box Office Mojo, after irregularities were discovered in the current figure. Ongoing weekly drops in the totals for several countries—Argentina being the worst affected—led to a drop in the overall worldwide total.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-94"><a href="#cite_note-94">[15]</a></sup> In view of what appears to be an aberration in the source, a previous figure is provided.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_LOTR3"><sup>RK</sup><i>Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King</i> saw its original gross corrected in 2019. The result of this correction is that <i>Spider-Man: Far From Home</i>, <i>Captain Marvel</i> and <i>Transformers: Dark of the Moon</i> all peaked one place lower than shown in the accompanying source.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_TS3"><sup>TS3</sup>Box Office Mojo revised the grosses for Pixar films in August 2016, resulting in the gross for <i>Toy Story 3</i> being corrected from $1.063 billion to $1.067 billion.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-95"><a href="#cite_note-95">[16]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-96"><a href="#cite_note-96">[17]</a></sup> This means that it peaked at number 4 at the end of its run, ahead of <i><a href="/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean:_Dead_Man%27s_Chest" title="Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest">Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest</a></i>, rather than at number 5 as indicated by the source.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_DM2"><sup>DM2</sup>Disney issued an erratum to the gross for <i><a href="/wiki/The_Lion_King" title="The Lion King">The Lion King</a></i> in May 2016, correcting its gross from $987.5 million to $968.5 million.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-97"><a href="#cite_note-97">[18]</a></sup> This means that <i>Despicable Me 2</i> peaked at number 19 at the end of its run, ahead of <i>The Lion King</i>, rather than at number 20 as indicated by the source.</span>
</p>
</div>
<h2><span class="mw-headline" id="Highest-grossing_films_adjusted_for_inflation">Highest-grossing films adjusted for inflation</span></h2>
<div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">See also: <a href="/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films_in_the_United_States_and_Canada#Adjusted_for_ticket-price_inflation" title="List of highest-grossing films in the United States and Canada">List of highest-grossing films in the United States and Canada § Adjusted for ticket-price inflation</a></div>
<div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">See also: <a href="/wiki/List_of_films_by_box_office_admissions" title="List of films by box office admissions">List of films by box office admissions</a></div>
<div class="thumb tright"><div class="thumbinner" style="width:222px;"><a class="image" href="/wiki/File:World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg"><img alt="A map of the world with different regions colored in correlating to inflation rates" class="thumbimage" data-file-height="1314" data-file-width="2560" decoding="async" height="113" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg/220px-World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg.png" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg/330px-World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg.png 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg/440px-World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg.png 2x" width="220"/></a> <div class="thumbcaption"><div class="magnify"><a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:World_Inflation_rate_2017.svg" title="Enlarge"></a></div>Inflation rates around the world vary, complicating inflation adjustment.</div></div></div>
<p>Because of the long-term effects of <a href="/wiki/Inflation" title="Inflation">inflation</a>, notably the significant increase of movie theater ticket prices, the list unadjusted for inflation gives far more weight to later films.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Bialik_(2010-1-29)_98-0"><a href="#cite_note-Bialik_(2010-1-29)-98">[19]</a></sup> The unadjusted list, while commonly found in the press, is therefore largely meaningless for comparing films widely separated in time, as many films from earlier eras will never appear on a modern unadjusted list, despite achieving higher commercial success when adjusted for price increases.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Pincus-Roth_(2009)_99-0"><a href="#cite_note-Pincus-Roth_(2009)-99">[20]</a></sup> To compensate for the devaluation of the currency, some charts make adjustments for inflation, but not even this practice fully addresses the issue, since ticket prices and inflation do not necessarily parallel one another. For example, in 1970, tickets cost $1.55 or about $6.68 in inflation-adjusted 2004 dollars; by 1980, prices had risen to about $2.69, a drop to $5.50 in inflation-adjusted 2004 dollars.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-LLU_(2004)_100-0"><a href="#cite_note-LLU_(2004)-100">[21]</a></sup> Ticket prices have also risen at different rates of inflation around the world, further complicating the process of adjusting worldwide grosses.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Bialik_(2010-1-29)_98-1"><a href="#cite_note-Bialik_(2010-1-29)-98">[19]</a></sup>
</p><p>Another complication is release in multiple formats for which different ticket prices are charged. One notable example of this phenomenon is <i>Avatar</i>, which was also released in 3D and IMAX: almost two-thirds of tickets for that film were for 3D showings with an average price of $10, and about one-sixth were for IMAX showings with an average price over $14.50, compared to a 2010 average price of $7.61 for 2D films.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Gray_(2010-02-03)_101-0"><a href="#cite_note-Gray_(2010-02-03)-101">[22]</a></sup> Social and economic factors such as population change<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Bialik_(2010-1-30)_102-0"><a href="#cite_note-Bialik_(2010-1-30)-102">[23]</a></sup> and the growth of international markets<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Kolesnikov-Jessop_103-0"><a href="#cite_note-Kolesnikov-Jessop-103">[24]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Hoad_104-0"><a href="#cite_note-Hoad-104">[25]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Frankel_105-0"><a href="#cite_note-Frankel-105">[26]</a></sup> also impact on the number of people purchasing theater tickets, along with audience demographics where some films sell a much higher proportion of discounted children's tickets, or perform better in big cities where tickets cost more.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Pincus-Roth_(2009)_99-1"><a href="#cite_note-Pincus-Roth_(2009)-99">[20]</a></sup>
</p><p>The measuring system for gauging a film's success is based on unadjusted grosses, mainly because historically this is the way it has always been done because of the practices of the film industry: the box-office receipts are compiled by theaters and relayed to the distributor, which in turn releases them to the media.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Bialik_(2007)_106-0"><a href="#cite_note-Bialik_(2007)-106">[27]</a></sup> Converting to a more representative system that counts ticket sales rather than gross is also fraught with problems because the only data available for older films are the sale totals.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Bialik_(2010-1-30)_102-1"><a href="#cite_note-Bialik_(2010-1-30)-102">[23]</a></sup> As the motion picture industry is highly oriented towards marketing currently released films, unadjusted figures are always used in marketing campaigns so that new blockbuster films can much more easily achieve a high sales ranking, and thus be promoted as a "top film of all time",<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-LLU_(2004)_100-1"><a href="#cite_note-LLU_(2004)-100">[21]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Leonhardt_107-0"><a href="#cite_note-Leonhardt-107">[28]</a></sup> so there is little incentive to switch to a more robust analysis from a marketing or even newsworthy point of view.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Bialik_(2007)_106-1"><a href="#cite_note-Bialik_(2007)-106">[27]</a></sup>
</p><p>Despite the inherent difficulties in accounting for inflation, several attempts have been made. Estimates depend on the <a href="/wiki/Price_index" title="Price index">price index</a> used to adjust the grosses,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Leonhardt_107-1"><a href="#cite_note-Leonhardt-107">[28]</a></sup> and the <a href="/wiki/Exchange_rate" title="Exchange rate">exchange rates</a> used to convert between currencies can also impact upon the calculations, both of which can have an effect on the ultimate rankings of an inflation adjusted list. <i>Gone with the Wind</i>—first released in 1939—is generally considered to be the most successful film, with <i><a href="/wiki/Guinness_World_Records" title="Guinness World Records">Guinness World Records</a></i> in 2014 estimating its adjusted global gross at $3.4 billion. Estimates for <i>Gone with the Wind</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span>s adjusted gross have varied substantially: its owner, <a href="/wiki/Turner_Entertainment" title="Turner Entertainment">Turner Entertainment</a>, estimated its adjusted earnings at $3.3 billion in 2007, a few years earlier than the <i>Guinness</i> estimate;<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-gwtw_tcm_108-0"><a href="#cite_note-gwtw_tcm-108">[29]</a></sup> other estimates fall either side of this amount, with one putting its gross just under $3 billion in 2010,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Shone_109-0"><a href="#cite_note-Shone-109">[30]</a></sup> while another provided an alternative figure of $3.8 billion in 2006.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-110"><a href="#cite_note-110">[31]</a></sup> Which film is <i>Gone with the Wind</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span>s nearest rival depends on the set of figures used: <i>Guinness</i> had <i>Avatar</i> in second place with $3 billion, while other estimates saw <i>Titanic</i> in the runner-up spot with first-run worldwide earnings of almost $2.9 billion at 2010 prices.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Shone_109-1"><a href="#cite_note-Shone-109">[30]</a></sup>
</p>

<link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1011217839" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/><div class="refbegin" style="">
<p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Inflation"><sup>Inf</sup>Inflation adjustment is carried out using the <a href="/wiki/Consumer_price_index" title="Consumer price index">Consumer price index</a> for advanced economies published by the <a href="/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund" title="International Monetary Fund">International Monetary Fund</a>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-112"><a href="#cite_note-112">[33]</a></sup> The index is uniformly applied to the grosses in the chart published by <i>Guinness World Records</i> in 2014, beginning with the 2014 index. The figures in the above chart take into account inflation that occurred in 2014, and in every available year since then, through 2019.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Titanic"><sup>T</sup><i>Guinness</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span> adjusted total for <i>Titanic</i> only increased by $102,000,000 between the 2012 (published in 2011) and 2015 editions, a rise of 4.2% shared by the other adjusted totals in the chart, and omitted the gross from a 3D re-release in 2012.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Guinness_2015_111-1"><a href="#cite_note-Guinness_2015-111">[32]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-113"><a href="#cite_note-113">[34]</a></sup> This chart incorporates the gross of $343,550,770 from the reissue and adjusts it from the 2014 index.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-114"><a href="#cite_note-114">[35]</a></sup> <i>Titanic</i> grossed a further $691,642 during a limited re-release in 2017 for its 20th anniversary, but this figure is not represented in the adjusted total.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-115"><a href="#cite_note-115">[36]</a></sup></span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Avengers_Endgame"><sup>AE</sup>Since <i>Avengers: Endgame</i> was released in 2019, no inflation adjustment has been applied to its gross.</span>
</p>
</div>
<h2><span class="mw-headline" id="High-grossing_films_by_year">High-grossing films by year</span></h2>
<div class="quotebox pullquote floatright" style="width:20em;; ; background-color: #F0EAD6;;"><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r887775652">.mw-parser-output .quotebox{background-color:#F9F9F9;border:1px solid #aaa;box-sizing:border-box;padding:10px;font-size:88%;max-width:100%}.mw-parser-output .quotebox.floatleft{margin:0.5em 1.4em 0.8em 0}.mw-parser-output .quotebox.floatright{margin:0.5em 0 0.8em 1.4em}.mw-parser-output .quotebox.centered{margin:0.5em auto 0.8em auto}.mw-parser-output .quotebox.floatleft p,.mw-parser-output .quotebox.floatright p{font-style:inherit}.mw-parser-output .quotebox-title{background-color:#F9F9F9;text-align:center;font-size:larger;font-weight:bold}.mw-parser-output .quotebox-quote.quoted:before{font-family:"Times New Roman",serif;font-weight:bold;font-size:large;color:gray;content:" “ ";vertical-align:-45%;line-height:0}.mw-parser-output .quotebox-quote.quoted:after{font-family:"Times New Roman",serif;font-weight:bold;font-size:large;color:gray;content:" ” ";line-height:0}.mw-parser-output .quotebox .left-aligned{text-align:left}.mw-parser-output .quotebox .right-aligned{text-align:right}.mw-parser-output .quotebox .center-aligned{text-align:center}.mw-parser-output .quotebox cite{display:block;font-style:normal}@media screen and (max-width:360px){.mw-parser-output .quotebox{min-width:100%;margin:0 0 0.8em!important;float:none!important}}</style>
<div class="quotebox-title" style="background-color: #F0EAD6;;"><span class="anchor" id="distributor_rental"></span>Glossary: <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Theatrical_rental" title="Theatrical rental">Distributor rentals</a></div>
<div class="quotebox-quote left-aligned" style="">Box-office figures are reported in the form of gross or <i>distributor rentals</i>, the latter being especially true of older films. Commonly mistaken for home video revenue, the rentals are the distributor's share of the film's theatrical revenue i.e. the box office gross less the exhibitor's cut.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Cones_116-0"><a href="#cite_note-Cones-116">[37]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Marich_117-0"><a href="#cite_note-Marich-117">[38]</a></sup> Historically, the rental price averaged at 30–40% when the distributors owned the theater chains, equating to just over a third of the gross being paid to the distributor of the film.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Balio_118-0"><a href="#cite_note-Balio-118">[39]</a></sup> In the modern marketplace, rental fees can vary greatly—depending on a number of factors—although the films from the major studios average out at 43%.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Cones_116-1"><a href="#cite_note-Cones-116">[37]</a></sup></div>
</div>
<p>Audience tastes were fairly eclectic during the 20th century, but several trends did emerge. During the <a href="/wiki/Silent_film" title="Silent film">silent era</a>, films with <a href="/wiki/War_film" title="War film">war themes</a> were popular with audiences, with <i>The Birth of a Nation</i> (<a href="/wiki/American_Civil_War" title="American Civil War">American Civil War</a>), <i>The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse</i>, <i>The Big Parade</i> and <i>Wings</i> (all <a href="/wiki/World_War_I" title="World War I">World War I</a>) becoming the most successful films in their respective years of release, with the trend coming to an end with <i>All Quiet on the Western Front</i> in 1930. With the advent of <a href="/wiki/Sound_film" title="Sound film">sound</a> in 1927, the <a href="/wiki/Musical_film" title="Musical film">musical</a>—the genre best placed to showcase the new technology—took over as the most popular type of film with audiences, with 1928 and 1929 both being topped by musical films. The genre continued to perform strongly in the 1930s, but the outbreak of <a href="/wiki/World_War_II" title="World War II">World War II</a> saw war-themed films dominate again during this period, starting with <i>Gone with the Wind</i> (American Civil War) in 1939, and finishing with <i>The Best Years of Our Lives</i> (World War II) in 1946. <i>Samson and Delilah</i> (1949) saw the beginning of a trend of increasingly expensive <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/List_of_historical_drama_films" title="List of historical drama films">historical dramas</a> set during <a href="/wiki/Ancient_Rome" title="Ancient Rome">Ancient Rome</a>/<a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Biblical_times" title="Biblical times">biblical times</a> throughout the 1950s as cinema competed with television for audiences,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-119"><a href="#cite_note-119">[40]</a></sup> with <i>Quo Vadis</i>, <i>The Robe</i>, <i>The Ten Commandments</i>, <i>Ben-Hur</i> and <i>Spartacus</i> all becoming the highest-grossing film of the year during initial release, before the genre started to wane after several high-profile failures.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HallNeale179_120-0"><a href="#cite_note-HallNeale179-120">[41]</a></sup> The success of <i>White Christmas</i> and <i>South Pacific</i> in the 1950s foreshadowed the comeback of the musical in the 1960s with <i>West Side Story</i>, <i>Mary Poppins</i>, <i>My Fair Lady</i>, <i>The Sound of Music</i> and <i>Funny Girl</i> all among the top films of the decade. The 1970s saw a shift in audience tastes to <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/High_concept" title="High concept">high concept</a> films, with six such films made by either <a href="/wiki/George_Lucas" title="George Lucas">George Lucas</a> or <a href="/wiki/Steven_Spielberg" title="Steven Spielberg">Steven Spielberg</a> topping the chart during the 1980s. The 21st century has seen an increasing dependence on franchises and <a href="/wiki/Film_adaptation" title="Film adaptation">adaptations</a>, with the box-office dominance of films based on pre-existing intellectual property at record levels.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-121"><a href="#cite_note-121">[42]</a></sup>
</p>
<div class="thumb tleft"><div class="thumbinner" style="width:172px;"><a class="image" href="/wiki/File:Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg"><img alt="A portrait of a bespectacled middle aged man." class="thumbimage" data-file-height="3248" data-file-width="2416" decoding="async" height="229" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/170px-Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/255px-Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/340px-Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg 2x" width="170"/></a> <div class="thumbcaption"><div class="magnify"><a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:Steven_Spielberg_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg" title="Enlarge"></a></div>Films directed by Steven Spielberg have been the highest-grossing film of the year on six occasions, and on three occasions have been the highest-grossing film of all time.</div></div></div>
<p>Steven Spielberg is the most represented director on the chart, with six films to his credit, occupying the top spot in 1975, 1981, 1982, 1984, 1989 and 1993. <a href="/wiki/Cecil_B._DeMille" title="Cecil B. DeMille">Cecil B. DeMille</a> (1932, 1947, 1949, 1952 and 1956) and <a href="/wiki/William_Wyler" title="William Wyler">William Wyler</a> (1942, 1946, 1959 and 1968) are in second and third place with five and four films respectively, while <a href="/wiki/D._W._Griffith" title="D. W. Griffith">D. W. Griffith</a> (1915, 1916 and 1920), <a href="/wiki/George_Roy_Hill" title="George Roy Hill">George Roy Hill</a> (1966, 1969 and 1973), <a href="/wiki/James_Cameron" title="James Cameron">James Cameron</a> (1991, 1997 and 2009) and the <a href="/wiki/Russo_brothers" title="Russo brothers">Russo brothers</a> (2016, 2018 and 2019) all feature heavily with three films apiece. George Lucas directed two chart-toppers in 1977 and 1999, but also served in a strong creative capacity as a producer and writer in 1980, 1981, 1983, 1984 and 1989 as well. The following directors have also all directed two films on the chart: <a href="/wiki/Frank_Lloyd" title="Frank Lloyd">Frank Lloyd</a>, <a href="/wiki/King_Vidor" title="King Vidor">King Vidor</a>, <a href="/wiki/Frank_Capra" title="Frank Capra">Frank Capra</a>, <a href="/wiki/Michael_Curtiz" title="Michael Curtiz">Michael Curtiz</a>, <a href="/wiki/Leo_McCarey" title="Leo McCarey">Leo McCarey</a>, <a href="/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock" title="Alfred Hitchcock">Alfred Hitchcock</a>, <a href="/wiki/David_Lean" title="David Lean">David Lean</a>, <a href="/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick" title="Stanley Kubrick">Stanley Kubrick</a>, <a href="/wiki/Guy_Hamilton" title="Guy Hamilton">Guy Hamilton</a>, <a href="/wiki/Mike_Nichols" title="Mike Nichols">Mike Nichols</a>, <a href="/wiki/William_Friedkin" title="William Friedkin">William Friedkin</a>, <a href="/wiki/Peter_Jackson" title="Peter Jackson">Peter Jackson</a>, <a href="/wiki/Gore_Verbinski" title="Gore Verbinski">Gore Verbinski</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Michael_Bay" title="Michael Bay">Michael Bay</a>; <a href="/wiki/Mervyn_LeRoy" title="Mervyn LeRoy">Mervyn LeRoy</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ken_Annakin" title="Ken Annakin">Ken Annakin</a> and <a href="/wiki/Robert_Wise" title="Robert Wise">Robert Wise</a> are each represented by one solo credit and one shared credit, and <a href="/wiki/John_Ford" title="John Ford">John Ford</a> co-directed two films. Disney films are usually co-directed and some directors have served on several winning teams: <a href="/wiki/Wilfred_Jackson" title="Wilfred Jackson">Wilfred Jackson</a>, <a href="/wiki/Hamilton_Luske" title="Hamilton Luske">Hamilton Luske</a>, <a href="/wiki/Clyde_Geronimi" title="Clyde Geronimi">Clyde Geronimi</a>, <a href="/wiki/David_Hand_(animator)" title="David Hand (animator)">David Hand</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ben_Sharpsteen" title="Ben Sharpsteen">Ben Sharpsteen</a>, <a href="/wiki/Wolfgang_Reitherman" title="Wolfgang Reitherman">Wolfgang Reitherman</a> and Bill Roberts have all co-directed at least two films on the list. Only seven directors have topped the chart in consecutive years: McCarey (1944 and 1945), Nichols (1966 and 1967), Spielberg (1981 and 1982), Jackson (2002 and 2003), Verbinski (2006 and 2007) and the Russo brothers (2018 and 2019).
</p><p>Because of release schedules—especially in the case of films released towards the end of the year—and different release patterns across the world, many films can do business in two or more calendar years; therefore the grosses documented here are not confined to just the year of release. Grosses are not limited to original theatrical runs either, with many older films often being re-released periodically so the figures represent all the business a film has done since its original release; a film's first-run gross is included in brackets after the total if known. Because of incomplete data it cannot be known for sure how much money some films have made and when they made it, but generally the chart chronicles the films from each year that went on to earn the most. In the cases where estimates conflict both films are recorded, and in cases where a film has moved into first place because of being re-released the previous record-holder is also retained.
</p>
<dl><dd></dd>
<dd><link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r981673959" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/><div class="legend"><span class="legend-color" style="background-color:#b6fcb6; color:black;font-size:50%;"> </span> <img alt="film currently playing" data-file-height="14" data-file-width="9" decoding="async" height="14" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/37/Dagger-14-plain.png" width="9"/> <i>Background shading indicates films playing in the week commencing 5 March 2021 in theaters around the world</i>.</div></dd></dl>
<div style="clear:both;"></div>

<caption>High-grossing films by year of release<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-122"><a href="#cite_note-122">[43]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-123"><a href="#cite_note-123">[44]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Dirks_(year)_124-0"><a href="#cite_note-Dirks_(year)-124">[45]</a></sup>
</caption>
<tbody><tr>
<th scope="col">Year
</th>
<th scope="col">Title
</th>

</th></tr>
<tr>
<th scope="row"><a href="/wiki/1915_in_film" title="1915 in film">1915</a>
</th>
<td><i><a href="/wiki/The_Birth_of_a_Nation" title="The Birth of a Nation">The Birth of a Nation</a></i>
</td>
<td align="right"><span data-sort-value="7007500000000000000♠">$50,000,000</span>–<span data-sort-value="7008100000000000000♠">100,000,000</span><br/><span data-sort-value="7007200000000000000♠">$20,000,000</span>+<sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Rentals"><a href="#endnote_Rentals">R</a></sup> (<span data-sort-value="7006520000000000000♠">$5,200,000</span>)<sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Rentals"><a href="#endnote_Rentals">R</a></sup>
</td>
<td align="right"><span data-sort-value="7005110000000000000♠">$110,000</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align:center;"><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-125"><a href="#cite_note-125">[# 80]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Wasko_126-0"><a href="#cite_note-Wasko-126">[# 81]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-127"><a href="#cite_note-127">[# 82]</a></sup>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<th scope="row"><a href="/wiki/1916_in_film" title="1916 in film">1916</a>
</th>
<td><i><a href="/wiki/Intolerance_(film)" title="Intolerance (film)">Intolerance</a></i>
</td>
<td align="right"><span data-sort-value="7006100000000000000♠">$1,000,000</span><sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_USgross"><a href="#endnote_USgross">*</a></sup><sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Rentals"><a href="#endnote_Rentals">R</a></sup> <sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Intolerance"><a href="#endnote_Intolerance">IN</a></sup>
</td>
<td align="right"><span data-sort-value="7005489653000000000♠">$489,653</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align:center;"><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-BlockWilson26_128-0"><a href="#cite_note-BlockWilson26-128">[# 83]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-129"><a href="#cite_note-129">[# 84]</a></sup>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<th scope="row"><a href="/wiki/1917_in_film" title="1917 in film">1917</a>
</th>
<td><i><a href="/wiki/Cleopatra_(1917_film)" title="Cleopatra (1917 film)">Cleopatra</a></i>
</td>
<td align="right"><span data-sort-value="7005500000000000000♠">$500,000</span><sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_USgross"><a href="#endnote_USgross">*</a></sup><sup class="reference plainlinks nourlexpansion" id="ref_Rentals"><a href="#endnote_Rentals">R</a></sup>
</td>
<td align="right"><span data-sort-value="7005300000000000000♠">$300,000</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align:center;"><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-BlockWilson26_128-1"><a href="#cite_note-BlockWilson26-128">[# 83]</a></sup>

</td>

</td>

</td>
<td style="text-align:center;"><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-343"><a href="#cite_note-343">[# 298]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-344"><a href="#cite_note-344">[# 299]</a></sup>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<th scope="row"><a href="/wiki/2021_in_film" title="2021 in film">2021</a>
</th>
<th scope="row" style="background:#b6fcb6;"><i><a href="/wiki/Hi,_Mom_(2021_film)" title="Hi, Mom (2021 film)">Hi, Mom</a></i> <img alt="film currently playing" data-file-height="14" data-file-width="9" decoding="async" height="14" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/37/Dagger-14-plain.png" width="9"/>
</th>
<td align="right">$783,400,000
</td>
<td class="table-na" data-sort-value="" style="background: #ececec; color: #2C2C2C; vertical-align: middle; text-align: center;">TBA

<link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1011217839" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/><div class="refbegin" style="">
<p>(...) Since grosses are not limited to original theatrical runs, a film's first-run gross is included in brackets after the total if known.
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_USgross"><sup>*</sup>Canada and U.S. gross only.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Rentals"><sup>R</sup>Distributor rental.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote"><sup>TBA</sup>To be ascertained.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Intolerance"><sup>IN</sup>No contemporary sources provide figures for <i>20,000 Leagues Under the Sea</i>, although <a href="/wiki/The_Numbers_(website)" title="The Numbers (website)">The Numbers</a> provides a figure of $8,000,000 for the North American box-office gross.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-346"><a href="#cite_note-346">[46]</a></sup> However, it is possible this figure has been mistaken for the gross of the 1954 remake which also earned $8,000,000 in North American rentals.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Finler358_347-0"><a href="#cite_note-Finler358-347">[47]</a></sup></span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_For_Heaven's_Sake"><sup>FH</sup>Some sources such as The Numbers state that <i>Aloma of the South Seas</i> is the highest grossing film of the year, earning $3 million.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-348"><a href="#cite_note-348">[48]</a></sup> However, no contemporary sources provide figures for <i>Aloma of the South Seas</i>, so it is unclear what the $3 million figure relates to. If it were the rental gross then that would have made it not only the highest-grossing film of the year, but one of the highest-grossing films of the silent era, and if that is the case it would be unusual for both <i>International Motion Picture Almanac</i> and <i>Variety</i> to omit it from their lists.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Sunny_Side_Up"><sup>SS</sup>It is not clear if the figure for <i>Sunny Side Up</i> is for North America or worldwide. Other sources put its earnings at $2 million,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-349"><a href="#cite_note-349">[49]</a></sup> which may suggest the higher figure is the worldwide rental, given the confusion over international figures during this period.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HallNeale6_350-0"><a href="#cite_note-HallNeale6-350">[50]</a></sup></span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_One_Night"><sup>ON</sup>The figure for <i>It Happened One Night</i> is not truly representative of its success: it was distributed as a package deal along with more than two dozen other Columbia films, and the total earnings were averaged out; the true gross would have been much higher.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Snow_White"><sup>S7</sup><i>Snow White</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span>s $418 million global <a href="/wiki/Cume" title="Cume">cume</a> omits earnings outside of North America from 1987 onwards.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_GWTW"><sup>GW</sup>It is not absolutely clear how much <i>Gone with the Wind</i> earned from its initial release. Contemporary accounts often list it as earning $32 million in North American rentals and retrospective charts have often duplicated this claim; however, it is likely this was the worldwide rental figure. Trade journals would collate the data by either obtaining it from the distributors themselves, who were keen to promote a successful film, or by surveying theaters and constructing an estimate. Distributors would often report the worldwide rental since the higher figure made the film appear more successful, while estimates were limited to performance in North America; therefore it was not unusual for worldwide and North American rentals to be mixed up. Following the outbreak of World War II, many of the foreign markets were unavailable to Hollywood so it became standard practice to just report on North American box-office performance.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HallNeale6_350-1"><a href="#cite_note-HallNeale6-350">[50]</a></sup> In keeping with this new approach, the North American rental for <i>Gone with the Wind</i> was revised to $21 million in 1947 ($11 million lower than the previous figure),<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-351"><a href="#cite_note-351">[51]</a></sup> and as of 1953—following the 1947 re-release—<i>Variety</i> was reporting earnings of $26 million.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-352"><a href="#cite_note-352">[52]</a></sup> Through 1956, MGM reported cumulative North American earnings of $30,015,000 and foreign earnings of $18,964,000, from three releases.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-BlockWilson129_353-0"><a href="#cite_note-BlockWilson129-353">[53]</a></sup> Worldwide rentals of $32 million from the initial release is consistent with the revised figures and later reported worldwide figures: they indicate that the film earned $21 million in North America and $11 million overseas from the initial release, and added a further $9 million in North America and $8 million overseas from subsequent re-releases up to 1956.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Mom_and_Dad"><sup>MD</sup><i>Mom and Dad</i> does not generally feature in 'high-gross' lists such as those published by <i>Variety</i> due to its independent distribution. Essentially belonging to the <a href="/wiki/Exploitation_film" title="Exploitation film">exploitation genre</a>, it was marketed as an educational <a href="/wiki/Social_guidance_film" title="Social guidance film">sex hygiene</a> film in an effort to circumvent <a href="/wiki/Film_censorship" title="Film censorship">censorship laws</a>. Falling foul of the <a href="/wiki/Motion_Picture_Production_Code" title="Motion Picture Production Code">Motion Picture Production Code</a>, <i>Mom and Dad</i> was prevented from obtaining mainstream distribution and restricted to independent and <a href="/wiki/Drive-in_theater" title="Drive-in theater">drive-in theaters</a>. It was the biggest hit of its kind, and remained in continual distribution until the 1970s when <a href="/wiki/Hardcore_pornography" title="Hardcore pornography">hardcore pornography</a> eventually took over. At the end of 1947 it had earned $2 million, and by 1949, $8 million; by 1956 it had earned $22 million in rentals, representing a gross of $80 million, and would have easily placed in the top ten films in the late 1940s and early 1950s. Estimates of its total earnings are as high as $100 million.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Unconquered"><sup>UN</sup>Chopra-Gant stipulates that the figure given for <i>Unconquered</i> is for North American box-office, but as was common at the time, the chart confuses worldwide and North American grosses. Other sources state that the takings for <i>Forever Amber</i> ($8 million) and <i>Life with Father</i> ($6.5 million)<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-354"><a href="#cite_note-354">[54]</a></sup> were in fact worldwide rental grosses, so it is possible this is also true of <i>Unconquered</i>.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Cinerama"><sup>CI</sup>The <a href="/wiki/Cinerama" title="Cinerama">Cinerama</a> figures represent gross amounts. Since the Cinerama corporation owned the theaters there were no rental fees for the films, meaning the studio received 100% of the box-office gross, unlike the case with most other films where the distributor typically receives less than half the gross. Since <i>Variety</i> at the time ranked films by their U.S. rental, they constructed a hypothetical rental figure for the Cinerama films to provide a basis for comparison to other films in their chart: in the case of <i>This Is Cinerama</i>, the $50 million worldwide gross was reconfigured as a $12.5 million U.S. rental gross; this is exactly 25% of the amount reported by Cinerama, so <i>Variety'</i>s formula seemingly halved the gross to obtain an estimate for the U.S. share, and halved it again to simulate a rental fee. <i>Variety</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span>s 'rental' amounts are often repeated, but have no basis in the reality of what the films actually earned—they are hypothetical figures conceived for comparative analysis.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-355"><a href="#cite_note-355">[55]</a></sup> All five Cinerama features collectively generated $120 million in worldwide box office receipts.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-356"><a href="#cite_note-356">[56]</a></sup></span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Greatest_Show"><sup>GS</sup><i>Variety</i> put the worldwide rental for <i>The Greatest Show on Earth</i> at around $18.35 million (with $12.8 million coming from the United States<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Finler358_347-1"><a href="#cite_note-Finler358-347">[47]</a></sup>) a year after its release; however, Birchard puts its earnings at just over $15 million up to 1962. It is likely that Birchard's figure is just the North American gross rental, and includes revenue from the 1954 and 1960 reissues.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Star_Wars"><sup>SW</sup>The "first run" <i>Star Wars</i> grosses do not include revenue from the 1997 special-edition releases; however, the figure does include revenue from the re-releases prior to the special editions.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_HP_budget"><sup>HP</sup>Production costs were shared with <i>Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows – Part 1</i>.</span>
</p>
</div>
<h2><span class="mw-headline" id="Timeline_of_highest-grossing_films">Timeline of highest-grossing films</span></h2>
<div class="thumb tright"><div class="thumbinner" style="width:172px;"><a class="image" href="/wiki/File:Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg"><img alt="The theatrical poster for The Birth of a Nation depicting a hooded man carrying a burning cross on horse back." class="thumbimage" data-file-height="1482" data-file-width="961" decoding="async" height="262" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg/170px-Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg/255px-Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg/340px-Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg 2x" width="170"/></a> <div class="thumbcaption"><div class="magnify"><a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:Birth_of_a_Nation_theatrical_poster.jpg" title="Enlarge"></a></div><i>The Birth of a Nation</i> pioneered many of the techniques used in filmmaking today, becoming the most successful film ever made at the time of its release.</div></div></div>
<p>At least eleven films have held the record of 'highest-grossing film' since <i>The Birth of a Nation</i> assumed the top spot in 1915. Both <i>The Birth of a Nation</i> and <i>Gone with the Wind</i> spent twenty-five consecutive years apiece as the highest-grosser, with films directed by Steven Spielberg holding the record on three occasions and James Cameron twice. Spielberg became the first director to break his own record when <i>Jurassic Park</i> overtook <i>E.T.</i>, and Cameron emulated the feat when <i>Avatar</i> broke the record set by <i>Titanic</i>. When it took over the top spot in 2019, <i>Avengers: Endgame</i> became the first sequel to hold the record of highest-grossing film, and in doing so ended thirty-six years of Spielberg/Cameron dominance.
</p><p>Some sources claim that <i>The Big Parade</i> superseded <i>The Birth of a Nation</i> as highest-grossing film, eventually being replaced by <i>Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs</i>, which in turn was quickly usurped by <i>Gone with the Wind</i>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Dirks_(2010)_357-0"><a href="#cite_note-Dirks_(2010)-357">[57]</a></sup> Exact figures are not known for <i>The Birth of a Nation</i>, but contemporary records put its worldwide earnings at $5.2 million as of 1919.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Wasko_358-0"><a href="#cite_note-Wasko-358">[58]</a></sup> Its international release was delayed by World War I, and it was not released in many foreign territories until the 1920s; coupled with further re-releases in the United States, its $10 million earnings as reported by <i><a href="/wiki/Variety_(magazine)" title="Variety (magazine)">Variety</a></i> in 1932 are consistent with the earlier figure.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Variety_(1932)_359-0"><a href="#cite_note-Variety_(1932)-359">[59]</a></sup> At this time, <i>Variety</i> still had <i>The Birth of a Nation</i> ahead of <i>The Big Parade</i> ($6,400,000) on distributor rentals and—if its estimate is correct—<i>Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs</i> ($8,500,000)<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-360"><a href="#cite_note-360">[60]</a></sup> would not have earned enough on its first theatrical run to take the record;<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-361"><a href="#cite_note-361">[61]</a></sup> although it would have been the highest-grossing 'talkie',<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-362"><a href="#cite_note-362">[62]</a></sup> displacing <i>The Singing Fool</i> ($5,900,000).<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-363"><a href="#cite_note-363">[63]</a></sup> Although received wisdom holds that it is unlikely <i>The Birth of a Nation</i> was ever overtaken by a silent-era film,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-364"><a href="#cite_note-364">[64]</a></sup> the record would fall to 1925's <i>Ben-Hur</i> ($9,386,000) if <i>The Birth of a Nation</i> earned significantly less than its estimated gross.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HallNeale163_365-0"><a href="#cite_note-HallNeale163-365">[65]</a></sup> In addition to its gross rental earnings through public exhibition, <i>The Birth of a Nation</i> played at a large number of private, club and organizational engagements which figures are unavailable for.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-du_Brow_366-0"><a href="#cite_note-du_Brow-366">[66]</a></sup> It was hugely popular with the <a href="/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan" title="Ku Klux Klan">Ku Klux Klan</a> who used it to drive recruitment,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-367"><a href="#cite_note-367">[67]</a></sup> and at one point <i>Variety</i> estimated its total earnings to stand at around $50 million.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-368"><a href="#cite_note-368">[68]</a></sup> Despite later retracting the claim, the sum has been widely reported even though it has never been substantiated.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Wasko_358-1"><a href="#cite_note-Wasko-358">[58]</a></sup> While it is generally accepted that <i>Gone with the Wind</i> took over the record of highest-grossing film on its initial release—which is true in terms of public exhibition—it is likely it did not overtake <i>The Birth of a Nation</i> in total revenue until a much later date, with it still being reported as the highest earner up until the 1960s.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-du_Brow_366-1"><a href="#cite_note-du_Brow-366">[66]</a></sup> <i>Gone with the Wind</i> itself may have been briefly overtaken by <i>The Ten Commandments</i> (1956), which closed at the end of 1960 with worldwide rentals of $58–60 million<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-369"><a href="#cite_note-369">[69]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HallNeale160_370-0"><a href="#cite_note-HallNeale160-370">[70]</a></sup> compared to <i>Gone with the Wind</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span>s $59 million;<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Oviatt_(1961)_371-0"><a href="#cite_note-Oviatt_(1961)-371">[71]</a></sup> if it did claim the top spot its tenure there was short-lived, since <i>Gone with the Wind</i> was re-released the following year and increased its earnings to $67 million. Depending on how accurate the estimates are, the 1959 remake of <i>Ben-Hur</i> may also have captured the record from <i>Gone with the Wind</i>: as of the end of 1961 it had earned $47 million worldwide,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-372"><a href="#cite_note-372">[72]</a></sup> and by 1963 it was trailing <i>Gone with the Wind</i> by just $2 million with international takings of $65 million,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Thomas_(1963)_373-0"><a href="#cite_note-Thomas_(1963)-373">[73]</a></sup> ultimately earning $66 million from its initial release.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-374"><a href="#cite_note-374">[74]</a></sup>
</p>
<div class="thumb tleft"><div class="thumbinner" style="width:172px;"><a class="image" href="/wiki/File:Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg"><img alt="" class="thumbimage" data-file-height="2388" data-file-width="1824" decoding="async" height="223" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg/170px-Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg/255px-Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg/340px-Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg 2x" width="170"/></a> <div class="thumbcaption"><div class="magnify"><a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:Deep_Throat_poster_2.jpg" title="Enlarge"></a></div>The 1972 pornographic film <i>Deep Throat</i> reportedly earned as much as $600 million, a figure that may have been inflated by gangsters in money laundering schemes.</div></div></div>
<p>Another film purported to have been the highest-grosser is the 1972 <a href="/wiki/Pornographic_film" title="Pornographic film">pornographic film</a> <i><a href="/wiki/Deep_Throat_(film)" title="Deep Throat (film)">Deep Throat</a></i>. In 1984, <a href="/wiki/Linda_Lovelace" title="Linda Lovelace">Linda Lovelace</a> testified to a United States Senate Judiciary Subcommittee on juvenile justice that the film had earned $600 million;<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-375"><a href="#cite_note-375">[75]</a></sup> this figure has been the subject of much speculation, since if it is accurate then the film would have made more money than <i>Star Wars</i>, and finished the 1970s as the highest-grossing film. The main argument against this figure is that it simply did not have a wide enough release to sustain the sort of sums that would be required for it to ultimately gross this amount.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-376"><a href="#cite_note-376">[76]</a></sup> Exact figures are not known, but testimony in a federal trial in 1976—about four years into the film's release—showed the film had grossed over $25 million.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-377"><a href="#cite_note-377">[77]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Roger_Ebert" title="Roger Ebert">Roger Ebert</a> has reasoned it possibly did earn as much as $600 million on paper, since mobsters owned most of the <a href="/wiki/Adult_movie_theater" title="Adult movie theater">adult movie theaters</a> during this period and would <a href="/wiki/Money_laundering" title="Money laundering">launder</a> income from drugs and prostitution through them, so probably inflated the box office receipts for the film.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-378"><a href="#cite_note-378">[78]</a></sup>
</p><p><i>The Birth of a Nation</i>, <i>Gone with the Wind</i>, <i>The Godfather</i>, <i>Jaws</i>, <i>Star Wars</i>, <i>E.T.</i> and <i>Avatar</i> all increased their record grosses with re-releases. The grosses from their original theatrical runs are included here along with totals from re-releases up to the point that they lost the record; therefore the total for <i>The Birth of a Nation</i> includes income from its reissues up to 1940; the total for <i>Star Wars</i> includes revenue from the late 1970s and early 1980s reissues but not from the 1997 Special Edition; the total for <i>E.T.</i> incorporates its gross from the 1985 reissue but not from 2002; the total for <i>Avatar</i> includes revenue from the 2010 Special Edition, which represents all of its earnings up to the point it relinquished the record. <i>Gone with the Wind</i> is represented twice on the chart: the 1940 entry includes earnings from its staggered 1939–1942 release (<a href="/wiki/Roadshow_theatrical_release" title="Roadshow theatrical release">roadshow</a>/<a href="/wiki/Wide_release" title="Wide release">general release</a>/<a href="/wiki/Discount_theater" title="Discount theater">second-run</a>)<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Bartel_379-0"><a href="#cite_note-Bartel-379">[79]</a></sup> along with all of its revenue up to the 1961 reissue prior to losing the record to <i>The Sound of Music</i> in 1966; its 1971 entry—after it took back the record—includes income from the 1967 and 1971 reissues but omitting later releases. <i>The Godfather</i> was re-released in 1973 after its success at the <a href="/wiki/45th_Academy_Awards" title="45th Academy Awards">45th Academy Awards</a>, and <i>Jaws</i> was released again in 1976, and their grosses here most likely include earnings from those releases. <i>The Sound of Music</i>, <i>The Godfather</i>, <i>Jaws</i>, <i>Jurassic Park</i> and <i>Titanic</i> increased their earnings with further releases in 1973, 1997, 1979, 2013 and 2012 respectively, but they are not included in the totals here because they had already conceded the record prior to being re-released.
</p>

<link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1011217839" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/><div class="refbegin" style="">
<p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Rentals2"><sup>R</sup>Distributor rental.</span>
</p><p><span class="citation wikicite" id="endnote_Reissue"><sup><img alt="Includes re-releases" data-file-height="14" data-file-width="9" decoding="async" height="14" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/f9/Double-dagger-14-plain.png" width="9"/></sup>Includes revenue from re-releases. If a film increased its gross through re-releases while holding the record, the year in which it recorded its highest gross is also noted in italics.</span>
</p>
</div>
<h2><span class="mw-headline" id="Highest-grossing_franchises_and_film_series">Highest-grossing franchises and film series</span></h2>
<div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">See also: <a href="/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_media_franchises" title="List of highest-grossing media franchises">List of highest-grossing media franchises</a></div>
<p>Prior to 2000, only seven <a href="/wiki/Film_series" title="Film series">film series</a> had grossed over $1 billion at the box office: <i><a href="/wiki/List_of_James_Bond_films" title="List of James Bond films">James Bond</a></i>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-396"><a href="#cite_note-396">[90]</a></sup> <i><a href="/wiki/Star_Wars" title="Star Wars">Star Wars</a></i>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-397"><a href="#cite_note-397">[91]</a></sup> <i><a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Indiana_Jones_(franchise)" title="Indiana Jones (franchise)">Indiana Jones</a></i>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-398"><a href="#cite_note-398">[92]</a></sup> <i><a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Rocky_(film_series)" title="Rocky (film series)">Rocky</a></i>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-399"><a href="#cite_note-399">[93]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-400"><a href="#cite_note-400">[94]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-401"><a href="#cite_note-401">[95]</a></sup> <i><a href="/wiki/Batman_in_film" title="Batman in film">Batman</a></i>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-402"><a href="#cite_note-402">[96]</a></sup> <i><a href="/wiki/Jurassic_Park" title="Jurassic Park">Jurassic Park</a></i>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-403"><a href="#cite_note-403">[97]</a></sup> and <i><a href="/wiki/List_of_Star_Trek_films_and_television_series" title="List of Star Trek films and television series">Star Trek</a></i>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-404"><a href="#cite_note-404">[98]</a></sup> Since the turn of the century that number has increased to over fifty (not including one-off hits such as <i>Avatar</i>, <i>Titanic</i>, and <i>Zootopia</i>).<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Numbers_franchises_405-0"><a href="#cite_note-Numbers_franchises-405">[99]</a></sup> This is partly due to inflation and market growth, but also to Hollywood's adoption of the <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Media_franchise" title="Media franchise">franchise</a> model: films that have built-in brand recognition, such as being based on a well-known literary source or an established character. The methodology is based on the concept that films associated with things audiences are already familiar with can be more effectively marketed to them, and as such are known as "pre-sold" films within the industry.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-economist_406-0"><a href="#cite_note-economist-406">[100]</a></sup>
</p><p>A franchise is typically defined to be at least two works derived from a common <a href="/wiki/Intellectual_property" title="Intellectual property">intellectual property</a>. Traditionally, the work has a tautological relationship with the property, but this is not a prerequisite. An enduring staple of the franchise model is the concept of the <i><a href="/wiki/Crossover_(fiction)" title="Crossover (fiction)">crossover</a></i>, which can be defined as "a story in which characters or concepts from two or more discrete texts or series of texts meet".<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-407"><a href="#cite_note-407">[101]</a></sup> A consequence of a crossover is that an intellectual property may be utilized by more than one franchise. For example, <i><a href="/wiki/Batman_v_Superman:_Dawn_of_Justice" title="Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice">Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice</a></i> belongs to not only the <a href="/wiki/Batman" title="Batman">Batman</a> and <a href="/wiki/Superman" title="Superman">Superman</a> franchises, but also to the <a href="/wiki/DC_Extended_Universe" title="DC Extended Universe">DC Extended Universe</a>, which is a <i><a href="/wiki/Shared_universe" title="Shared universe">shared universe</a></i>. A shared universe is a particular type of crossover where a number of characters from a wide range of fictional works wind up sharing a fictional world.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-408"><a href="#cite_note-408">[102]</a></sup> The most successful shared universe in the medium of film is the <a href="/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe" title="Marvel Cinematic Universe">Marvel Cinematic Universe</a>, a crossover between multiple superhero properties owned by <a href="/wiki/Marvel_Comics" title="Marvel Comics">Marvel Comics</a>. The Marvel Cinematic Universe is also the highest-grossing franchise, amassing over $22 billion at the box office.
</p><p>The <i>Star Wars</i> films are the highest-grossing series based on a single property, earning over $10 billion at the box office (although the <a href="/wiki/Eon_Productions" title="Eon Productions">Eon</a> <i>James Bond</i> films have earned over $18 billion in total when adjusted to current prices).<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-410"><a href="#cite_note-410">[104]</a></sup> If ancillary income from merchandise is included, then <i>Star Wars</i> is the most lucrative property;<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Harrod_411-0"><a href="#cite_note-Harrod-411">[105]</a></sup> it holds the <i>Guinness</i> world record for the "most successful film merchandising franchise" and was valued at £19.51 billion in 2012 (approximately $30 billion).<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-412"><a href="#cite_note-412">[106]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-413"><a href="#cite_note-413">[107]</a></sup> The Marvel Cinematic Universe has had the most films gross over $1 billion with nine. The four <i><a href="/wiki/Avengers_(comics)_in_other_media#Live_action" title="Avengers (comics) in other media">Avengers</a></i> films and the two <a href="/wiki/Frozen_(franchise)" title="Frozen (franchise)"><i>Frozen</i></a> films are the only franchises where each installment has grossed over $1 billion. Along with <a href="/wiki/The_Lion_King_(franchise)" title="The Lion King (franchise)"><i>The Lion King</i></a>, these are also the only franchises to have a series average of over $1 billion per film.
</p>
<dl><dd><link href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r981673959" rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style"/><div class="legend"><span class="legend-color" style="background-color:#b6fcb6; color:black;"><span class="legend-text"><img alt="film currently playing" data-file-height="14" data-file-width="9" decoding="async" height="14" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/37/Dagger-14-plain.png" width="9"/></span></span> <i>Background shading indicates that at least one film in the series is playing in the week commencing 5 March 2021 in theaters around the world</i>.</div></dd></dl>

</td></tr>
<tr>
<td colspan="6">
<table class="wikitable collapsible collapsed" style="border-collapse:collapse; margin:auto; width:100%;">
<tbody><tr>
<td style="background:#ffffaa; width:5%; text-align:center;">
</td>
<th style="text-align:left; background:#ffffaa; width:30%"><i><a href="/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe:_Phase_Three" title="Marvel Cinematic Universe: Phase Three">Phase Three</a></i>
</th>
<td style="background:#ffffaa; text-align:right; width:11%">$13,505,266,577
</td>

</td>
<td style="background:#ffffaa; text-align:right; width:11%">$1,227,751,507
</td>
<td style="background:#ffffaa; width:38%"><i>Avengers: Endgame</i> ($2,797,800,564)
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td style="background:#ffffaa">1
</td>
<td style="background:#ffffaa"><i><a href="/wiki/Avengers:_Endgame" title="Avengers: Endgame">Avengers: Endgame</a> </i>(2019)<i></i>
</td>
<td style="background:#ffffaa; text-align:right;">$2,797,800,564<br/>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td style="background:#ffffaa">2
</td>
