href="/wiki/Taika_Waititi" title="Taika Waititi">Taika Waititi</a> as <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Korg_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Korg (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Korg</a>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RadioTimesCameos_65-7"><a href="#cite_note-RadioTimesCameos-65">[60]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Angela_Bassett" title="Angela Bassett">Angela Bassett</a> as <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Ramonda_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Ramonda (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Ramonda</a>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-ScreenRantCast_45-13"><a href="#cite_note-ScreenRantCast-45">[40]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Michael_Douglas" title="Michael Douglas">Michael Douglas</a> as <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Hank_Pym_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Hank Pym (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Hank Pym</a>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RadioTimesCameos_65-8"><a href="#cite_note-RadioTimesCameos-65">[60]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Michelle_Pfeiffer" title="Michelle Pfeiffer">Michelle Pfeiffer</a> as <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Janet_van_Dyne_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Janet van Dyne (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Janet van Dyne</a>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RadioTimesCameos_65-9"><a href="#cite_note-RadioTimesCameos-65">[60]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Linda_Cardellini" title="Linda Cardellini">Linda Cardellini</a> as <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Laura_Barton_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Laura Barton (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Laura Barton</a>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RadioTimesCameos_65-10"><a href="#cite_note-RadioTimesCameos-65">[60]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Maximiliano_Hern%C3%A1ndez" title="Maximiliano Hernández">Maximiliano Hernández</a> as <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Jasper_Sitwell_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Jasper Sitwell (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Jasper Sitwell</a>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RadioTimesCameos_65-11"><a href="#cite_note-RadioTimesCameos-65">[60]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Frank_Grillo" title="Frank Grillo">Frank Grillo</a> as <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Brock_Rumlow_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Brock Rumlow (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Brock Rumlow</a>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Grillo_68-0"><a href="#cite_note-Grillo-68">[63]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Robert_Redford" title="Robert Redford">Robert Redford</a> as <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Alexander_Pierce_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Alexander Pierce (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Alexander Pierce</a>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RadioTimesCameos_65-12"><a href="#cite_note-RadioTimesCameos-65">[60]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Callan_Mulvey" title="Callan Mulvey">Callan Mulvey</a> as Jack Rollins,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RadioTimesCameos_65-13"><a href="#cite_note-RadioTimesCameos-65">[60]</a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/Ty_Simpkins" title="Ty Simpkins">Ty Simpkins</a> as <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Harley_Keener" title="Harley Keener">Harley Keener</a>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RadioTimesCameos_65-14"><a href="#cite_note-RadioTimesCameos-65">[60]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Sean_Gunn" title="Sean Gunn">Sean Gunn</a> reprised his role as <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Kraglin_Obfonteri" title="Kraglin Obfonteri">Kraglin Obfonteri</a> and was credited for the role,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-BFI_69-0"><a href="#cite_note-BFI-69">[64]</a></sup> though footage of his appearance is not clearly visible in the film.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-KraglinSeanGunn_70-0"><a href="#cite_note-KraglinSeanGunn-70">[65]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Natalie_Portman" title="Natalie Portman">Natalie Portman</a> appears as <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Jane_Foster_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Jane Foster (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Jane Foster</a> through the use of footage from a <i><a href="/wiki/Thor:_The_Dark_World" title="Thor: The Dark World">Thor: The Dark World</a></i> (2013) deleted scene, as well as new voice over that Portman recorded for when Foster appears talking in the distance.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Portman_71-0"><a href="#cite_note-Portman-71">[66]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/James_D%27Arcy" title="James D'Arcy">James D'Arcy</a> reprises his role as <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Edwin_Jarvis_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Edwin Jarvis (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Edwin Jarvis</a> from the MCU television series <i><a href="/wiki/Agent_Carter_(TV_series)" title="Agent Carter (TV series)">Agent Carter</a></i>, marking the first time a character introduced in an MCU television series appears in an MCU film.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-D'Arcy_72-0"><a href="#cite_note-D'Arcy-72">[67]</a></sup>
</p><p>Additionally, <a href="/wiki/Hiroyuki_Sanada" title="Hiroyuki Sanada">Hiroyuki Sanada</a> portrays <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Akihiko_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Akihiko (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Akihiko</a>, a <a href="/wiki/Yakuza" title="Yakuza">Yakuza</a> boss operating in Tokyo who opposes Barton.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-ScreenRantCast_45-14"><a href="#cite_note-ScreenRantCast-45">[40]</a></sup> Lexi Rabe portrays <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Morgan_Stark_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Morgan Stark (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Morgan Stark</a>, Tony and Pepper's daughter.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-BFI_69-1"><a href="#cite_note-BFI-69">[64]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-ScreenRantCast_45-15"><a href="#cite_note-ScreenRantCast-45">[40]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Katherine_Langford" title="Katherine Langford">Katherine Langford</a> was cast as an older Morgan, but her scene was cut from the final film.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Langford_73-0"><a href="#cite_note-Langford-73">[68]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-LangfordCut_74-0"><a href="#cite_note-LangfordCut-74">[69]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Emma_Fuhrmann" title="Emma Fuhrmann">Emma Fuhrmann</a> portrays an older <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Cassie_Lang_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Cassie Lang (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Cassie Lang</a>, Scott's daughter, after the character was played as a child by <a href="/wiki/Abby_Ryder_Fortson" title="Abby Ryder Fortson">Abby Ryder Fortson</a> in previous MCU films.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-EmmaFuhrmann_75-0"><a href="#cite_note-EmmaFuhrmann-75">[70]</a></sup> Avengers co-creator <a href="/wiki/Stan_Lee" title="Stan Lee">Stan Lee</a> has a posthumous cameo in the film, appearing <a href="/wiki/De-aging_in_film" title="De-aging in film">digitally de-aged</a> as a car driver in 1970; this is his final film appearance.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-LeeCameo_76-0"><a href="#cite_note-LeeCameo-76">[71]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Ken_Jeong" title="Ken Jeong">Ken Jeong</a> and <a href="/wiki/Yvette_Nicole_Brown" title="Yvette Nicole Brown">Yvette Nicole Brown</a> cameo as a storage facility guard and a S.H.I.E.L.D. employee, respectively.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RadioTimesCameos_65-15"><a href="#cite_note-RadioTimesCameos-65">[60]</a></sup> Co-director Joe Russo (credited as Gozie Agbo) has a cameo appearance as a grieving gay man, which is the first time an openly homosexual character has been portrayed in an MCU film.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-GDL_77-0"><a href="#cite_note-GDL-77">[72]</a></sup> Joe's daughters Ava and Lia portray Barton's daughter Lila and a fan of Hulk, respectively.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-EWCameos_78-0"><a href="#cite_note-EWCameos-78">[73]</a></sup> Thanos creator <a href="/wiki/Jim_Starlin" title="Jim Starlin">Jim Starlin</a> also appears as a grieving man.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-EWCameos_78-1"><a href="#cite_note-EWCameos-78">[73]</a></sup> The character <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Howard_the_Duck_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Howard the Duck (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Howard the Duck</a> appears in a non-speaking cameo.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HowardDuck_79-0"><a href="#cite_note-HowardDuck-79">[74]</a></sup>
</p>
<h2><span class="mw-headline" id="Production">Production</span></h2>
<div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">Further information: <a href="/wiki/Production_of_Avengers:_Infinity_War_and_Avengers:_Endgame" title="Production of Avengers: Infinity War and Avengers: Endgame">Production of Avengers: Infinity War and Avengers: Endgame</a></div>
<div class="thumb tright"><div class="thumbinner" style="width:222px;"><a class="image" href="/wiki/File:Anthony_and_Joe_Russo_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg"><img alt="" class="thumbimage" data-file-height="3048" data-file-width="4752" decoding="async" height="141" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Anthony_and_Joe_Russo_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/220px-Anthony_and_Joe_Russo_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Anthony_and_Joe_Russo_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/330px-Anthony_and_Joe_Russo_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Anthony_and_Joe_Russo_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/440px-Anthony_and_Joe_Russo_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg 2x" width="220"/></a> <div class="thumbcaption"><div class="magnify"><a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:Anthony_and_Joe_Russo_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg" title="Enlarge"></a></div><a href="/wiki/Russo_brothers" title="Russo brothers">Joe and Anthony Russo</a>, the directors</div></div></div>
<p>In October 2014, Marvel announced a two-part sequel to <i><a href="/wiki/Avengers:_Age_of_Ultron" title="Avengers: Age of Ultron">Avengers: Age of Ultron</a></i> (2015), titled <i>Avengers: Infinity War</i>. <i>Part 1</i> was scheduled to be released on May 4, 2018, with <i>Part 2</i> scheduled for May 3, 2019.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-InfinityWar_80-0"><a href="#cite_note-InfinityWar-80">[75]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Oct2014Event_81-0"><a href="#cite_note-Oct2014Event-81">[76]</a></sup> In April 2015, Marvel announced that <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Anthony_and_Joe_Russo" title="Anthony and Joe Russo">Anthony and Joe Russo</a> would direct both parts of <i>Avengers: Infinity War</i>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Russos2_82-0"><a href="#cite_note-Russos2-82">[77]</a></sup> with <a href="/wiki/Back-to-back_film_production" title="Back-to-back film production">back-to-back filming</a> expected to begin in 2016.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Russos_83-0"><a href="#cite_note-Russos-83">[78]</a></sup> That same month, <a href="/wiki/Kevin_Feige" title="Kevin Feige">Kevin Feige</a> said the films were titled as two parts of a single film because they had "such shared elements," but he would not actually describe them as "<a href="/wiki/List_of_films_split_into_multiple_parts" title="List of films split into multiple parts">one story that's cut in half</a>", and Feige said that both parts were "two distinct movies."<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-FeigeApril2015_84-0"><a href="#cite_note-FeigeApril2015-84">[79]</a></sup> By May 2015, <a href="/wiki/Christopher_Markus_and_Stephen_McFeely" title="Christopher Markus and Stephen McFeely">Christopher Markus and Stephen McFeely</a> signed on to write the screenplays for both parts of the film.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-MarkusMcFeely_85-0"><a href="#cite_note-MarkusMcFeely-85">[80]</a></sup> The following May, the Russos revealed that they would be retitling the two films, to further remove the misconception that the films were one large film split in two, with Joe stating, "The intention is we will change [the titles], we just haven't come up with [them] yet."<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Retitled_86-0"><a href="#cite_note-Retitled-86">[81]</a></sup> In July 2016, Marvel removed the film's title, simply referring to it as untitled <i>Avengers</i> film.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Retitled2_87-0"><a href="#cite_note-Retitled2-87">[82]</a></sup> Feige and the Russo brothers indicated the title was being withheld because it would give away plot details for this film and <i>Infinity War</i>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-FeigeTitle_88-0"><a href="#cite_note-FeigeTitle-88">[83]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RussosTitle_89-0"><a href="#cite_note-RussosTitle-89">[84]</a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Principal_photography" title="Principal photography">Principal photography</a> began on August 10, 2017,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-FilmingStart_90-0"><a href="#cite_note-FilmingStart-90">[85]</a></sup> under the <a href="/wiki/Working_title" title="Working title">working title</a> <i>Mary Lou 2</i>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-WorkingTitle_91-0"><a href="#cite_note-WorkingTitle-91">[86]</a></sup> at <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Pinewood_Atlanta_Studios" title="Pinewood Atlanta Studios">Pinewood Atlanta Studios</a> in <a href="/wiki/Fayette_County,_Georgia" title="Fayette County, Georgia">Fayette County, Georgia</a>, with <a href="/wiki/Trent_Opaloch" title="Trent Opaloch">Trent Opaloch</a> serving as <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Director_of_photography" title="Director of photography">director of photography</a>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Atlanta_92-0"><a href="#cite_note-Atlanta-92">[87]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Pinewood_93-0"><a href="#cite_note-Pinewood-93">[88]</a></sup> The film, along with <i>Infinity War</i>, were shot using <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/IMAX/Arri" title="IMAX/Arri">IMAX/Arri</a> 2D cameras, thus marking the first time that a Hollywood feature film was shot entirely with IMAX digital cameras.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-THRIMAX_94-0"><a href="#cite_note-THRIMAX-94">[89]</a></sup> That same month, filming occurred in <a href="/wiki/The_Gulch_(Atlanta)" title="The Gulch (Atlanta)">The Gulch</a> area of <a href="/wiki/Downtown_Atlanta" title="Downtown Atlanta">Downtown Atlanta</a>, near the <a href="/wiki/Five_Points_station" title="Five Points station">Five Points</a> <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/MARTA" title="MARTA">MARTA</a> station, and <a href="/wiki/Piedmont_Park" title="Piedmont Park">Piedmont Park</a>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-AtlantaAugFilming_95-0"><a href="#cite_note-AtlantaAugFilming-95">[90]</a></sup> Feige explained that the films were originally scheduled to be filmed simultaneously but were ultimately shot <a href="/wiki/Back-to-back_film_production" title="Back-to-back film production">back-to-back</a>, as "It became too complicated to cross-board them like that, and we found ourselves—again, something would always pay the price. We wanted to be able to focus and shoot one movie and then focus and shoot another movie."<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-ColliderApril2017_96-0"><a href="#cite_note-ColliderApril2017-96">[91]</a></sup> Anthony Russo originally felt it made more sense to shoot the films simultaneously due to financial and logistical reasons considering the large number of cast members,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RussosFilmingReason_97-0"><a href="#cite_note-RussosFilmingReason-97">[92]</a></sup> and had suggested that "some days we'll be shooting the first movie and some days we'll be shooting the second movie. Just jumping back and forth."<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Atlanta_92-1"><a href="#cite_note-Atlanta-92">[87]</a></sup> The 2013 <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Asgard_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe)" title="Asgard (Marvel Cinematic Universe)">Asgard</a> scenes were shot at <a href="/wiki/Durham_Cathedral" title="Durham Cathedral">Durham Cathedral</a> in <a href="/wiki/Durham,_England" title="Durham, England">Durham</a>, England during production of <i>Infinity War</i> in early May 2017.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Durham_98-0"><a href="#cite_note-Durham-98">[93]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Durham2_99-0"><a href="#cite_note-Durham2-99">[94]</a></sup> Production <a href="/wiki/Wrap_(filmmaking)" title="Wrap (filmmaking)">wrapped</a> on January 11, 2018,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-FilmingEnd_100-0"><a href="#cite_note-FilmingEnd-100">[95]</a></sup> although additional filming took place in <a href="/wiki/Dutchess_County,_New_York" title="Dutchess County, New York">Dutchess</a> and <a href="/wiki/Ulster_County,_New_York" title="Ulster County, New York">Ulster</a> counties in New York in June 2018.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DutchessUlsterFilming_101-0"><a href="#cite_note-DutchessUlsterFilming-101">[96]</a></sup> Reshoots began by September 7, 2018,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-ReshootsStart_102-0"><a href="#cite_note-ReshootsStart-102">[97]</a></sup> and concluded on October 12, 2018.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-ReshootsEnd_103-0"><a href="#cite_note-ReshootsEnd-103">[98]</a></sup> More reshoots occurred in January 2019.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Jan2019Reshoots_104-0"><a href="#cite_note-Jan2019Reshoots-104">[99]</a></sup> Location shooting also took place in <a href="/wiki/St_Abbs" title="St Abbs">St Abbs</a>, Scotland, which doubled for New Asgard in Norway.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-StAbbs_105-0"><a href="#cite_note-StAbbs-105">[100]</a></sup> Evans and Hemsworth each earned $15<span class="nowrap"> </span>million for the film.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-EvansHemsworthPay_106-0"><a href="#cite_note-EvansHemsworthPay-106">[101]</a></sup>
</p><p>The film's official title, <i>Avengers: Endgame</i>, and final U.S. release date of April 26, 2019, were revealed with the film's first trailer in December 2018.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-TitleReveal_107-0"><a href="#cite_note-TitleReveal-107">[102]</a></sup> Visual effects for the film were created by <a href="/wiki/Industrial_Light_%26_Magic" title="Industrial Light &amp; Magic">Industrial Light &amp; Magic</a>, <a href="/wiki/Weta_Digital" title="Weta Digital">Weta Digital</a>, <a href="/wiki/DNEG" title="DNEG">DNEG</a>, <a href="/wiki/Framestore" title="Framestore">Framestore</a>, <a href="/wiki/Cinesite" title="Cinesite">Cinesite</a>, <a href="/wiki/Digital_Domain" title="Digital Domain">Digital Domain</a>, Rise, <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Lola_VFX" title="Lola VFX">Lola VFX</a>, Cantina Creative, Capital T, Technicolor VFX, and <a href="/wiki/Territory_Studio" title="Territory Studio">Territory Studio</a>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-ArtofVFX_108-0"><a href="#cite_note-ArtofVFX-108">[103]</a></sup> As with previous MCU films, Lola worked on the <a href="/wiki/De-aging_in_film" title="De-aging in film">de-aging</a> sequences; the film features 200 de-aging and aging shots.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-THRDeAging_109-0"><a href="#cite_note-THRDeAging-109">[104]</a></sup> Downey, Evans, Ruffalo, Hemsworth, Johansson, and Renner were de-aged to their 2012 appearances for scenes recreated from <i><a href="/wiki/The_Avengers_(2012_film)" title="The Avengers (2012 film)">The Avengers</a></i> (2012).<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-ApocalypseDeAging_110-0"><a href="#cite_note-ApocalypseDeAging-110">[105]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Michael_Douglas" title="Michael Douglas">Michael Douglas</a>, <a href="/wiki/John_Slattery" title="John Slattery">John Slattery</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Stan_Lee" title="Stan Lee">Stan Lee</a> were also de-aged for the 1970 New Jersey sequence;<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-LeeCameo_76-1"><a href="#cite_note-LeeCameo-76">[71]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-VultureDeAging_111-0"><a href="#cite_note-VultureDeAging-111">[106]</a></sup> Douglas' appearance in <i><a href="/wiki/The_Streets_of_San_Francisco" title="The Streets of San Francisco">The Streets of San Francisco</a></i> was referenced.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DouglasTSoSF_112-0"><a href="#cite_note-DouglasTSoSF-112">[107]</a></sup> Lola also aged-up Evans for the final scene where he is portrayed as an elderly man, using some make-up and a stand-in as reference.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-ApocalypseDeAging_110-1"><a href="#cite_note-ApocalypseDeAging-110">[105]</a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Jeffrey_Ford_(film_editor)" title="Jeffrey Ford (film editor)">Jeffrey Ford</a> and <a href="/wiki/Matthew_Schmidt" title="Matthew Schmidt">Matthew Schmidt</a> served as the film's editors.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Editors_113-0"><a href="#cite_note-Editors-113">[108]</a></sup>
</p>
<h2><span class="mw-headline" id="Music">Music</span></h2>
<div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Avengers:_Endgame_(soundtrack)" title="Avengers: Endgame (soundtrack)">Avengers: Endgame (soundtrack)</a></div>
<div class="thumb tright"><div class="thumbinner" style="width:172px;"><a class="image" href="/wiki/File:Alan_Silvestri_2009.jpg"><img alt="" class="thumbimage" data-file-height="1180" data-file-width="919" decoding="async" height="218" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Alan_Silvestri_2009.jpg/170px-Alan_Silvestri_2009.jpg" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Alan_Silvestri_2009.jpg/255px-Alan_Silvestri_2009.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Alan_Silvestri_2009.jpg/340px-Alan_Silvestri_2009.jpg 2x" width="170"/></a> <div class="thumbcaption"><div class="magnify"><a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:Alan_Silvestri_2009.jpg" title="Enlarge"></a></div><a href="/wiki/Alan_Silvestri" title="Alan Silvestri">Alan Silvestri</a> composed the film's score.</div></div></div>
<p>In June 2016, <a href="/wiki/Alan_Silvestri" title="Alan Silvestri">Alan Silvestri</a>, who composed the score for <i><a href="/wiki/The_Avengers_(2012_film)" title="The Avengers (2012 film)">The Avengers</a></i>, was revealed to be returning to score both <i>Infinity War</i> and <i>Endgame</i>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Silvestri_114-0"><a href="#cite_note-Silvestri-114">[109]</a></sup> The Russos started working with Silvestri on the <i>Endgame</i> score in early November 2018,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RussoNov2018PostUpdate_115-0"><a href="#cite_note-RussoNov2018PostUpdate-115">[110]</a></sup> and it was completed in late March 2019.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-SilvestriDone_116-0"><a href="#cite_note-SilvestriDone-116">[111]</a></sup> A soundtrack album featuring Silvestri's score was released by <a href="/wiki/Hollywood_Records" title="Hollywood Records">Hollywood Records</a> digitally on April 26, 2019, with a physical release on May 24.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Soundtrack_117-0"><a href="#cite_note-Soundtrack-117">[112]</a></sup> A music video for the track "Portals", composed for the climactic scene in which reinforcements arrive for the Avengers, was released on June 13.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-PortalsMusicVideo_118-0"><a href="#cite_note-PortalsMusicVideo-118">[113]</a></sup>
</p><p>Silvestri described the score as having the most versatile tone of the franchise, ranging from "thunderous percussion and powerful brass" for the action sequences to minimalist, jazz-inspired music for Ant-Man and the quantum realm. Silvestri reprises his themes from the previous <i>Avengers</i> films and <i>Captain America: The First Avenger</i>, including material he wrote for Thanos and the Infinity Stones in <i>Infinity War</i>. He found writing the music to end Captain America's story poignant, since he had "been on this journey with him since the beginning".<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-SilvestriTHR_119-0"><a href="#cite_note-SilvestriTHR-119">[114]</a></sup> The film also uses the <i><a href="/wiki/Ant-Man_(film)" title="Ant-Man (film)">Ant-Man</a></i> (2015) theme by <a href="/wiki/Christophe_Beck" title="Christophe Beck">Christophe Beck</a>, the <i><a href="/wiki/Doctor_Strange_(2016_film)" title="Doctor Strange (2016 film)">Doctor Strange</a></i> (2016) theme by <a href="/wiki/Michael_Giacchino" title="Michael Giacchino">Michael Giacchino</a>, and the <i><a href="/wiki/Captain_Marvel_(film)" title="Captain Marvel (film)">Captain Marvel</a></i> theme by <a href="/wiki/Pinar_Toprak" title="Pinar Toprak">Pinar Toprak</a>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-MCUThemes_120-0"><a href="#cite_note-MCUThemes-120">[115]</a></sup> Additionally, the songs "<a href="/wiki/Come_and_Get_Your_Love" title="Come and Get Your Love">Come and Get Your Love</a>" by <a href="/wiki/Redbone_(band)" title="Redbone (band)">Redbone</a> and "<a href="/wiki/It%27s_Been_a_Long,_Long_Time" title="It's Been a Long, Long Time">It's Been a Long, Long Time</a>" by <a href="/wiki/Jule_Styne" title="Jule Styne">Jule Styne</a> and <a href="/wiki/Sammy_Cahn" title="Sammy Cahn">Sammy Cahn</a> are used, after previously being heard in <i>Guardians of the Galaxy</i> and <i><a href="/wiki/Captain_America:_The_Winter_Soldier" title="Captain America: The Winter Soldier">Captain America: The Winter Soldier</a></i> (2014), respectively.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Songs_121-0"><a href="#cite_note-Songs-121">[116]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-LongLongTime_122-0"><a href="#cite_note-LongLongTime-122">[117]</a></sup>
</p>
<h2><span class="mw-headline" id="Marketing">Marketing</span></h2>
<p>The marketing campaign for <i>Endgame</i> cost over $200 million, the most for any Marvel Studios film.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-PromoCosts_123-0"><a href="#cite_note-PromoCosts-123">[118]</a></sup> Promotional partners included <a href="/wiki/Stand_Up_to_Cancer" title="Stand Up to Cancer">Stand Up to Cancer</a>, <a href="/wiki/Mastercard" title="Mastercard">Mastercard</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ulta_Beauty" title="Ulta Beauty">Ulta Beauty</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Audi_e-tron_GT" title="Audi e-tron GT">Audi e-tron GT</a> concept car (which appears in the film), <a href="/wiki/McDonald%27s" title="McDonald's">McDonald's</a>, <a href="/wiki/GEICO" title="GEICO">GEICO</a>, <a href="/wiki/Coca-Cola" title="Coca-Cola">Coca-Cola</a>, <a href="/wiki/Google" title="Google">Google</a>, <a href="/wiki/General_Mills" title="General Mills">General Mills</a>, <a href="/wiki/The_Hertz_Corporation" title="The Hertz Corporation">Hertz</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ziploc" title="Ziploc">Ziploc</a>, <a href="/wiki/Oppo" title="Oppo">Oppo</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Synchrony_Financial" title="Synchrony Financial">Synchrony Financial</a>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-PromoCosts_123-1"><a href="#cite_note-PromoCosts-123">[118]</a></sup>
</p><p>A year prior to the film's release, Germain Lussier of <i><a href="/wiki/Io9" title="Io9">io9</a></i> spoke on the approach Marvel might have to take in marketing the film, given the end of <i>Infinity War</i> where many established characters die. He questioned if those characters would appear on posters and in toy campaigns and if the actors portraying them would participate in press events leading up to the film's release. Lussier felt Disney and Marvel could focus on the original Avengers team members, who make up the majority of the living characters, but noted it would be more beneficial to show the return of the dead characters, which would  "build a mystery and curiosity about <em>how</em> they come back. It could create a whole new level of interest about the film while having all the stars front and center, as they should be".<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-MarketingMay2018_124-0"><a href="#cite_note-MarketingMay2018-124">[119]</a></sup> Feige spoke to this in June 2018, stating that these dead characters would not be featured in any marketing for the film.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-FeigeMarketingJun2018_125-0"><a href="#cite_note-FeigeMarketingJun2018-125">[120]</a></sup> He presented a behind-the-scenes video from the film at <a href="/wiki/CineEurope" title="CineEurope">CineEurope</a>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-CineEurope_126-0"><a href="#cite_note-CineEurope-126">[121]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-CineEuropeClarify_127-0"><a href="#cite_note-CineEuropeClarify-127">[122]</a></sup> and said that the official marketing campaign for the film would begin at the end of 2018.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-FeigeTitleJun2018_128-0"><a href="#cite_note-FeigeTitleJun2018-128">[123]</a></sup> In early December 2018, ahead of the first trailer's release, Graeme McMillan of <i><a href="/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter" title="The Hollywood Reporter">The Hollywood Reporter</a></i> spoke to the "fevered anticipation" surrounding it and felt remarkable, which was mostly "fan-created, without noticeable direction from Marvel or the filmmakers involved" and that the amount of knowledge surrounding the film without any type of promotion was "a kind of brand awareness" that the most distributors want for. Because of this, McMillan urged Marvel not to release any trailers for the film since "the advanced level of enthusiasm that's already out there [...] is only likely to build as it gets closer to" the film's release. That said, he added that the eventual release of the trailer would take away the "<a href="/wiki/Schr%C3%B6dinger%27s_cat" title="Schrödinger's cat">Schrödinger's cat</a>-esque position" as it was "almost guaranteed" to cause fans a disappointment at this point.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-THRTrailerAnticipation_129-0"><a href="#cite_note-THRTrailerAnticipation-129">[124]</a></sup>
</p><p>The first trailer for the film was released on December 7, 2018.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-THRTrailer1Newby_130-0"><a href="#cite_note-THRTrailer1Newby-130">[125]</a></sup> Dustin Sandoval, vice president of digital marketing for Marvel Studios, stated the marketing team "made the choice" to prevent including the "title or hashtag" for the film of its trailer's posts, allowing fans to see the trailer without spoiling it by watching "at the end".<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-SandovalTrailer_131-0"><a href="#cite_note-SandovalTrailer-131">[126]</a></sup> Richard Newby, also of <i>The Hollywood Reporter</i> felt, while not much new was revealed in the trailer, it "offers a somber glimpse of a universe made unrecognizable" and lets the viewer "sit, rightfully so, with the ending of <i>Avengers: Infinity War</i> and our questions of loss". Newby also noted how the trailer "calls back to beginnings of the MCU [...] [as] the visual language used in the trailer does a great service by highlighting these characters' humble beginnings", and concluded it leaves viewers with "just as many questions as we had before".<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-THRTrailer1Newby_130-1"><a href="#cite_note-THRTrailer1Newby-130">[125]</a></sup> Austen Goslin of <i><a href="/wiki/Polygon_(website)" title="Polygon (website)">Polygon</a></i> pointed out that the title not only references a line Doctor Strange tells Tony Stark in <i>Infinity War</i>, but also a line spoken by Stark in <i><a href="/wiki/Avengers:_Age_of_Ultron" title="Avengers: Age of Ultron">Avengers: Age of Ultron</a></i>. Goslin said, "The scene surrounding this line in <i>Age of Ultron</i> is one of the most important ones in the movie. Things look dark, and the group of heroes face an enemy they don't think they can defeat." As such, the <i>Endgame</i> trailer "mirrors this perfectly" and "shows us that the Avengers' two most prominent characters are who they've always been: Iron Man, a pessimist who keeps fighting no matter how hopeless things look, and Captain America, an optimist who believes that nothing is hopeless when the world's heroes fight together".<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-PolygonTrailer1_132-0"><a href="#cite_note-PolygonTrailer1-132">[127]</a></sup> The trailer was viewed 289 million times in its first 24 hours, becoming <a href="/wiki/List_of_most-viewed_online_videos_in_the_first_24_hours#Top_trailers" title="List of most-viewed online videos in the first 24 hours">the most viewed trailer in that time period</a>, surpassing the record of <i>Avengers: Infinity War</i> (230 million views). The trailer also set a record for <a href="/wiki/Twitter" title="Twitter">Twitter</a> conversation for a film trailer in the first 24 hours, generating 549,000 mentions.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-FirstTrailerViews_133-0"><a href="#cite_note-FirstTrailerViews-133">[128]</a></sup> By January 3, 2019, <i><a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/BoxOffice" title="BoxOffice">BoxOffice</a></i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span>s "Trailer Impact" metric service indicated approximately 77–78% of people surveyed who viewed the <i>Endgame</i> trailer in the past three weeks had expressed interest in seeing the film. In the three weeks it was measured by the service, the trailer was number one for all and had the top two percent of respondents express interest in seeing the film since the service's introduction in March 2018.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-TrailerImpactData_134-0"><a href="#cite_note-TrailerImpactData-134">[129]</a></sup>
</p><p>The second trailer for the film, along with the theatrical release poster, was released on March 14, 2019. All 13 actors featured on the poster received top-billing except for Danai Gurira, whose name still appeared in the poster's bottom <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Billing_block" title="Billing block">billing block</a> along with Benedict Wong, Jon Favreau, and Gwyneth Paltrow (none of whom were featured on the poster). Despite this, her exclusion in the top billing prompted criticism from some fans.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-GuriraTopBillingPolygon_42-1"><a href="#cite_note-GuriraTopBillingPolygon-42">[37]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-GuriraTopBillingEW_135-0"><a href="#cite_note-GuriraTopBillingEW-135">[130]</a></sup> Petrana Radulovic of <i>Polygon</i> noted how an actor is credited on the poster "is a complex process" based on "dealing with agents, fees, and movie star demands."<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-GuriraTopBillingPolygon_42-2"><a href="#cite_note-GuriraTopBillingPolygon-42">[37]</a></sup> However, Marvel Studios released an updated poster later that day with Gurira in the top billing.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-GuriraTopBillingPolygon_42-3"><a href="#cite_note-GuriraTopBillingPolygon-42">[37]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-GuriraTopBillingEW_135-1"><a href="#cite_note-GuriraTopBillingEW-135">[130]</a></sup> The second trailer was viewed 268 million times in the first 24 hours, becoming the second most-viewed trailer in that time period behind the film's first trailer.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-SecondTrailerViews_136-0"><a href="#cite_note-SecondTrailerViews-136">[131]</a></sup>
</p>
<h2><span class="mw-headline" id="Release">Release</span></h2>
<h3><span class="mw-headline" id="Theatrical">Theatrical</span></h3>
<p><i>Avengers: Endgame</i> had its world premiere at the <a href="/wiki/Los_Angeles_Convention_Center" title="Los Angeles Convention Center">Los Angeles Convention Center</a> on April 22, 2019.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-LAPremiere_137-0"><a href="#cite_note-LAPremiere-137">[132]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-LAConventionCenter_138-0"><a href="#cite_note-LAConventionCenter-138">[133]</a></sup> Disney converted the convention center's Hall K for the film's premiere, working with <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Dolby" title="Dolby">Dolby</a> and <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/QSC_Audio" title="QSC Audio">QSC Audio</a> to install a 70 foot (21 metres) screen, <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Dolby_Vision" title="Dolby Vision">Dolby Vision</a> projectors, and a <a href="/wiki/Dolby_Atmos" title="Dolby Atmos">Dolby Atmos</a> sound system. The convention center also held the premiere's red carpet arrival and after-party.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-LAConventionCenter_138-1"><a href="#cite_note-LAConventionCenter-138">[133]</a></sup> The film was released in Australia, China, and other parts of Asia and Europe on April 24,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-ChinaRelease_139-0"><a href="#cite_note-ChinaRelease-139">[134]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-April24Release_140-0"><a href="#cite_note-April24Release-140">[135]</a></sup> in the United Kingdom on April 25,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-UKRelease_141-0"><a href="#cite_note-UKRelease-141">[136]</a></sup> in the United States and India on April 26,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-KlementieffAvengers4_62-1"><a href="#cite_note-KlementieffAvengers4-62">[57]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-TitleReveal_107-1"><a href="#cite_note-TitleReveal-107">[102]</a></sup> and in Russia on April 29<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineTracking2_142-0"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineTracking2-142">[137]</a></sup> in <a href="/wiki/IMAX" title="IMAX">IMAX</a> and <a href="/wiki/3D_film" title="3D film">3D</a>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-IMAXRelease_143-0"><a href="#cite_note-IMAXRelease-143">[138]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-3DRelease_144-0"><a href="#cite_note-3DRelease-144">[139]</a></sup> It was originally scheduled to be released in the United States on May 3.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-InfinityWar_80-1"><a href="#cite_note-InfinityWar-80">[75]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Oct2014Event_81-1"><a href="#cite_note-Oct2014Event-81">[76]</a></sup> <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Radio_Liberty" title="Radio Liberty">Radio Liberty</a> alleged that the <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Russian_government" title="Russian government">Russian government</a> postponed the film's release in that country to promote Russian-produced films.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RussianDelay_145-0"><a href="#cite_note-RussianDelay-145">[140]</a></sup> <i>Avengers: Endgame</i> is part of <a href="/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe:_Phase_Three" title="Marvel Cinematic Universe: Phase Three">Phase Three</a> of the MCU.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-PhaseThree_146-0"><a href="#cite_note-PhaseThree-146">[141]</a></sup>
</p><p>Following the release of the second trailer for Marvel Studios' <i><a href="/wiki/Spider-Man:_Far_From_Home" title="Spider-Man: Far From Home">Spider-Man: Far From Home</a></i> (2019) on May 6, Marvel began showing it at the end of <i>Endgame</i> screenings with a message before the film from <i>Far From Home</i> star Tom Holland telling the audience to stay until the end of the credits to see the trailer.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-FarFromHomeTrailer_147-0"><a href="#cite_note-FarFromHomeTrailer-147">[142]</a></sup> In June, Feige announced that <i>Avengers: Endgame</i> would be re-released in theaters with seven minutes of new post-credits footage, including a Stan Lee tribute, an unfinished deleted scene, and the opening scene of <i>Far From Home</i>. A limited edition poster would also be given out at select theaters. The re-release began on June 28 in the United States, across 1,040 theaters.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Rerelease2_148-0"><a href="#cite_note-Rerelease2-148">[143]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Rerelease_149-0"><a href="#cite_note-Rerelease-149">[144]</a></sup>
</p>
<h3><span class="mw-headline" id="Home_media">Home media</span></h3>
<p><i>Avengers: Endgame</i> was released in the US on <a href="/wiki/Digital_distribution" title="Digital distribution">digital download</a> by <a href="/wiki/Walt_Disney_Studios_Home_Entertainment" title="Walt Disney Studios Home Entertainment">Walt Disney Studios Home Entertainment</a> on July 30, and on <a href="/wiki/Ultra_HD_Blu-ray" title="Ultra HD Blu-ray">Ultra HD Blu-ray</a>, <a href="/wiki/Blu-ray" title="Blu-ray">Blu-ray</a>, and <a href="/wiki/DVD-Video" title="DVD-Video">DVD</a> on August 13.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HomeMedia_150-0"><a href="#cite_note-HomeMedia-150">[145]</a></sup> The film was released on both Digital HD and Blu-ray on September 2 in the UK.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-UKHomeRelease_151-0"><a href="#cite_note-UKHomeRelease-151">[146]</a></sup> Streaming is exclusive to <a href="/wiki/Disney%2B" title="Disney+">Disney+</a> since November 12.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DisneyStreaming_152-0"><a href="#cite_note-DisneyStreaming-152">[147]</a></sup> The digital and Blu-ray releases include behind-the-scenes featurettes, audio commentary, deleted scenes, and a blooper reel.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HomeMedia_150-1"><a href="#cite_note-HomeMedia-150">[145]</a></sup> Despite being filmed with IMAX cameras and released in IMAX theaters in the 1.90:1 <a href="/wiki/Aspect_ratio" title="Aspect ratio">aspect ratio</a>, the home media release only includes the cropped <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/2.39:1" title="2.39:1">2.39:1</a> aspect ratio version that was used for non-IMAX screenings.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HomeNotImax_153-0"><a href="#cite_note-HomeNotImax-153">[148]</a></sup>
</p>
<h2><span class="mw-headline" id="Reception">Reception</span></h2>
<h3><span class="mw-headline" id="Box_office">Box office</span></h3>
<div class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable" role="note">Main article: <a href="/wiki/List_of_box_office_records_set_by_Avengers:_Endgame" title="List of box office records set by Avengers: Endgame">List of box office records set by Avengers: Endgame</a></div>
<p><i>Avengers: Endgame</i> grossed <span style="white-space: nowrap">$858.3<span class="nowrap"> </span>million</span> in the United States and Canada, and <span style="white-space: nowrap">$1.939<span class="nowrap"> </span>billion</span> in other territories, for a worldwide total of <span style="white-space: nowrap">$2.798<span class="nowrap"> </span>billion</span>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-TheNumbers_3-1"><a href="#cite_note-TheNumbers-3">[3]</a></sup> becoming the <a href="/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films#Highest-grossing_films" title="List of highest-grossing films">highest-grossing film of all time</a>,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HighestGrossingFilm1_154-0"><a href="#cite_note-HighestGrossingFilm1-154">[149]</a></sup> as well as the <a href="/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films_in_the_United_States_and_Canada#Not_adjusted_for_inflation" title="List of highest-grossing films in the United States and Canada">second-highest-grossing film of all time in the United States and Canada</a>. Adjusted for inflation, <i><a href="/wiki/Gone_with_the_Wind_(film)" title="Gone with the Wind (film)">Gone with the Wind</a></i> (1939) remains the highest-grossing film, and <i>Avengers: Endgame</i> is the <a href="/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films#Highest-grossing_films_adjusted_for_inflation" title="List of highest-grossing films">fifth-highest-grossing film of all time worldwide</a>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-HighestGrossingFilm2_155-0"><a href="#cite_note-HighestGrossingFilm2-155">[150]</a></sup>
</p><p>The film had a worldwide opening of $1.2<span class="nowrap"> </span>billion, the <a href="/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_openings_for_films#Biggest_worldwide_openings_since_2002" title="List of highest-grossing openings for films">biggest of all time</a> and nearly double <i>Infinity War</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span>s previous record of $640<span class="nowrap"> </span>million.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineINT_156-0"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineINT-156">[151]</a></sup> It was also the fastest film ever to eclipse the $1<span class="nowrap"> </span>billion and $1.5<span class="nowrap"> </span>billion mark, doing so in just five days and eight days, respectively.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-MoreRecordsBroken_157-0"><a href="#cite_note-MoreRecordsBroken-157">[152]</a></sup> <i><a href="/wiki/Deadline_Hollywood" title="Deadline Hollywood">Deadline Hollywood</a></i> estimated the film would <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Break_even" title="Break even">break even</a> just five days after its release, which is "unheard of for a major studio tentpole during its opening weekend".<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineProfitAnalysis_2-1"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineProfitAnalysis-2">[2]</a></sup> The website eventually calculated the final net profit of the film to be $890<span class="nowrap"> </span>million, accounting for production budgets, P&amp;A, talent participations, and other costs, with box office grosses, and ancillary revenues from home media, placing it first on their list of 2019's "Most Valuable Blockbusters".<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineProfitAnalysisFinal_158-0"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineProfitAnalysisFinal-158">[153]</a></sup>
</p><p>On May 4, the film's earnings at the global box office passed the entire theatrical run of <i>Infinity War</i> and became the fastest film ever to gross $2<span class="nowrap"> </span>billion worldwide, amassing the amount in only 11 days (beating <i><a href="/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film)" title="Avatar (2009 film)">Avatar</a></i> (2009) that did so in 47 days).<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-BillionRecord_159-0"><a href="#cite_note-BillionRecord-159">[154]</a></sup> It also became the fifth film to surpass this threshold (after <i>Avatar</i>, <i><a href="/wiki/Titanic_(1997_film)" title="Titanic (1997 film)">Titanic</a></i> (1997), <i><a href="/wiki/Star_Wars:_The_Force_Awakens" title="Star Wars: The Force Awakens">Star Wars: The Force Awakens</a></i> (2015), and <i>Infinity War</i>),<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-PassThreshold_160-0"><a href="#cite_note-PassThreshold-160">[155]</a></sup> and the second film to surpass the threshold of $2.5<span class="nowrap"> </span>billion, doing so in just 20 days, outpacing <i>Avatar</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span>s record of 72 days.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-2.5Billion_161-0"><a href="#cite_note-2.5Billion-161">[156]</a></sup>
</p>
<h4><span class="mw-headline" id="Pre-sale_records">Pre-sale records</span></h4>
<p>In late December 2018, <i>Endgame</i> was listed as the most anticipated films of 2019, which ranked second by <a href="/wiki/IMDb" title="IMDb">IMDb</a> behind <i><a href="/wiki/Captain_Marvel_(film)" title="Captain Marvel (film)">Captain Marvel</a></i>, ranked first by the ticketing service <a href="/wiki/Fandango_Media" title="Fandango Media">Fandango</a>, and ranked first in overall by <a href="/wiki/Atom_Tickets" title="Atom Tickets">Atom Tickets</a>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-IMDbFandangoAnticipation_162-0"><a href="#cite_note-IMDbFandangoAnticipation-162">[157]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-AtomTicketsAnticipation_163-0"><a href="#cite_note-AtomTicketsAnticipation-163">[158]</a></sup>
</p><p>Due to the high demand when pre-sale tickets became available in the U.S. on April 2, 2019, customers on both Atom Tickets and Fandango experienced long wait times and system delays, while <a href="/wiki/AMC_Theatres" title="AMC Theatres">AMC Theatres</a>' website and app crashed completely for several hours. The same day, Fandango announced the film became its top-selling pre-sale title for the first 24 hours, topping <i>Star Wars: The Force Awakens</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span> previous record in just six hours. Atom said the film was also the website's best-selling first-day film (outselling <i><a href="/wiki/Aquaman_(film)" title="Aquaman (film)">Aquaman</a></i> (2018) by four times), and <a href="/wiki/Regal_Cinemas" title="Regal Cinemas">Regal Cinemas</a> reported that <i>Endgame</i> had sold more tickets in its first eight hours than <i>Infinity War</i> did in its entire first week.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-PreSale_164-0"><a href="#cite_note-PreSale-164">[159]</a></sup> The film grossed $120–140<span class="nowrap"> </span>million in pre-sales alone.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineTracking2_142-1"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineTracking2-142">[137]</a></sup> The day prior to the film's release, Fandango announced it was its biggest pre-selling title of all time, beating <i>The Force Awakens</i>, with over 8,000 sold-out showtimes across the country.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-FandangoSellout_165-0"><a href="#cite_note-FandangoSellout-165">[160]</a></sup>
</p><p>In India, the film sold one million tickets in just one day for the English, Hindi, Tamil, and Telugu language screenings; 18 tickets were sold every second.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineTracking2_142-2"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineTracking2-142">[137]</a></sup> In China, pre-sale tickets became available on April 12 and sold a record one million tickets in just six hours, outselling <i>Infinity War</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span>s first 24-hour total within the first hour,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-ChinaTickets_166-0"><a href="#cite_note-ChinaTickets-166">[161]</a></sup> and made $114.5<span class="nowrap"> </span>million (<a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/RMB" title="RMB">RMB</a> 770 million) just from pre-sales.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineChina_167-0"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineChina-167">[162]</a></sup>
</p>
<h4><span class="mw-headline" id="United_States_and_Canada">United States and Canada</span></h4>
<div class="thumb tright"><div class="thumbinner" style="width:222px;"><a class="image" href="/wiki/File:North_American_Domestic_Box_Office.png"><img alt="" class="thumbimage" data-file-height="835" data-file-width="1404" decoding="async" height="131" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/North_American_Domestic_Box_Office.png/220px-North_American_Domestic_Box_Office.png" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/North_American_Domestic_Box_Office.png/330px-North_American_Domestic_Box_Office.png 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/North_American_Domestic_Box_Office.png/440px-North_American_Domestic_Box_Office.png 2x" width="220"/></a> <div class="thumbcaption"><div class="magnify"><a class="internal" href="/wiki/File:North_American_Domestic_Box_Office.png" title="Enlarge"></a></div>Chart of the North American box office gross of <i>Avengers: Endgame</i> against the four <a href="/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films_in_the_United_States_and_Canada" title="List of highest-grossing films in the United States and Canada">highest-grossing films in the market</a></div></div></div>
<p>On April 4, industry tracking projected the film would gross $200–250<span class="nowrap"> </span>million domestically during its opening weekend, although some insiders saw those figures as conservative and expected a $260–300<span class="nowrap"> </span>million debut.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-VarietyTracking_168-0"><a href="#cite_note-VarietyTracking-168">[163]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineTracking_169-0"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineTracking-169">[164]</a></sup> By the week of its release, domestic estimates had risen to $260–270<span class="nowrap"> </span>million, with some insiders still suggesting a $300<span class="nowrap"> </span>million debut was possible. The film played in 4,662 theaters, 410 of which were in IMAX; it was the widest release ever, surpassing the record of <i><a href="/wiki/Despicable_Me_3" title="Despicable Me 3">Despicable Me 3</a></i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span>s (2017) 4,529 theaters.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineTracking2_142-3"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineTracking2-142">[137]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-VarietyRecordsBroken_170-0"><a href="#cite_note-VarietyRecordsBroken-170">[165]</a></sup> <i>Avengers: Endgame</i> <a href="/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_openings_for_films" title="List of highest-grossing openings for films">earned $357.1<span class="nowrap"> </span>million in its opening weekend</a>, breaking <i>Infinity War</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span>s record by nearly $100<span class="nowrap"> </span>million. It also set records for Friday ($157.5<span class="nowrap"> </span>million, including $60<span class="nowrap"> </span>million from Thursday night previews), Saturday ($109.3<span class="nowrap"> </span>million), and Sunday ($90.4<span class="nowrap"> </span>million) totals, as well as was more of a total gross alone than the previous box office high of all films combined ($314<span class="nowrap"> </span>million).<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DEADopening_171-0"><a href="#cite_note-DEADopening-171">[166]</a></sup> The film then made $36.9<span class="nowrap"> </span>million on Monday and $33.1<span class="nowrap"> </span>million on Tuesday, both the third-highest of all time.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-MondayGross_172-0"><a href="#cite_note-MondayGross-172">[167]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-TuesdayGross_173-0"><a href="#cite_note-TuesdayGross-173">[168]</a></sup> In its second weekend the film made $147.4<span class="nowrap"> </span>million (the second-best sophomore frame ever) for a 10-day total of $621.3<span class="nowrap"> </span>million. It was the fastest film to ever pass the $600<span class="nowrap"> </span>million milestone, beating <i>The Force Awakens</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span> 12 days and less than half the 26 days it took <i>Infinity War</i>.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DEADwknd2_174-0"><a href="#cite_note-DEADwknd2-174">[169]</a></sup> The following week, the film grossed $64.8<span class="nowrap"> </span>million, the fourth-best third weekend ever. It also passed the $700<span class="nowrap"> </span>million mark, tying <i>The Force Awakens</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span> record of 16 days.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DEADwknd3_175-0"><a href="#cite_note-DEADwknd3-175">[170]</a></sup> <i>Endgame</i> was finally dethroned in its fourth weekend by newcomer <i><a href="/wiki/John_Wick:_Chapter_3_%E2%80%93_Parabellum" title="John Wick: Chapter 3 – Parabellum">John Wick: Chapter 3 – Parabellum</a></i> (2019).<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Weekend4_176-0"><a href="#cite_note-Weekend4-176">[171]</a></sup> It made $17.2<span class="nowrap"> </span>million the following weekend (and a total of $22.3<span class="nowrap"> </span>million over the four-day Memorial Day frame), crossing the $800<span class="nowrap"> </span>million threshold domestically.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Weekend5_177-0"><a href="#cite_note-Weekend5-177">[172]</a></sup> During the re-release, which occurred over its tenth weekend, the film was added to 1,040 theaters and made $6.1<span class="nowrap"> </span>million, an increase of 207% from the previous weekend.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineRerelease_178-0"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineRerelease-178">[173]</a></sup> In its thirteenth weekend the film made $1.2<span class="nowrap"> </span>million, which put it over the threshold to top <i>Avatar</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span>s all-time record.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RecordWknd_179-0"><a href="#cite_note-RecordWknd-179">[174]</a></sup>
</p>
<h4><span class="mw-headline" id="Other_territories">Other territories</span></h4>
<p>Internationally, <i>Endgame</i> was projected to gross around $680<span class="nowrap"> </span>million over its first five days for a global debut of $850–950<span class="nowrap"> </span>million.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-THRtracking_180-0"><a href="#cite_note-THRtracking-180">[175]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Varietytracking_181-0"><a href="#cite_note-Varietytracking-181">[176]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineTracking2_142-4"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineTracking2-142">[137]</a></sup> The film was initially projected to gross $250–280<span class="nowrap"> </span>million in China in its opening weekend,<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineTracking2_142-5"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineTracking2-142">[137]</a></sup> but made a record $107.5<span class="nowrap"> </span>million (RMB 719 million) in the country on its first day, including $28.2<span class="nowrap"> </span>million (RMB 189<span class="nowrap"> </span>million) from midnight, 3 AM and 6<span class="nowrap"> </span>am screenings, beating <i><a href="/wiki/The_Fate_of_the_Furious" title="The Fate of the Furious">The Fate of the Furious</a></i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">'</span>s (2017) previous record of $9.1<span class="nowrap"> </span>million. Due to the record-breaking first day, partnered with word of mouth (with a 9.1 on local review aggregator <a href="/wiki/Douban" title="Douban">Douban</a> and a 9.3 on ticket website <a class="mw-redirect" href="/wiki/Maoyan" title="Maoyan">Maoyan</a>), debut projections were increased to over $300<span class="nowrap"> </span>million.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineChina_167-1"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineChina-167">[162]</a></sup> Overall, the film made $169<span class="nowrap"> </span>million on the first day from international countries, the highest total of all time. Its largest markets after China were India ($9<span class="nowrap"> </span>million), South Korea ($8.4<span class="nowrap"> </span>million; the largest non-holiday single day gross ever), Australia ($7.1<span class="nowrap"> </span>million), France ($6<span class="nowrap"> </span>million), and Italy ($5.8<span class="nowrap"> </span>million).<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineINT_156-1"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineINT-156">[151]</a></sup> Like domestically, the film ended up over-performing and debuted to $866<span class="nowrap"> </span>million overseas. Its largest markets, every one of which set the record for best-ever opening in the country, were China ($330.5<span class="nowrap"> </span>million; RMB 2.22<span class="nowrap"> </span>billion), the United Kingdom ($53.8<span class="nowrap"> </span>million), South Korea ($47.4<span class="nowrap"> </span>million), Mexico ($33.1<span class="nowrap"> </span>million), Australia ($30.8<span class="nowrap"> </span>million), Brazil ($26<span class="nowrap"> </span>million), Spain ($13.3<span class="nowrap"> </span>million), Japan ($13<span class="nowrap"> </span>million),<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-DeadlineINT_156-2"><a href="#cite_note-DeadlineINT-156">[151]</a></sup> and Vietnam ($10<span class="nowrap"> </span>million).<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-EIUEG_182-0"><a href="#cite_note-EIUEG-182">[177]</a></sup> It also made $21.6<span class="nowrap"> </span>million over its first four days in Russia after a delay of its premiere that was caused by the Russian government.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RussianDelay_145-1"><a href="#cite_note-RussianDelay-145">[140]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RussianDelay2_183-0"><a href="#cite_note-RussianDelay2-183">[178]</a></sup><sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-RussianDelay3_184-0"><a href="#cite_note-RussianDelay3-184">[179]</a></sup>
</p><p>In its first week, the film's top five largest international markets were China (<span style="white-space: nowrap">$459.4<span class="nowrap"> </span>million</span>), the United Kingdom (<span style="white-space: nowrap">$68.2<span class="nowrap"> </span>million</span>), South Korea (<span style="white-space: nowrap">$60.3<span class="nowrap"> </span>million</span>), Mexico (<span style="white-space: nowrap">$48.6<span class="nowrap"> </span>million</span>), and India (<span style="white-space: nowrap">$40.9<span class="nowrap"> </span>million</span>).<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-FiveInternationalMarkets_185-0"><a href="#cite_note-FiveInternationalMarkets-185">[180]</a></sup> A week after its release, it became the highest-grossing foreign film of all time in China<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-ChinaForeign_186-0"><a href="#cite_note-ChinaForeign-186">[181]</a></sup> and India.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-IndiaForeign_187-0"><a href="#cite_note-IndiaForeign-187">[182]</a></sup> In its second weekend the film's running total passed $1.569<span class="nowrap"> </span>billion from international markets, passing <i>Titanic</i> as the second-highest film overseas of all time.<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-PassThreshold_160-1"><a href="#cite_note-PassThreshold-160">[155]</a></sup>
</p><p>As of January 2021<sup class="plainlinks noexcerpt noprint asof-tag update" style="display:none;"><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Avengers:_Endgame&amp;action=edit">[update]</a></sup>, the film's top international markets were China ($629<span class="nowrap"> </span>million), United Kingdom ($115<span class="nowrap"> </span>million), South Korea ($105<span class="nowrap"> </span>million), Brazil ($86<span class="nowrap"> </span>million), and Mexico ($78<span class="nowrap"> </span>million).<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-TheNumbers_3-2"><a href="#cite_note-TheNumbers-3">[3]</a></sup>
</p>
<h3><span class="mw-headline" id="Critical_response">Critical response</span></h3>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r978413945/mw-parser-output/.tmulti">.mw-parser-output .tmulti .thumbinner{display:flex;flex-direction:column}.mw-parser-output .tmulti
